The introduction of non-native species has been one of the most important causes of biodiversity loss in recent centuries, but we know little about how these introductions affect native species.
We found that the most common lineage of Bd is not present in all areas of the United States, but rather appears to have been introduced from other parts of the country.
We developed a new genotyping tool to identify and characterize genetic variation in a large collection of isolates from around the world.
We also show that there is a need for increased surveillance of chytridiomycosis in areas where multiple Bd lineages have been detected.
Homologous recombination is an essential biological process for genome maintenance and repair.
We have previously shown that RuvAB is able to convert stalled replication forks into Holliday junctions ( HJs) in vitro, but it has not been demonstrated whether this activity occurs in vivo.

We show that the RuvAB complex is required for efficient recombination, and that the RuvA protein has two distinct functions: it binds to single-stranded DNA and stimulates the ATP-dependent unwinding of double-stranded DNA by RuvB.
The Antarctic Ice Sheet is one of the most important components of Earth's climate system, with its contribution to global sea level rise being the largest source of uncertainty for the next century.
We show that this process is likely to be irreversible and could lead to an additional contribution of 3 m or more to global sea level over the next few centuries to millennia.
Vaults are large ribonucleoprotein particles that have been implicated in the transport of nucleic acids across cellular membranes, but their exact function is unknown.
We have determined the structure of a single particle of the viral capsid protein, VP1, which forms a half-vault with a molecular weight of approximately 400 kDa.

We used cryo-electron microscopy and single-particle analysis to determine the structure of the major vault protein ( MVP) in its native state, which is an important component of the nuclear pore complex.

We have used microarray analysis of gene expression during infection to identify genes that are induced when C. albicans infects mice, and we show that these genes are also induced when the fungus grows in vitro under conditions that mimic those encountered during infection.
We identified 9 transcription factors that are important for growth and survival of C. albicans in the host.
We found that one of these genes, rlmA, is essential for the fungus to cause disease in mice and that it is also important for virulence in humans.
Odorant receptors ( ORs) are G protein-coupled receptors that mediate the detection of volatile molecules in air.
However, most olfactory receptors are retained within the endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) and do not reach the plasma membrane.
We have used a combination of experimental and computational approaches to identify the molecular determinants underlying this phenomenon.
We have developed a method for generating synthetic olfactory receptors with improved stability and expression, which we use to test the role of conserved amino acids in the receptor structure.
Human herpesviruses ( HHV) have been implicated as potential triggers for MS, but their role is still unclear.
The role of HCMV infection in the development of AD is unknown.
We show here that infection of rhesus macaques with HHV-6A or HHV-6B accelerates the onset of EAE.
We found that HHV-6 infection of mice and monkeys resulted in increased severity of experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis ( EAE), an animal model for multiple sclerosis.

We found that HHV-6B infection is associated with increased severity of EAE in marmosets, and that HHV-6B-infected cells are present within inflammatory lesions in the central nervous system ( CNS) of these animals.
However, there is little evidence on how these interventions work together to reduce dengue transmission.

We found that, compared to current control measures, Wolbachia could be more effective at reducing dengue transmission than current vector control methods ( such as insecticide spraying) but would not be cost-effective if implemented alone.
We show that even small improvements in vector control can have large effects on disease burden and economic impact.
We found that loss of SPDEF in mouse prostate cancer cells increases the expression of Foxm1, which is known to promote cell proliferation and survival.
We also show that SPDEF is downregulated in prostate cancer cells and that this reduction correlates with increased expression of Foxm1.
The brain's ability to sense food is essential for survival, yet we know little about how this information is processed in the brain.
We show here that activation of these neurons promotes food intake by engaging an extensive neural circuit involving the endogenous opioid system.
Our results provide new insights into how the brain regulates food intake and may lead to novel approaches for treating obesity.
Critical limb ischaemia ( CLI) is a severe condition where there is insufficient blood supply to the leg or foot.

The main aim of this review was to find out if there is evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCTs) to support the use of opioids in people with cancer who have moderate to severe pain and cannot be treated by other methods.
The review authors found no studies that compared lumbar sympathectomy with other treatments for severe leg pain caused by peripheral arterial disease.

Study characteristics We found one randomised controlled trial (RCT) that compared lumbar sympathectomy versus prostacyclin for the treatment of critical limb ischaemia in patients with peripheral arterial disease who were not suitable for revascularisation.

The review authors found low quality evidence from one small trial in people with Buerger's disease showing that prostaglandin treatment was better at treating ulcers and preventing limb loss than surgery.
More research is needed to find out if there are any benefits or harms from surgery in people with other types of peripheral arterial disease.
Rho GEFs are known to be overexpressed and activated in many cancers, but their role in cancer progression has not been fully elucidated.
We have used genetically modified mice to demonstrate that Vav2 and Vav3 are required for the initiation phase of skin carcinogenesis in vivo.
We show that this is due to the loss of Vav1 and Vav2, which are expressed in the basal layer of the epidermis but not in the suprabasal layers.
We show that Netrin signaling in the ventral midline acts on the axon of the anterior commissure neuron by activating the receptor UNC-5, which is expressed on the axon itself.
We show that the UNC-40/DCC receptor is required for this process by mediating repulsion from the UNC-6/Netrin source.
Infants as young as six months old are sensitive to the sound patterns of their native language and begin to distinguish between them from other languages.
We show that this algorithm predicts the phonetic learning patterns of human infants, including those for consonant place of articulation and vowel height.

Our results suggest that infants' perception and production of speech sounds are closely linked in development, and that this link is mediated by a mechanism that involves the interaction between auditory and motor systems.
Autophagy is a conserved cellular process that degrades damaged organelles and proteins to maintain cell homeostasis.
Autophagy is an evolutionarily conserved cellular degradation pathway that degrades damaged organelles and misfolded proteins to maintain cellular homeostasis.
We found that NBR1 is required for autophagy during infection by the biotroph Pseudomonas syringae but not during infection by the necrotroph Xanthomonas campestris pv.
Inflammatory bowel diseases ( IBDs), including Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, are chronic inflammatory disorders of the gastrointestinal tract with unknown etiology.
We used a new technique to identify proteins in the blood of IBD patients and found that there are many differences between the protein profiles of IBD patients and healthy controls.
We used mass spectrometry-based proteomics to identify and quantify 1128 proteins in blood samples from 213 IBD patients and 15 healthy controls.
We have used this approach to identify genetic variants influencing protein levels in Crohn's disease.

We found that genetic variants in the gene encoding Macrophage Stimulating 1 ( MST1), which is involved in inflammation and immune response, are associated with increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes mellitus.
We have developed an approach that combines proteomics and genome-wide association studies ( GWAS) to identify genes and pathways involved in inflammatory bowel disease ( IBD).

Alvimopan for preventing ileus after abdominal surgery Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether or not alvimopan (a drug that stimulates gut motility) reduces the risk of ileus (temporary blockage of the intestines) after abdominal surgery.

Study characteristics We found one study with 43 participants comparing alvimopan (8 mg) to placebo, which was given after surgery to people who had bowel movements within 24 hours of surgery and were able to take oral diet.
We used data from a previous trial to see if we could identify which people were most likely to benefit from this treatment.
The review authors found one study with 149 participants comparing oral alvimopan versus placebo for 12 hours after surgery.
The results showed that, on average, 1 in 5 people who had surgery for an ingrowing toenail were still experiencing pain and discomfort after 6 weeks.
Alvimopan also reduced the number of people who had major complications from surgery such as death, heart attack or stroke.
Alvimopan did not affect the number of patients who were readmitted to hospital, the number of heart problems or the amount of pain medication used after surgery.
This reduces the risk of complications such as pneumonia and the need to insert a tube through the nose (nasogastric tube) to feed the baby.
Alpha 1 antitrypsin is a protein that protects the lungs from damage by an enzyme called elastase, which is produced by the body to help break down proteins and other substances.

The review authors found one small randomised trial (enrolling 1000 infants) comparing a1-antitrypsin given at birth with no treatment, and another small randomised trial (enrolling 70 infants) comparing a1-antitrypsin given at birth with placebo.
We found no evidence to support the use of a1-antitrypsin for preventing chronic lung disease in preterm infants, but more research is needed.

Genome-wide association studies ( GWAS) have identified many genetic risk factors for autism spectrum disorder ( ASD), but most of these variants are not associated with increased risk in isolation and their combined effect is unknown.
We show that these mutations lead to neurodevelopmental phenotypes consistent with those observed in human patients and in other Drosophila models of autism spectrum disorders ( ASD).
We found that, in addition to the expected effects on gene expression levels, we could identify significant effects on protein-protein interactions and functional pathways.
We found that for most genes in these regions, changes in copy number and expression levels are correlated.
We found that these mutations have very different effects on the function of the synapse and that they are associated with different clinical phenotypes in patients.
Cancer cells consume glutamine and excrete glutamate, which is the first intermediate of glutaminolysis.
We show that this process occurs in the cytosol and produces glutamate as a byproduct.
We show that this is due to the fact that the cell needs to synthesize more nucleotides than it consumes, and that the excess is released as glutamate.
This work provides insight into how glutamine metabolism is altered during tumor progression, which may lead to new therapeutic strategies for treating tumors with high glutamine addiction.
We applied our approach to the study of early human embryonic development and found that the expression of genes involved in cell proliferation is highly dynamic during this period.
We used a recently published dataset of single cells from human embryos to identify genes whose expression is consistent throughout early development.
We found that genes with high expression variability are enriched for those involved in cell-cell adhesion and signaling pathways.
Our results demonstrate the importance of considering gene expression variability when analyzing scRNA-seq data and provide insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying human embryogenesis.
In this study, we show that the Kaposi's sarcoma-associated herpesvirus ( KSHV) genome forms a large-scale chromatin-like structure in latently infected cells.
The formation and function of these structures are poorly understood, but they have been implicated in the regulation of gene expression and genome stability.
Our results suggest that the formation of LANA multimers is important for efficient replication of KSHV in vivo, as well as for the establishment of latency.
We show that LANA oligomers are essential for efficient replication, and that they form large nuclear bodies that are important for maintaining the stability of the KSHV genome.
We have used an in situ TEM technique to study the structural changes of individual gold nanoparticles during catalytic reactions.
This work demonstrates that the high activity of ultrasmall Au clusters is due to their unique electronic structure, which is different from that of larger nanoparticles and bulk metals.
Phosphite oxidation, a key step in the global biogeochemical cycle of phosphorus, is catalyzed by a unique enzyme called phytate kinase.
We show that P. anaerolimi uses a unique metabolic strategy to grow on CO2 and H2 by reducing CO2 to formic acid ( HCOOH) using an enzyme called carbon monoxide dehydrogenase ( CODH).
The review found no evidence to support the use of central lines over peripheral intravenous lines for providing nutrients to newborn babies.

The review found that people who had a central line were able to consume more nutrients than those who did not have one, but there are potential complications from this treatment such as infection or damage to the vein.
The number of needles used was lower for infants with a percutaneous approach and the number of catheters/cannulae required was also lower.

The review found that there is insufficient evidence to support the routine use of central venous feeding in people with head and neck cancer who are unable to eat normally, as it does not improve survival or quality of life.
The review found that, compared with peripheral intravenous feeding, central venous feeding may reduce the number of needles or tubes required for feeding but does not seem to have other advantages.
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT) is a treatment in which the body is exposed to high levels of oxygen.

We show that this defense is mediated by the production of reactive oxygen species and that it functions in concert with another defense mechanism that involves the inhibition of the enzyme superoxide dismutase.
We found that SodA is required for resistance to hydrogen peroxide but not to nitric oxide, whereas SodM is required for resistance to both.
These findings suggest that the acquisition of a second SOD is a key virulence factor in S. aureus infection.
In this study, we found that S. mutans harbors a unique lysine acetyltransferase ( KAT) that is responsible for the acetylation of GtfB and GtfC, which are important virulence factors in S. mutans.
We have found that the KAT ActG is able to acetylate GtfA and GtfB proteins of Streptococcus mutans, which are involved in sucrose metabolism.
We found that ActG is an important regulator of EPS production, which plays a key role in bacterial adhesion to surfaces and biofilm formation.
However, little is known about the clinical characteristics and outcomes of COVID-19 in dialysis patients.
We found 34 studies on 1, 944 COVID-19 cases among 21, 873 dialysis patients from 12 countries in four WHO regions.
We found that COVID-19 was more severe in dialysis patients, especially those on peritoneal dialysis.
We show that this compound is effective in reducing pain in several animal models of pain, including both inflammatory and neuropathic pain, with no observable side effects.

This study demonstrates that the C5a receptor is an attractive target for the development of new analgesics with reduced side effects, as it has been shown previously that the blockade of this receptor prevents the development of tolerance to morphine in mice.

We find that the spreading of failures is driven by four general mechanisms: external ( i. e., from outside), temporal ( i. e., in time), spatial ( i. e., in space), and functional ( i. e., through interactions).
Our results provide a general framework to study the propagation of failures in complex networks.
Leishmaniasis is a neglected tropical disease caused by Leishmania parasites transmitted by sandflies.
We identified a protein, cytosolic tryparedoxin peroxidase ( cTXNPx), which is involved in the detoxification of reactive oxygen species.
The compound is able to induce reactive oxygen species ( ROS) production, which causes cell death in the parasite.
We have used genetic deletion to demonstrate that this gene is essential for the survival of Leishmania donovani in vitro and in vivo.
This study provides new insights into the mechanism of action of these compounds and highlights their potential as lead structures for further development against leishmaniasis.
Rhomboid-like ( PARL) is an integral membrane protein that cleaves other proteins within the inner mitochondrial membrane and has been implicated in mitochondrial function and disease.
We found that loss of PARL function leads to a severe neurodegenerative disorder with features reminiscent of human mitochondrial diseases such as Leigh syndrome and MELAS.
Our results show that loss of PARL leads to progressive mitochondrial dysfunction and neurodegeneration, which is consistent with the clinical presentation of patients with mutations in this gene.
However, in some situations, such as when we are anticipating pain or a financial loss, we tend to prefer to receive the punishment sooner rather than later.
We tested whether people's preferences for when they receive painful stimuli are consistent with two possible ways of valuing future pain.
We find that the brain's response to future pain is not constant over time; instead, it changes in a way that depends on how far away the pain is.
We show that this is consistent with a simple value-based model in which the brain represents the future pain as a discounted sum of its immediate and delayed components.
We show that framing pain as relief rather than pain reduces the discounting of future pain and increases the willingness to pay for pain relief.
We show that this effect is not due to fear or anxiety, and we propose a model that explains how the brain combines information about the magnitude and timing of punishment to guide decisions.
The ability of cells to sense and respond to their environment is essential for survival, growth, and development.

We have identified a novel function for the protein kinase A ( PKA) signaling pathway in regulating RNA polymerase II ( RNAPII) phosphorylation and transcriptional pausing during transcription elongation.
We show that Spt4 is directly regulated by stress and that this regulation is important for efficient transcription in response to stress.
The light-harvesting antenna proteins are essential components of photosynthetic organisms and play an important role in regulating the transfer of absorbed light energy to reaction centers.
The photosynthetic antenna proteins are responsible for absorbing sunlight and transferring energy to the reaction center protein.
Our results have important implications for the design of artificial light-harvesting systems, as well as for understanding how nature optimizes energy transfer in biological systems.
The disease is characterized by inflammation of the colon, which may lead to severe complications such as bowel obstruction or perforation.
The treatment for this condition is usually steroids given by injection.
Surgery is often required for people whose condition does not improve with medication.
Cyclosporin A ( CsA) is an immunosuppressive agent used to treat severe inflammatory diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis and multiple sclerosis.
Cyclosporin A is an immunosuppressive agent, which has been used in some cases as a treatment option for people with severe ulcerative colitis.
Quality of evidence The quality of the evidence was low due to the small number of participants included in the studies.
However, there are concerns about side effects and long-term safety.
Here, we describe a method to generate an array of nanoliter droplets in a microfluidic device with a simple design.
We demonstrate that this method is capable of generating high-quality single-cell RNA-seq libraries from single cells, which are confined to nanowells on a microfluidic chip.
We demonstrate that this device can be used to perform high-throughput, automated measurements of single-cell mechanics in microfluidic devices with minimal user intervention.
We have previously shown that tubercidin ( TUB), an adenosine analog, has potent anti-Leishmanial activity in vitro and in vivo.
We have identified a novel gene, which we named tubercidin resistance protein ( TRP), in Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
We found that TRP is a membrane protein with a predicted single transmembrane helix and an intracellular C-terminal region, which we show to be essential for its function.
These results provide new insights into the role of purine metabolism in Leishmania and suggest that targeting this metabolic pathway could lead to the development of novel antileishmanial agents.

We have used single cell RNA sequencing to identify hundreds of genes expressed in specific cells during development, and we have identified several dozen transcription factors that are expressed in only one or two cell types.

We show that these factors are required to maintain the proper balance between the two cell types, and that they act in parallel with the Notch signaling pathway to regulate the expression of the downstream target gene mls-2/HMX.
Our results demonstrate that the two genes are functionally redundant, but have distinct roles during development.
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase ( G6PD) deficiency is a genetic disorder affecting millions of people worldwide, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa.
However, whether G6PD deficiency protects against malaria in women has been controversial.

We found no evidence for an association between G6PD deficiency and severe malaria, but there was some evidence for a protective effect against severe malaria in children who had one copy of the mutation.
We show that in the case of G6PD deficiency, only females are protected from severe malaria because they have two copies of the normal gene and one copy of the mutated gene.

We show that this balance between protection from malaria and increased risk of adverse outcomes is maintained through balancing selection, which keeps the protective allele at low frequencies in populations.
In plants, the process of gamete formation is called gametogenesis, which involves multiple rounds of cell division in the haploid generation ( gametophytes) before the production of gametes.
We identified a mutation in the MOS7 gene that causes embryo arrest at an early stage of development.
We find that MOS7 is required for proper localization of the spindle pole body and for accurate chromosome segregation in meiosis I.
Antibiotics for gastroenteritis Gastroenteritis is an inflammation of the stomach or intestines (bowel) which causes symptoms such as vomiting, abdominal pain, fever, and watery stools.
The Multiplex Gastrointestinal Pathogens Panel ( GPP) is a new test that detects multiple types of bacteria, viruses and parasites in stool samples.
We found that the most important factors were the cost of treating each type of illness, the number of people who get sick, and how long they are ill for.
We found that there are many uncertainties in how well these tests work, and how much they might be used by doctors.
However, GPP testing may produce many false positives ( i. e., patients who test positive but do not have an infection).
We also found that there is no evidence for the effectiveness of this approach.
Cells respond to DNA damage by inhibiting RNA polymerase II ( Pol II) to allow for efficient repair of damaged DNA.
We show that p300 acetylates and activates the RNA polymerase II-associated protein Spt5 in response to DNA damage, thereby promoting expression of genes involved in cell cycle arrest and apoptosis.
We examined the relationship between female sociality and infant mortality in wild white-faced capuchins ( Cebus capucinus).
We found that capuchin females form strong social bonds with each other, and these bonds are predicted by their genetic relatedness, rank differences, and whether they have infants together.
We found that offspring of highly social females had higher survival rates than those of less social females.
Our results show that, even though females may benefit from living with other females, males can impose costs on their mates and offspring through increased aggression.
The axon initial segment ( AIS) is the region of the neuron where action potentials are generated.
We show that this is due to the compartmentalization of action potentials in dendrites, which leads to a non-linear relationship between input and output.
Hydrogen is an attractive alternative to fossil fuels for transportation and power generation because it has high energy density and zero tailpipe emissions.
We report here the development of a synthetic metabolic pathway for the efficient production of hydrogen gas ( H2) from glucose and xylose, two major components of lignocellulosic biomass.
Hydrogen production from renewable sources has been proposed as an alternative to fossil fuels, but it still faces several challenges in terms of efficiency and cost.
We found that memory CD8 T cells are activated by RSV infection, leading to increased production of IFN-g in the lungs and blood.
Our data suggest that while RSV-specific CD8 T cells are important for protection against RSV infection, they may also contribute to severe lung pathology during secondary infections.
The ability to estimate time is essential for our daily life, but the underlying mechanisms are still unclear.
We found that this temporal tuning is not limited to specific stimuli but rather reflects a more general mechanism for representing time.
Our findings suggest that the brain represents time as an abstract quantity, and that this representation is not limited to auditory or visual stimuli but also applies to tactile stimuli.
Retinoic acid ( RA), a vitamin A derivative, is essential for normal development and maintenance of adult tissues.
However, the molecular mechanism by which RA regulates the expression of these genes is unknown.

Lytic polysaccharide monooxygenases ( LPMOs) are a family of enzymes that catalyze oxidative cleavage of polysaccharides, including cellulose and chitin, to produce oxidized products with improved digestibility for biofuel production.
Lignin peroxidases ( LPMOs) are enzymes that catalyze oxidative cleavage of lignin, which is a major component of plant biomass.
We have used a combination of experiments and computer simulations to study the reaction mechanism of this enzyme.
This work provides new insights into the mechanism of action of LPMO enzymes and their potential applications in biotechnology.
The enzyme MTHFD1 is essential for the synthesis of thymidylate, purines, and methionine from dietary sources.

We found that the mutation leads to reduced levels of several metabolites involved in one-carbon metabolism, including thymidylate synthetase ( TS) cofactor 5-methyl-tetrahydrofolate ( 5-Me-THF), which is required for DNA synthesis.
The ability of glioblastoma multiforme ( GBM) to invade normal brain tissue is one of its most malignant features.
We use a computational model to investigate how the shape and size of glioma cells influence their collective behavior, specifically whether they form flocks or streams.
The presence of these streams is associated with cancer progression, and their absence is associated with metastasis.
Our results provide a framework to identify the conditions under which different collective behaviors emerge in swarms and how they are related to each other.
Metabolomics is an emerging field of study that aims to identify and quantify all small molecules present within cells, tissues, or biofluids.
We show that l-methionine deprivation is sufficient to induce apoptosis in cancer cells by activating p53 through activation of AMPK.
The use of bacterial L-methionine deaminase ( MetD) as an antiangiogenic agent is limited by its immunogenicity and poor stability in vivo.
We have engineered a human enzyme to degrade l-methionine and demonstrated its ability to reduce methionine levels in vivo.
The accumulation of misfolded protein aggregates is associated with many human diseases, including neurodegenerative disorders.
We previously showed that CBS activity in a yeast model of homocysteine accumulation was rescued by altering the cellular environment.
We found that all three treatments activate heat shock protein 70 ( Hsp70), and that this activation is necessary to prevent cell death.
We show that this approach is applicable to other genes with similar structures and functions, including the human p53 tumor suppressor protein and the folate metabolism enzyme MTHFR.
Intermediate filaments ( IFs) are an important component of the cytoskeleton and play a key role in maintaining tissue integrity.
The intermediate filaments ( IFs) are one of three major components of the cytoskeleton in eukaryotic cells.
We have developed a novel experimental setup to study the dynamics of protein synthesis in real-time with high spatial resolution.
Our results show that the association is driven by electrostatic interactions between the two proteins, which are mediated by their negatively charged surfaces.
Neglected tropical diseases ( NTDs) are a group of infectious diseases that disproportionately affect the world's poorest populations.
In this study, we aimed to identify all relevant actors involved in NTD research in Germany and their international collaborations.
We found that the majority of studies were conducted in Europe ( 71%), and that researchers from Germany contributed to more than half of all studies.
We found that NTD research in Germany is highly dispersed, with more than 200 research institutions involved.

We have previously shown that T. brucei infection in mice leads to reduced numbers of mature B cells in the spleen and lymph nodes, which is associated with an inability to mount effective humoral immune responses against secondary infections.
However, nothing is known about whether such an immune memory exists in humans infected with Trypanosoma brucei rhodesiense, which causes sleeping sickness in East Africa.
We found that Gambian sleeping sickness causes a significant reduction in the number of circulating CD8+ T cells, but not CD4+ T cells or B cells.

We found that the levels of antibodies against red blood cells were significantly lower in HAT patients compared to healthy individuals, and that this reduction was associated with increased parasite load.
We found that the immune response to the parasite is preserved in HAT patients, which suggests that B cells are functional in these individuals.
The ability to regulate one's own behavior is essential for achieving goals and maintaining well-being.
We show that self-reported measures of self-control are highly reliable and predictive of future outcomes, but that behavioral measures are less so.

We have developed an in vitro model to study how sperm navigate through the female reproductive tract and found that the biophysical properties of the epithelium are critical for guiding sperm migration.
We show that the combination of microgrooves and gentle fluid flow is sufficient to guide sperm cells toward the site of fertilization.

Our results show that the parasite's ability to swim against flow is due to its unique swimming mechanism, which involves three anterior flagella rather than one posterior flagellum as in other trypanosomes.
Feedback loops are ubiquitous in biological systems, and they play important roles in many cellular processes such as cell cycle regulation, signal transduction, and metabolism.
We have analyzed the phase response to light pulses in wild-type mice and mice with mutations in PER1 or PER2.
We show how these two mechanisms interact and how they contribute to the robustness of the circadian clock in response to light pulses.
Autophagy is an important cellular process for maintaining homeostasis and preventing disease, but its role in cancer remains controversial.
Elevated ozone ( O3) is known to cause widespread crop damage, but the magnitude of this effect has been difficult to quantify due to the lack of long-term data on both O3 and yields.
However, there are no estimates of the impact on crop yields.
Schistosomiasis, caused by parasitic worms, is a neglected tropical disease endemic to sub-Saharan Africa and affects millions of people worldwide.

We found that mice infected with S. mansoni were less responsive to vaccination than uninfected mice, suggesting that an ongoing schistosome infection may interfere with the ability of vaccines to induce protective immunity.

We found that individuals who had schistosome infections when they received hepatitis B vaccination were less likely to have adequate levels of antibodies against the vaccine, but this did not impact their protection from hepatitis B virus infection.
We found that schistosome infection does not interfere with the development of immunity to tetanus and hepatitis B vaccination, but may impair the response to yellow fever vaccine.
We found that the immune response to the vaccine was reduced in children with schistosomiasis, but this did not affect the number of children who developed protection against hepatitis B infection.

We found that individuals who had been treated for schistosomiasis were less likely to have high levels of antibodies against hookworms and other intestinal worms than those who had not received treatment.
The NF-kB family of proteins is involved in numerous biological processes, including inflammation and immune responses.
We have discovered a new member of the NF-kB family, named RelAp43, and found that it is expressed in many different cell types.
We show that RelAp43 is expressed ubiquitously, but its expression is regulated by different stimuli to produce distinct patterns of activation.
The authors show that this interaction is conserved across all known members of the Lyssavirus genus and that it results in the inhibition of the NF-kB signaling pathway.
The formation of tooth enamel is unique in nature because it requires the assembly of enamel matrix proteins ( EMPs), which are secreted from ameloblasts, into an ordered structure.
We have discovered an evolutionary conserved motif in ameloblastin and amelogenin that is essential for their self-assembly into nanoscale structures.
This study provides new insights into how the proteins in the organic matrix are organized to form the hard, calcified structure of tooth enamel.
The aggregation of tau protein is associated with neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease ( AD).
We show that these oligomers are distinct from those formed by tau in disease, suggesting they may represent an early intermediate state in the formation of pathological aggregates.
We also found that different tau conformations are associated with different phosphorylation patterns and that these patterns change as the disease progresses.
We show that tau is composed of multiple subpopulations with distinct structural properties, which are altered in disease-relevant aggregates.
The hepatitis B virus ( HBV) genome is replicated through a unique process involving reverse transcription of an RNA template, which is synthesized from the minus strand of the viral genome.
The viral genome is packaged by the viral polymerase and its associated RNA template, called the pregenomic ( pg) RNA, into the capsid shell.
We have previously shown that phosphorylation of capsid protein ( Cp) is required for efficient viral RNA packaging in vitro.
We also show that cellular protein phosphatase 1 ( PP1), which has been implicated in other RNA virus infections, is required for efficient viral replication.

This work demonstrates an efficient method for fabricating nanoporous graphene membranes with high gas permeance and excellent gas separation performance, which may be used in various applications such as gas separation, catalysis, and energy storage.
The method is simple and scalable, which makes it possible to produce large-scale high-quality graphene membranes for water purification applications.
The review authors found no studies that compared the effectiveness and safety of these two treatments in people with acute stroke.
The review found that there is some evidence that using nasal steroids reduces the symptoms of allergic rhinitis and otitis media with adenoidectomy compared to placebo or no treatment.

Quality of the evidence The quality of the evidence for all outcomes was very low due to the small number of participants and trials in each analysis, as well as the high risk of bias associated with the use of self-reported outcome measures.
However, how this process is regulated remains unclear.

We find that this type of signaling network is capable of generating several important features of epithelial morphogenesis including the ability to sense external growth factors, generate self-sustained growth patterns, and trigger self-organized finger-like protrusions.
We show that the pattern formation mechanism is based on the interaction of two diffusing species with opposing effects and does not rely on Turing instability.
The shape of cells is an important indicator of their function, but it is difficult to quantify in real time.
We present a method for analyzing and quantifying the dynamics of cell boundaries in time-lapse microscopy images.
We find that the frequency of the oscillations is determined by the speed of the cell movement, and that the amplitude of the oscillation is related to the size of the cell.
We show that the frequency of these events is regulated by the activity of the small GTPase Rac1 and its downstream effectors.
We also discuss potential applications of our method in clinical practice.
Streptococcus mutans is a major human oral bacterium and a primary cause of tooth decay.
We have developed a new genetic tool for studying gene function in Streptococcus mutans by using a modified Cas9 nuclease ( Cas9Smu).
We have developed a method to create a library of CRISPR-Cas9 guide RNAs ( sgRNAs) targeting all essential and non-essential genes in S. aureus.
We found that 77% of the strains were defective in growth, and we identified several genes whose loss-of-function is lethal or causes severe growth defects.
This study provides a resource for future studies on S. mutans pathogenesis and identifies potential drug targets.
In this study we present a method for integrating multiple biological networks and processes into a single model of a whole cell.
We show that the integration of a new module into an existing system will have different effects depending on the nature of the interaction with the rest of the system.

Traction for hip fractures Review question We reviewed the evidence on the use of traction in people with hip fractures.
The review authors found no evidence from randomised controlled trials that traction is effective in improving recovery after a hip fracture.
The review authors found six studies (with 210 participants) that compared traction versus no traction in people with an acute hip fracture before they had surgery.
The review found ten studies, which included 1,111 participants, and compared traction to no traction for the treatment of broken bones in children's arms and legs.

We found that there were no differences between groups for any of the outcomes, although the proportion of children with decayed teeth in the fillings was slightly higher in the fluoride varnish group.
The results of this review show that there is insufficient evidence to support the use of any particular type of antibiotic for the treatment of acute bronchiolitis in children.
We found that, on average, children who received FV were less likely to have an adverse reaction than those receiving FS.
The results showed that using fluoride varnish was as good as using fluoride gel for preventing tooth decay over a three year period.
Bone remodeling is a dynamic process by which osteoblasts and osteoclasts are involved in bone formation and resorption, respectively.
We generated an opg mutant mouse model to study the role of OPG in bone metabolism.
We show that this mechanism is mediated by heparan sulfate proteoglycans ( HSPGs) on the cell surface and that it is essential for bone resorption in vivo.
Genome-wide association studies have identified many genetic variants associated with complex diseases, but it is difficult to interpret these findings because they are often located far from genes.
Genetic variation in gene expression has been extensively studied using RNA sequencing ( RNA-seq) data from human tissues, but these studies have focused only on mRNA levels.
We used quantitative proteomics to measure the abundance of 441 human proteins in 68 different individuals and found that many proteins are highly variable across individuals.
We identified several genes and pathways involved in paclitaxel resistance, including a novel gene, which we validated as a potential therapeutic target.
These results demonstrate how proteomics can be used to identify potential drug targets for cancer treatment and may lead to new therapies for this disease.

Continuous positive airway pressure therapy for obstructive sleep apnoea syndrome in children Obstructive sleep apnoea syndrome (OSAS) is a common condition where breathing stops or becomes irregular during sleep, causing daytime sleepiness and other problems.
We found no studies in older people, and we do not know if it would be safe or effective for them to use.

We found that, although CPAP is effective at reducing sleepiness and improving quality of life for older adults with OSA, it does not improve their health-related quality of life or reduce the risk of death.
We identified 511 putative sRNAs from the Clostridium difficile genome sequence using computational methods.
We also identified several small RNAs ( sRNAs) that are expressed during different stages of bacterial growth and may play roles in controlling virulence.

We identified 16 putative c-di-GMP responsive elements ( RREs) in the genome of Pseudomonas aeruginosa PAO1 and demonstrated that they are functional regulators of virulence factor production and biofilm formation.
This study represents the first comprehensive analysis of small RNAs in Clostridium difficile and will serve as a starting point for further studies on their function and regulation.
We found that HCV induces production of proinflammatory cytokines such as tumor necrosis factor-alpha ( TNF-a) through an endocytic pathway.
We found that TNF-a produced in response to HCV infection activates TNFR1, which is expressed on the surface of infected cells and inhibits viral replication.

We found that TNF-a is required for efficient antiviral responses by promoting interferon signaling through the upregulation of interferon receptor subunit 2 ( IFNAR2) and activation of interferon stimulated genes.
In many biological systems, such as foraging animals or bacteria, individuals must balance two competing goals: to exploit their current environment and to explore new environments.
Animals often rely on social learning to acquire knowledge about their environment and to improve their foraging success.
We show that this mechanism is robust to various types of noise in both sensory and behavioral systems.
The tubular endosome is an organelle that mediates the recycling of proteins and lipids from the cell surface to the plasma membrane.
The mechanism of this process has been studied extensively in vitro and in vivo; however, the molecular mechanisms involved are not fully understood.
Here, we identify a novel role for the endocytic sorting protein SNX-3 in the formation of these structures.
We found that the loss of snx-3 causes defects in the formation and maintenance of the intestinal tube, which is essential for nutrient uptake from food particles.
We show that the retromer complex is not required for this process and instead identify a novel protein called RAB-5 as an essential component of the endosome-to-Golgi transport machinery.
We show that this is achieved through a novel mechanism in which the PX domain of SNX-3 competes with another endosomal protein, EEA-1, for binding to the early endosome membrane.
The results presented in this work provide an opportunity for developing a new type of battery with high energy density and long cycle life.
Our results suggest that osteocytes are key targets for the anabolic effects of Wnt/b-catenin signaling, which may be exploited to treat diseases of bone loss.
The ability to predict which antibodies are protective against SARS-CoV-2 infection has been hampered by the lack of information about the antibody responses associated with protection.
We isolated and characterized two broadly cross-reactive human monoclonal antibodies from an individual who had been infected with multiple HCV genotypes.
We identified two monoclonal antibodies ( mAbs) that bind to the envelope protein E2 and neutralize all genotypes tested.

We found that the antibody response is highly specific for the virus strain causing the initial infection and that the ability to clear subsequent infections depends on the affinity of the antibodies produced by memory B cells.
Neurotrophins are proteins that regulate neuronal growth, differentiation, and survival in the nervous system.
BDNF is produced by neurons and muscle cells ( myocytes) in response to exercise.
BDNF is produced by neurons and glial cells throughout the nervous system; however, it has been shown to be also secreted from skeletal muscle tissue.
We found that BDNF is required for maintaining the balance between fast-twitch ( type II) and slow-twitch ( type I) muscle fibers in adult mice.
Our results suggest that BDNF may be a novel therapeutic target to treat muscle wasting conditions, such as muscular dystrophy.

We found that NTHI strains causing invasive disease are more likely to be resistant to complement-mediated killing and to induce apoptosis of human airway epithelial cells via the tumor necrosis factor-alpha ( TNF-a) signaling pathway.

We found that while both invasive and non-invasive strains of H. pylori activate the same signaling pathways in macrophages, only non-invasive bacteria are able to sustain activation of these pathways in epithelial cells.
The role of the stroke liaison worker ( SLW) is to support people with stroke and their families.
We found no evidence to support the use of these interventions for any group of people with dementia, but we do not know if they are harmful.
We developed and tested ways to increase physical activity in care homes for older people.
We recruited 12 care homes across Yorkshire to take part in the study.

We interviewed people who work in care homes ( including managers, nurses, care workers and relatives) to find out their views on how well the current system works and what they think would be needed for it to improve.
The MoveMore study aimed to see if or how much more people moved around at home by using a simple, low-cost technology-based system called MoveMore.
We found that there were many things that could be done to improve the quality of life for people living in care homes.
We found that there is little evidence to support the use of these interventions, but we also identified some gaps in the literature that could be addressed by future research.

We also found that staff were very interested to learn more about how to improve quality of life and that they wanted to know what would make their job easier, but that there was little support available to help them do so.
We also found that we need to improve the way in which the intervention is delivered, for example by using more interactive elements such as video clips or quizzes.
ABCA12 is an ATP-binding cassette transporter that regulates lipid transport in keratinocytes and has been implicated in several skin diseases.

We have used a genetic approach to identify a mouse model of HI and show that this model exhibits many features of human HI, including skin fragility, abnormal skin permeability, and defective lipid metabolism.
We show here that Abca12 is also required for the formation of lamellar granules, which are secreted onto the surface of the airway epithelium to form a protective barrier against pathogens.
We also show that Abca12 is required for Abca1-mediated cholesterol efflux from cells, which has important implications for understanding how Abca1 regulates plasma membrane sterol levels in vivo.
We found that OtsB1 and OtsB2 are both required to maintain the virulence of Mtb in mice but only OtsB1 is required for growth on glucose.
The metabolic reprogramming in cancer cells is one of the hallmarks of cancer, which is essential for tumor growth and metastasis.
In this study, we found that EBV-encoded microRNA BART1-5p promotes NPC progression through promoting glycolysis and angiogenesis.
We found that EBV-encoded miRNA Bart1-5p could target AMPKa1 and regulate the AMPK/mTOR/HIF1 pathway, leading to increased glucose uptake and angiogenesis in NPC cells.
This study provides new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying the pathogenesis of NPC, which may be useful to develop novel therapeutic strategies for this disease.
The venom of Bothrops jararaca is one of the most studied venoms from snakes found in Brazil, but little is known about the ontogeny of its venom components.

We found that the venom composition of B. jararaca undergoes significant changes during development and maturation, which are likely related to the shift from ectothermic to endothermic prey consumption.
Our results show that the venom of young snakes is more complex than adult venom, and that this change is associated with an increase in neurotoxins and a decrease in metalloproteinases.
We show that fruit traits have evolved rapidly in response to changes in the diversity and abundance of dispersers, but not in response to other factors such as climate or soil fertility.

We show that fruit size is strongly correlated with the number of frugivorous animals that consume it, suggesting that selection for larger fruits has been driven by competition among consumers to access resources.
The weathered bedrock layer is thought to play an important role in controlling soil formation and landscape evolution.
The infiltration of water into the subsurface is an important component of the hydrologic cycle, but it is difficult to measure directly in the field.

Here we show that rock moisture--the exchangeable water stored in weathered bedrock--is an important component of terrestrial hydrology and plays a key role in controlling groundwater recharge and streamflow generation.
Rock moisture storage has been shown to be important in local-scale studies but is largely absent from large-scale models of the Earth system.

The TCR repertoire is generated through a process called V(D) J recombination, which involves the random combination of different gene segments to generate an enormous number of unique receptor sequences.
We have developed a method to recover and sequence TCR sequences from single cells with high efficiency by combining crosslinking with RNA sequencing.
We also demonstrate the ability of this method to identify rare cell types and to perform gene expression analysis in single cells.

We found that CLInt-Seq is as sensitive as traditional methods of detecting virus-specific T cells, but has several advantages in terms of speed, cost, and ability to identify rare antigen-specific T cell populations.
The central nervous system ( CNS) is an important target for drug therapy.
We have determined the structure of an amyloid-forming segment of the major curli protein, CsgA, which forms a steric zipper-like structure with a unique intermolecular interface.
Our results provide new insight into the molecular details of curli assembly and may lead to strategies to disrupt amyloid-based bacterial biofilm formation.
The Vpu protein is an accessory protein encoded by HIV-1, which plays important roles in viral pathogenesis.
The HIV-1 accessory protein Vpu is known to downregulate the cell surface expression of CD4 by binding to it and recruiting the cellular E3 ubiquitin ligase SCFSkp2 for degradation.
In addition, we show that Vpu also blocks the degradation of other cellular proteins targeted by bTrCP, including Emi1 and Cdc25A.
We show that this phosphorylation event is required for efficient Vpu-mediated down-regulation of CD4 and that it occurs in a manner dependent on the cellular protein kinase CK1a.
We found that the Vpu protein is important for efficient virus production and that it has an essential function in the viral life cycle.
We use a coupled poroelastic model to estimate stress and pore pressure changes in the subsurface of the Delaware Basin, an area with high induced seismicity.
Our results suggest that the observed seismicity is caused by the injection of fluids into the subsurface, which causes stress changes in the surrounding rock and leads to earthquakes.
We show that the basement stress response depends on the permeability of the overlying sedimentary layer, which is difficult to measure directly.
The cohesin complex is essential for maintaining genome integrity by holding together replicated chromosomes until they are properly segregated to daughter cells at the end of each cell division.
Cohesin is an essential protein complex that plays a central role in chromosome segregation during cell division and in transcriptional regulation in non-dividing cells.
Cohesin is a ring-shaped protein complex that holds sister chromatids together during meiosis.
We have used genome-wide analysis to identify genes whose expression is altered in cells from individuals with CdLS, and we show that these changes are associated with clinical severity.
We found that cohesin is not only involved in chromosome segregation but also plays a role in gene expression regulation by binding to the promoters of the dysregulated genes.
We have used a new technique to measure gene expression in S. pneumoniae and identified genes that are expressed during infection.
We have identified 2150 transcription start sites in the genome of this organism.
We also identified many new regulatory RNAs in the 5' untranslated region of genes.
We also show that this regulatory mechanism is important for bacterial survival and pathogenicity in vivo.
Our results provide a comprehensive view of the regulatory network of this pathogen and highlight potential targets for intervention.
The molecular mechanism by which auxin regulates gene expression has been extensively studied in Arabidopsis thaliana, but it remains unclear whether this mechanism is conserved across plant species.
The TIR1/AFB family of receptors are central to this process and have been shown to be important for plant development and defense responses against pathogens.
We show that these mutations affect the ability of TIR1 to bind Aux/IAA proteins and promote their degradation.
Our results suggest that TIR1 oligomerization is an important regulatory mechanism for auxin signaling, which may be conserved in other plants.
This study demonstrates that the accumulation of [^18F]FAZA is associated with increased blood flow and metabolic activity, but not with other markers of hypoxia or acidosis.
The ability to sense and respond to changes in synaptic activity is essential for learning and memory, but how this is achieved at the molecular level remains unclear.
The propagation of electrical activity from dendrites to soma is known as backpropagation and has been shown to be important for synaptic plasticity.
We show here that bAPs trigger calcium release from intracellular stores in cardiac myocytes.
Here, we show that calcium release from intracellular stores is not only essential for the generation of backpropagating action potentials but also plays an important role in their regulation.
We show that this calcium signal is mediated by the ryanodine receptor ( RyR) and is essential for the formation of new spines in response to stimulation.
We show that this mechanism is important for the generation of calcium transients and synaptic plasticity at individual synapses.
In this study, we show that NKT cells are not required for resistance to L. donovani infection in mice.

We found that iNKT cell activation by a-galactosylceramide ( a-GalCer), which is commonly used to activate iNKT cells in vitro and in vivo, exacerbates disease in mice infected with Leishmania donovani.
We found that a-GalCer was unable to induce protective immunity against Leishmania major, and this defect was due to an inability to activate CD8+ T cells.
We found that a-GalCer treatment during chronic Leishmania donovani infection resulted in a significant reduction in parasite burden and increased survival of infected mice.
These results highlight the need to consider the context of the host and pathogen in designing NKT cell-based therapies.
We developed PDkit, an open-source software framework to support the development and testing of new methods for assessing PD symptoms.
This work aims to provide a common framework for researchers working on the analysis of these data, allowing them to compare their results with others more easily.

The phosphohexomutases are a family of enzymes that catalyze the reversible transfer of phosphate groups between sugar molecules, and they play important roles in carbohydrate metabolism in bacteria, fungi, plants, and animals.
Phosphoglucomutase is an enzyme that catalyzes the conversion between two sugar phosphate molecules, glucose 6-phosphate and glucose 1-phosphate.

The Bacillus subtilis GlmM protein is an example of such a protein, which catalyzes the conversion of glucosamine 6-phosphate to glucosamine 1-phosphate in the biosynthesis of bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan.
We show that these enzymes have an additional function in the cell wall synthesis pathway, which is important for bacterial survival under stress conditions.
DNA is constantly exposed to damage from endogenous and exogenous sources, which can cause mutations in the genome.
DNA damage is a major threat to genomic integrity and cellular survival.
We have previously shown that in the nematode Caenorhabditis elegans, the TLS protein complex is required for maintaining genomic integrity during meiosis and mitosis.
In this study we have used a combination of genetic approaches to identify the types of mutations that occur in cells lacking one or more components of the translesion synthesis pathway.
Here we show that in the nematode Caenorhabditis elegans, TLS is not only error-free but also anti-clastogenic, i. e., it prevents mutations from occurring at damaged sites.
In this study, we used molecular methods to detect and identify Leishmania parasites from humans in Zambia.
In this study, we investigated the prevalence of Taenia solium infection among people living in a rural area of eastern Zambia.
We conducted a survey to determine the prevalence of schistosomiasis in school children from two communities in the city of Cotonou, Benin.
We found that Taenia solium infection is common among school children, with 12.
The asymmetric division of cells is an important mechanism for generating cellular diversity in multicellular organisms.
Here we report on the first quantitative analysis of the first cell division in 42 species of nematode worms.
We have generated a large dataset of subcellular phenotypes for 143 nematode species, including 128 strains from 27 Caenorhabditis species and 15 non-Caenorhabditis species.
We have used this system to analyze the role of microtubules in spindle orientation and found that they are essential for proper spindle positioning.
We found that although all cells undergo asymmetric division, there are large differences between species in the extent to which this is achieved by different mechanisms.
We find that the basic mechanism of asymmetric cell division is conserved between C. elegans and C. briggaemus, but the underlying mechanisms are not identical.
The World Health Organization ( WHO) has set ambitious goals for the elimination of onchocerciasis as a public health problem by 2020 and trachoma as a blinding disease by 2020.
The World Health Organization ( WHO) has set ambitious targets to eliminate LF as a public health problem by 2020, but progress is slow and uneven across countries.
However, in many parts of the world, especially in Africa, there is no reliable information on the distribution of these diseases.
The disease is transmitted by the bite of infected black flies, which breed in water bodies such as rivers and streams.
We show that the most important contacts in protein structure are not strongly affected by evolutionary history but rather reflect the physical interactions within proteins.
We show that these spurious contacts are compatible in size and number with those found in real protein structures.
We also found that miR-34a and miR-21 are regulated by mTORC1 signaling in vivo.
The immunomodulatory drug (IMiD) thalidomide binds to the E3 ligase cereblon and induces the degradation of target proteins by the ubiquitin-proteasome system.
We show that the thalidomide binding site is located in the N-terminal domain of Cereblon, and we identify residues within this domain that are critical for thalidomide binding.
We found that Rabex-5 is required for the activation of the interferon regulatory factor 7 ( IRF7), which is essential for the expression of type I interferons, by binding to its promoter region.
Our results suggest that the immunosuppressive activity of IMiDs is mediated by their ability to disrupt the interaction between Rabex-5 and Cereblon.

Here, we show that androgens suppress the production of chemokine ligand 13 ( CXCL13) in prostate cancer cells by inhibiting its transcription through direct binding to the promoter region of the gene encoding for this protein.
We found that TGF-b signaling is required for fibrosis in this model and that blocking TGF-b signaling with a small molecule inhibitor prevents fibrosis.
Chlamydia trachomatis ( Ct) causes trachoma, which is the leading infectious cause of blindness in the world.
We found that one of these strains was more likely to be present in high numbers than others, and that this strain was also associated with increased severity of disease.
We found that the two viruses were genetically different, suggesting they were from separate sources.
However, the role of vulnerability in shaping the impacts of floods is still poorly understood.
We present a global analysis of flood risk, combining high-resolution flood hazard maps with population data.
We find that vulnerability has decreased globally over time, with the greatest reductions occurring in developing countries.
We find that, even with no further climate change, the number of people exposed to flooding will increase by more than 50% in the coming decades due to urbanization and land use changes.
Water is ubiquitous, yet its properties remain poorly understood at the molecular level.

We use combined experimental and computational methods to study water adsorption on nanoscale transition metal carbides ( TMCs) and find that they exhibit unique properties compared to other carbon-based materials.
We show that hydrophobic ions, such as K^+, increase water mobility in the confined space of the interlayer and enhance water removal from the surface.
We have identified a novel mutation in RECQL4, which is associated with RTS, RAPADILINO syndrome and Baller-Gerold syndrome.
The disease is characterized by a variety of clinical features, including short stature and low bone density.
In this study, we show that Recql4 is essential for bone formation by osteoblasts, cells responsible for bone growth and repair.
We found that RecQL4 is required for normal bone formation by regulating the proliferation of osteoblasts, which are cells responsible for bone mineralization.
We found that deletion of Recql4 in osteoblasts and osteocytes in mice resulted in reduced bone mass due to increased bone resorption by osteoclasts.
We found that the absence of Recql4 alone is insufficient to cause cancer in mice but that the combination of Recql4 and Pold deficiency does lead to tumors.
Our results suggest that RECQL4 is a tumor suppressor gene in humans.
Visceral leishmaniasis, also known as Kala-azar, is a disease caused by parasites of the genus Leishmania.
We have previously identified a genetic locus on chromosome 22q12 as a major risk factor for leprosy in the Aringa population of South Sudan.

In this study, we have used a genome-wide association approach to identify genetic variants associated with susceptibility to leprosy in a population of individuals from the Masalit ethnic group living in northern Nigeria.
We found that the genetic diversity of the two groups is very low, with many genes showing significant differences between them.
This study provides new insights into the genetic basis of this disease, which is common among people of African descent.
However, the molecular mechanisms that regulate this process remain unknown.
We show that the shape of the heart is determined by the shape of individual cells within it.
We use zebrafish to study how the heart develops and find that the shape of individual cells within the heart is critical for the overall shape of the organ.
We show that the ability to form sarcomeres is essential for maintaining normal heart function and that this process is regulated by the actin cytoskeleton.
Urbanization has been identified as one of the most important factors in the future of humanity's sustainability.
Urbanization has been linked to increased economic productivity and poverty reduction; however, urbanization also exacerbates inequality.
This study demonstrates how such assessments can be extended to other goals, including those related to climate change, health, education, and poverty reduction.
We sequenced the genome of the whale shark ( Rhincodon typus), which is one of the largest vertebrates on Earth.
We show that the whale shark has an unusually low metabolic rate for its body size, which is consistent with its slow growth rate and feeding behavior.
The relationship between longevity and body mass has been well established in animals but not in plants.
We found that the GC content is inversely related to the codon adaptation index, which is a measure of how well the genome adapts to the genetic code.
We found that the whale shark has many genes that are associated with longevity and that these genes are conserved in other long-lived animals.
Recent studies have shown that many short open reading frames ( smORFs) are translated in eukaryotes, but their functions remain largely unknown.
We have identified a large number of short open reading frames ( smORFs) in the Drosophila genome that encode small proteins with hydrophobic domains.
We have identified a novel protein called Hemotin, which is expressed by immune cells of the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster and is required for normal phagocytosis.
We found that the loss of this protein results in accumulation of undigested bacterial debris inside cells, which leads to increased cell death and reduced lifespan.
This study identifies Stannin, a protein known to be important for iron homeostasis, as the functional homolog of hemotin in vertebrates.
The use of heparin to prevent blood clots in the extracorporeal circuit has been shown to reduce the risk of thrombosis but also increases the risk of bleeding.

This review found that there is insufficient evidence to support the use of continuous renal replacement therapy with or without intermittent dialysis for people with acute kidney failure and severe heart disease.
Anticoagulants such as warfarin and aspirin are commonly prescribed to prevent blood clots from forming in the legs ( deep vein thrombosis) or lungs ( pulmonary embolism).
We wanted to find out if there are any medications that could prevent blood clots from forming in the machine or in the blood vessels, and whether these would be safe and effective.
Study characteristics We found 34 randomised controlled trials with a total of 1960 participants, published up to July 2015.
We found only one trial comparing citrate with no anticoagulant and this did not provide any evidence for or against its use.
There was not enough evidence to show whether any of these drugs were better than others.

More research is needed to find out if there is a benefit of using citrate instead of heparin for patients on CRRT, and whether it would be safe to start CRRT without any medication to prevent blood clotting.
We show that, in fact, the four scaling laws are not independent but interdependent, and that they combine to produce a single, unified scaling law for all eukaryotes.
We show that this scaling is consistent with predictions of metabolic theory, but only when we account for differences in body size among species.
We show that the scaling of growth and mortality rates is consistent across species, suggesting that they are driven by similar mechanisms.
Cystic fibrosis ( CF) is caused by mutations in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator ( CFTR), which are mostly located within the protein-coding region of the gene.

We have previously identified a protein called CAL as an adaptor molecule that mediates this degradation, and we show here that it is also required for the normal trafficking of wild-type CFTR to the plasma membrane.
Here we describe a rational drug discovery approach that uses computer-aided protein design to create small molecules that bind to CFTR and stabilize its function in cells.

We have developed a method for designing proteins with specific binding properties, which is based on the idea of using a library of known protein structures as templates to generate new sequences that are likely to fold into similar shapes.

We found that our method was able to predict with high accuracy which peptides would bind to CAL and which would not, and we were able to identify several new peptides that are likely to be good candidates for further study as potential drugs.
Our results suggest that these compounds may be useful as adjunctive therapies to improve the clinical outcome of patients with cystic fibrosis.
The BET family of proteins is composed of two subfamilies, BRD2/BRD3 and BRD4, which are known to play important roles in transcriptional regulation.
We show that a new class of BET inhibitors, called proteolysis-targeting chimeras ( PROTACs), is more effective than existing BET inhibitors and has reduced toxicity.
Our results suggest that GNE-6642 may be a promising new therapy for patients with castration-resistant prostate cancer.
Plants have evolved various strategies to cope with heat stress, including changes in leaf morphology and photosynthesis.
The phytochromes, which sense light, and the phytohormones, such as auxin, interact to regulate plant growth.
We show that PIF4 directly activates the expression of YUCCA8, an enzyme involved in auxin biosynthesis.
We found that YUC8 is required for the normal growth inhibition of hypocotyls in response to light and heat stress.
We found that the phytochrome-interacting factor ( PIF) transcription factors are important regulators of hypocotyl growth at different temperatures.

Our study provides new insights into how the phytochrome-interacting factor ( PIF) transcription factors integrate their action with the auxin signaling pathway to regulate plant growth and development.
Climate change is expected to alter both plant genotypes and microbial communities in soils.
We found that the composition of mycorrhizal fungi associated with Pinus edulis trees was strongly influenced by host genetics, but not by environmental factors such as soil type or climate.
We found that endophytic fungi play an important role in drought tolerance of their host plants, and that this is mediated by the production of secondary metabolites.
Our results suggest that forest trees may be able to adapt to climate change by increasing their association with beneficial fungi.
DNA is replicated only once per cell division, and this process must be completed before the cell divides.
We have found that in budding yeast, the protein phosphatase Cdc14 binds to and dephosphorylates Mck1p, which is a homolog of GSK-3.
We show that Mck1p is required for efficient degradation of Cdc6p by the SCF complex.
We show that Gsk3 is required for proper cell cycle progression and that it acts by promoting the degradation of Cdc6, a key component of the origin licensing complex.
Livestock is an important source of income in many rural communities, but also poses a risk to human health due to zoonotic diseases such as brucellosis.

In this study we assessed the knowledge about dengue fever among residents in an urban area of northern Vietnam and examined the relationship between the presence of Aedes mosquitoes and the number of domestic animals kept at home.
We found that the risk of dengue fever was higher in areas with high levels of human population density, low income, and poor sanitation conditions.

The results showed no evidence for an increased risk of dengue infection among people living in households with animals, but there was a clear need for education about how to prevent mosquito bites and therefore dengue fever.
Oct1 is an important regulator of stem cells in many tissues, including the hematopoietic system.
We found that deletion of Oct1 in intestinal stem cells or Paneth cells leads to severe defects in stem cell function and increased susceptibility to colitis.
We show that Oct1 is required for tumor formation and progression in a mouse model of colon cancer.
We show that Oct1 is essential for normal intestinal homeostasis and that its loss leads to colitis and cancer.

The Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis ( GPELF) aims to interrupt transmission of filarial parasites by treating entire communities with drugs that kill adult worms and microfilariae, thereby reducing the number of infected individuals in the community.
In order to assess whether or not transmission has been interrupted, the World Health Organization recommends conducting Transmission Assessment Surveys ( TAS).
The results from this study show that the school-based test missed many cases of trachoma, and that the community-based survey found more cases than the school-based test.
We have determined the crystal structure of an antibody from a human survivor of the 1918 pandemic influenza virus infection bound to the hemagglutinin protein of this virus.
We show that the 1F1 heavy chain is important for influenza A virus infection of human cells, but not mouse cells, by interacting with host cell surface glycoproteins.
During mitosis, the genome is replicated but not transcribed; however, gene expression is re-established in the daughter cells after mitosis.
We show that mitosis is accompanied by waves of transcriptional activation in human cancer cells.
We show that this is due to the fact that the measured gene expression levels are averaged over a population of cells with different internal cell cycle times.
We find that the transcriptional program is controlled by a small number of key regulators, which are activated in a specific order during the cell cycle.
Abdominal aortic aneurysms are bulges in the main artery of the abdomen.
The current practice is to monitor small aneurysms and only operate on them if they grow larger than 55mm.
Medications such as ACE inhibitors, beta blockers, statins and antibiotics have been suggested to slow down the growth of AAAs.
We found no evidence from randomised controlled trials that any form of medical treatment is effective at slowing down the growth of abdominal aortic aneurysms.
We found seven studies with 1558 participants to include in our review.
The results showed that roxithromycin reduced the risk of developing pneumonia by 0.86 mm, and this result was statistically significant.
The antibiotics were generally well-tolerated, but there was one case of an allergic reaction to the antibiotic ceftriaxone.
The review found that propranolol, which is one type of beta-blocker drug, did not prevent migraine attacks but may reduce their frequency when taken regularly.
The brain is constantly bombarded by sensory stimuli, but we are only aware of some of them.

Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) is a treatment used to help babies breathe more easily by increasing the amount of oxygen they receive and keeping their lungs open.

Study characteristics The evidence is current to 31 January 2016.
The review authors found 11 studies, with a total of 1205 babies who received either dexamethasone or placebo before birth.
The studies did not show any significant differences in the number of deaths, or in the number of people who had surgery for complications (such as bleeding) between the two groups.

We found that these genes are regulated by the circadian clock in an oscillatory manner, with some genes being induced at night and others during the day.

This work demonstrates that the surface of nanoparticles can be modified by adsorption of small molecules in ways that are similar to those used in nanoparticle synthesis but with much weaker bonding strength.

Toxoplasma gondii is an apicomplexan parasite that infects a wide range of hosts, including humans and mice.
The Toxoplasma invasion process involves the formation of a tight junction between the parasite and the host cell membrane, which is essential for successful infection.
The rhoptries are secretory organelles located in the apical end of the parasite that play an essential role in host cell invasion.
We show that this protein is essential for the formation of the rhoptry neck, which is required for invasion of host cells by Toxoplasma.
We show that this is due to an inability to form the apical junction between the parasite and the host cell.
We found that the rhoptry neck is essential for invasion but not for attachment or egress.
