Ultrasound scan versus routine mid-pregnancy screening for detecting abnormal growth in the baby Ultrasound scans are commonly used during pregnancy to check on the health of the baby, but it is not clear whether they improve outcomes for the mother or her baby.

The review authors searched for studies that compared the use of ultrasound to measure the size of the baby's head (biparietal diameter) with measuring the abdominal circumference by tape, or with feeling the abdomen to estimate the size of the baby.
We found no evidence to support or refute the use of progesterone for preventing preterm birth.

Dengue is the most prevalent mosquito-borne viral disease in the world today, with over 100 million cases annually.
Vector control is one of the main strategies to reduce dengue transmission, and there are many different types of interventions available.
We found only nine studies that used the gold standard research design, called a randomized controlled trial.

We conclude that there is insufficient evidence to recommend any particular method for preventing dengue infection, but further research is needed to identify the most effective interventions.

We also looked at how well the tool worked in practice and what it would cost if used as part of a policy to change the number of nurses working on wards.
We found that nurses felt that there was adequate staffing for most shifts, but not all shifts.

We found that staffing levels below the minimum required to provide safe care were associated with poor-quality care, but we did not find evidence that higher-than-required staffing levels improved quality.
We found that there are many factors that affect how much work nurses do, and we have developed a model to help us understand this.
We found that the Safer Nursing Care Tool is useful for identifying areas of concern, but it does not provide information about how many nurses are needed to meet the needs of patients.

We found that, although these policies would save money in the short term, they would not be cost-effective if implemented on their own and would require additional measures to ensure adequate staffing levels.

Inhalers containing beclomethasone or budesonide versus other inhalers for children with asthma Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether beclomethasone and budesonide inhalers are better than other types of inhaler for treating children with asthma.
Fluticasone is one of the most commonly prescribed steroids for treating asthma, but it has not been compared with other commonly used steroids such as beclomethasone or budesonide.
The review found no significant difference between these three steroids in terms of improvement in symptoms, lung function or peak flow.
The results showed that there was no difference in the number of children who had an adverse event (such as a sore throat) when using either corticosteroid with or without antibiotics.
However, there is some evidence that fluticasone may cause more side effects (such as a sore throat) than other steroids.

This study provides an example for how data from routine health care settings can be used to identify individuals at risk of infection with tapeworms, which may lead to more targeted interventions and improved public health.

Laparoscopic and robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery for prostate cancer Review question We reviewed the evidence about the benefits and harms of laparoscopic and robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery versus open surgery for men with prostate cancer.
Key results We found that there was no difference between the two types of surgery for survival, quality of life, sexual function and urinary complications.
We found no evidence to support or refute any particular type of surgery for men with locally advanced prostate cancer.

Key results The review found that there was little to no difference between robotic-assisted and open surgery for prostate cancer in terms of overall complication rates, serious complication rates, quality of life, pain control, and survival.
The studies were not of good quality and we are uncertain whether or not RARP reduces pain after surgery.
The review authors found no studies that looked at the effect of surgeon volume on outcomes after surgery.
Parkinson's disease is caused by mutations in genes encoding proteins involved in mitochondrial function, including PINK1 and Parkin.
Parkin is an E3 ligase that plays a key role in mitophagy, a selective autophagic degradation of damaged mitochondria.
We propose a model for how this occurs, involving a chain reaction mechanism of mitochondrial ubiquitination.
Chagas disease is caused by the protozoan parasite Trypanosoma cruzi, which infects millions of people in Latin America.
The ability of T. cruzi to survive and replicate within mammalian cells is dependent upon its ability to acquire nutrients from the host cell.

We found that T. cruzi amastigotes acquire lipids from the host cell, including cholesterol and triacylglycerols, which are likely derived from the endoplasmic reticulum.
The dengue virus is transmitted by mosquitoes and causes severe disease in humans.
The majority of severe dengue cases occur in children, and these are often caused by a second infection with a different serotype.

The development of an effective vaccine against dengue virus ( DENV) has been challenging, in part because it requires elicitation of a broad and potent neutralizing antibody response to multiple serotypes.
A recent phase III clinical trial of a live attenuated tetravalent dengue vaccine showed disappointing results in terms of protection from disease.
Our results provide new insights into the complex nature of dengue virus antibody responses and may help to explain why some individuals are protected from severe disease while others are not.

We found that p27 is required for mitochondrial function and that its loss leads to decreased ATP production and increased reactive oxygen species ( ROS) generation.
We show that caffeine increases mitochondrial p27 levels, which is required for its effects on mitochondrial function.

The emergence of artemisinin-resistant Plasmodium falciparum parasites in Southeast Asia is a major threat to malaria control efforts, but the mechanisms underlying this resistance are not well understood.

In this study, we compared the cost-effectiveness of three different strategies for treating vivax malaria in Cambodia: ( i) current practice using artemisinin-based combination therapy ( ACT) with artesunate and mefloquine; ( ii) an alternative ACT with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine and mefloquine; and ( iii) adding dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine to the current ACT regimen as chemoprophylaxis.
We found that triple ACT treatment was less effective at reducing the spread of MQ resistance than single or double ACT treatment and had little effect on the decline of PPQ resistance.
We found that the introduction of these drugs had an immediate effect on reducing the number of cases of malaria in the community, but also led to the emergence of drug resistance.
However, chemoprophylaxis is not currently used in most endemic areas due to concerns about drug resistance and side effects.

We show that A-T cells have increased levels of reactive oxygen species ( ROS) and that this is due to an increase in expression of the NADPH oxidase subunit NOX4.

Our results suggest that NOX4 is a potential target for therapeutic intervention in ataxia-telangiectasia.
The nuclear pore complex ( NPC) is a large proteinaceous structure that spans the nuclear envelope and mediates the exchange of macromolecules between the cytoplasm and nucleus.
The nuclear pore complex ( NPC) is a large proteinaceous structure that mediates the transport of macromolecules across the nuclear envelope.
We have developed a method to quantify the number of each protein in the NPC and found that the number of most proteins is consistent with a single copy per NPC.
We find that the NPC in S. cerevisiae has a very different composition compared with the human NPC, which may be important for its function.
We also show that the stoichiometry of the complex is altered in mutants with defects in protein-protein interactions.

The aim of this study was to find out if giving clot-busting drugs (fibrinolytics) in the coronary arteries would help prevent microvascular obstruction and improve outcomes for patients with heart attack.
We recruited 267 patients and were unable to show any difference between the two treatments.

Men are less likely to attend weight loss programmes than women, and there is no evidence that they lose more weight if they do attend.
The Football Fans in Training ( FFIT) programme was developed to help men lose weight and improve their health through football.
The programme was delivered in groups, using a combination of behaviour change techniques, diet advice and exercise classes.

The study compared two groups of people who had completed the programme, one group received an email reminder to attend a reunion session after 9 months, while the other did not receive this reminder.
The study compared the effects of two different types of diet, one with a low carbohydrate content ( Atkins) and one with a low fat content ( Weight Watchers).

The Orkney Islands in Scotland were an important cultural center during the Neolithic period ( 3800-2500 BC).
The Neolithic is characterized by the construction of monumental tombs and the introduction of new technologies such as pottery and stone tools.
Here, we use ancient DNA to test whether there was an influx of new people during the transition from the Neolithic to the Bronze Age in the Orkney Islands, Scotland.
We show that the majority of Y-chromosome lineages found in modern European populations are derived from the Neolithic farmers who arrived in Europe around 6, 000 years ago.

The GluClR is an important target for insecticides, but its structure has not been solved to date.
The development of resistance to ivermectin ( IVM) in mosquitoes has been reported from several countries, but the underlying mechanism( s) remain unknown.
We have identified and characterized a novel GluClR from the parasitic nematode Haemonchus contortus, which is an important parasite of livestock.
We show that IVM induces a long-lasting, tonic current in neurons that is mediated by activation of GABAA receptors.
We show that this is due to an interaction between IVM and the GluClR pore region, which prevents the channel from opening.
Our results provide new insights into how IVM interacts with its target, which may help in understanding resistance mechanisms and in the design of more potent derivatives.
The development of plant organs relies on the coordinated division of cells in specific directions, which is essential for the formation of tissues with distinct shapes and functions.
We use the stereotypical division pattern of Arabidopsis thaliana embryos to investigate how cells select their division planes.
We use this approach to predict and validate the location of cell division in a developing Drosophila embryo.
We found that the division plane is determined by the position of the nucleus and the orientation of the cell wall.

Our results suggest that GILT plays an essential role in the development of T. spiralis larvae, and it is likely to be involved in the invasion of host cells by the parasite.
We show that micrometer-sized plastic debris can cause damage to biological cells through a simple physical mechanism involving membrane deformation and rupture.
We show that microplastics can cause significant changes in cell membrane properties such as surface charge and elasticity.
We show that this is due to the formation of a proteinaceous plug at the pore entrance which reduces the permeability of the membrane and causes its collapse.

We demonstrate that microplastic particles are able to induce cell death in human red blood cells, and we show that this is due to the physical interaction between the plastic particle and the membrane of the cell.
ABP1 is known to be involved in many plant developmental processes, including root growth inhibition by auxin.

We found that the mutant plants are defective in the regulation of auxin biosynthesis and transport but not in the transcriptional responses to auxin.

We found that many mycobacterial genes lack a typical 5' UTR and do not use a canonical Shine-Dalgarno sequence to recruit the ribosome for translation initiation.

Vitamin E for treating dementia Review question We reviewed the evidence about the effect of vitamin E on people with mild cognitive impairment or Alzheimer's disease.
The review authors searched for all relevant studies up to September 2015.

Vitamin E for mild cognitive impairment and Alzheimer's disease Review question We reviewed the evidence about the effects of vitamin E on people with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) or Alzheimer's disease (AD).
Study characteristics The evidence is current to 22 April 2016.
Study characteristics We found six studies (with a total of 1007 participants) that met our inclusion criteria.
The review authors found 11 studies, with a total of 1, 111 participants.
Vitamin E did not affect the risk of developing Alzheimer's disease in people with mild cognitive impairment over 36 months.
The review authors did not find any evidence to support the use of vitamin E for reducing symptoms of dementia and improving quality of life.
There was no evidence of any effect on cognitive decline or mortality.
However, these results were based on small numbers of participants in the included studies and so more research is needed to confirm them.

Epinephrine for bronchiolitis in infants under two years Review question We reviewed the evidence about the effect of epinephrine (adrenaline) on death, hospitalization, duration of hospital stay, length of oxygen therapy, need for ventilation, and side effects in babies with bronchiolitis.
We found 11 studies, with a total of 1, 111 participants.
We found no evidence of any difference in the number of people admitted to hospital on day one, or seven days later, between those who received epinephrine and those who received salbutamol.
We found that inhaled epinephrine shortened length of stay, but this result was not consistent across all studies and may be due to differences between studies.
The results show that epinephrine is superior to placebo in terms of reducing pain and improving function in the first 24 hours after treatment.

Surgical treatment for rhegmatogenous retinal detachment Review question We reviewed the evidence about the effectiveness of different types of surgery to treat rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RRD).

The main treatments for this condition include vitrectomy (removal of the vitreous gel), scleral buckle (placement of a flexible band around the eye to push the retina back in place) or pneumatic retinopexy (insertion of air into the eye to reattach the retina).
We wanted to find out which treatments are best at preventing further detachment, preserving vision and avoiding complications.
We also looked at whether or not there was an effect on quality of life, costs and side effects.
The review authors found only one study (with 30 participants) comparing scleral buckle with vitrectomy, but this was not considered to be of good quality evidence.

The discovery of DNA methylation, an important epigenetic modification, in bacteria by Walter Doerfler in 1965 was followed by the discovery of other types of DNA modifications such as methylated cytosine, adenine, uracil, and thymine.
We have discovered that the two seemingly unrelated DNA modifications, ^6mA methylation and phosphorothioation ( PT) are both recognized by the same protein, Methyltransferase-like 1 ( MLH1).

We have developed an Escherichia coli strain expressing both PT and ^6mA modification enzymes on two different DNA sequences, demonstrating that this system is versatile for genome engineering applications.

The infectious agent is made up of abnormally folded proteins called prions.
Prions are infectious proteins that propagate by templating their own misfolding and aggregation, leading to disease.
We have used a novel method for separating different types of infectious prions based on their size by centrifugation through a density gradient.
We show that the hydrodynamics of these particles is dominated by their size rather than their density.
This work shows that these small assemblies are highly resistant to proteolytic degradation and have templating activity for prion propagation in vitro.
The mitral cells are the principal output neurons of the olfactory bulb and play an essential role in processing odors.

However, there is evidence that the olfactory bulb also uses another mechanism for synchronizing neurons, namely stochastic synchronization, in which the synchronized activity arises from correlations between the input signals received by each neuron.
The majority of studies assume that the correlation between neurons is fixed, but in reality it may be dynamic.
We find that this is possible if the mitral cells are synchronized with each other, which would be expected to occur during olfactory stimulation.
We show how this mechanism could be implemented in the olfactory bulb, where granule cells are known to provide excitatory feedback to mitral cells.
We show how these two mechanisms could be implemented in a biologically plausible way and demonstrate their ability to improve performance on a variety of tasks.
The establishment of totipotency during early embryonic development requires the erasure of somatic cell-specific epigenetic marks and the acquisition of pluripotent chromatin states.
We have identified a novel role for the histone methyltransferase MLL2 in controlling gene expression during oocyte maturation and early embryonic development.
Our findings suggest that MLL2 plays an important role in the epigenetic reprogramming of the genome during meiosis, which may be essential for normal development of the embryo.

Our results suggest that the role of MLL2 in early embryonic development may be limited to the establishment of the initial H3K4 methylation pattern, while other H3K4 methyltransferases take over later on.

The bacterial mechanosensitive channel MscL is an archetypal mechanosensor, which senses membrane tension and opens to relieve it when it exceeds a threshold value.

The mechanosensitive channel of large conductance ( MscL) is an archetypical example of a mechanosensor, which responds to membrane tension by undergoing large-scale conformational rearrangements and opening.
We report here the crystal structure of an archaeal mechanosensitive channel protein ( MscL) from Methanosarcina acetivorans in two distinct conformational states.
Our results show how the different parts of the protein work together to open the pore, and they suggest how this process might be regulated by other proteins in the cell.
Cells are able to sense their environment and move towards or away from it, but how they do this is not understood.
Cells move by extending and retracting membrane extensions, called pseudopodia.
We use computer simulations to study how cells move in response to chemical signals and how they form protrusions called pseudopods.
We show that the formation of pseudopodia is not only driven by the internal forces generated by actin polymerization but also by external forces exerted by the substrate.
We have developed a new method to analyze the directionality of cell migration by analyzing the angle between the leading edge and the body of the cell.

We find that the key features of cell migration are the fraction of persistent pseudopodia, the degree of left-right alternation in pseudopodia formation, and the spread in direction of these pseudopodia.

We show here that the CtrA phosphatase, which is required to turn off the master regulator of cell cycle progression, forms an adaptor protein with the ClpXP protease that promotes its own degradation.
We show that phosphorylation of CpdR by RcdA is required for its function as a transcriptional regulator but not for its role in regulating CtrA proteolysis.
We show that this process is mediated by a novel protein complex consisting of two proteins, ClpXP and CtrA, which are both required for the degradation of CtrA.
We show that in two different tasks, participants' decisions are influenced by the probability of an event occurring and not just its absolute frequency.

We show that participants' behavior is consistent with a model in which they update their beliefs about the world by sampling from a set of possible probabilities and then holding those probabilities constant until a new sample is obtained.
Here, we show that human observers use a probabilistic strategy to make decisions about whether or not to switch categories based on the number of times they have seen each category recently.

We found that participants' behavior was best described by a model in which they use a combination of two strategies: one in which they update their belief about the world using a simple average of past experiences, and another in which they update their belief based on the most recent experience.
Shigella flexneri is an enteropathogenic bacterium that causes severe diarrhea and dysentery in humans.
The bacteria enter host cells by a mechanism called macropinocytosis, which is thought to be an endocytic pathway for large particles such as viruses or bacteria.
We also show that the formation of macropinosomes is required for efficient cell-cell fusion and that the small GTPase Rab11 plays an important role in this process.
We show that the bacteria-containing vacuole ( BCV) is formed by the fusion of the Salmonella-containing vacuole with an endosome, which we term the"macropinosome".
This study provides a new model of how Salmonella invades host cells and may help to explain why this pathogen is able to cause disease in humans.
Neuronal synchronization is thought to play a key role in information processing and memory formation, but its underlying mechanisms are not well understood.
However, how this heterogeneity affects information transfer between neurons and brain regions is unknown.

We show that the interplay between delay and frequency leads to a rich variety of dynamical behaviors, including in-phase and anti-phase states, which are associated with different levels of phase coherence between the two populations.
We show that this is possible if the two populations have a specific phase relationship with each other.
Understanding how the face grows is important for understanding normal development, as well as for treating abnormal facial deformities.
Here, we identify the transcription factor FOXO6 as a novel regulator of Hippo signaling in the developing mouse embryo.
We show that FoxO6 is required for normal craniofacial development by regulating Hippo signaling, which controls cell proliferation in this tissue.
Our findings reveal an important role for Hippo signaling in regulating craniofacial development through its regulation of FoxO6 expression.
However, studying aneuploidy is difficult due to its lethal nature and the lack of tools for generating specific aneuploidies.
Here, we describe a new method for inducing aneuploidy in Drosophila melanogaster.
We show that neural stem cells are able to survive for extended periods with abnormal numbers of chromosomes, but eventually undergo rapid changes in chromosome number and structure.
We find that this delay in response is due to the unique properties of neural stem cells and their progeny.
Leptin has been shown to be an important regulator of energy homeostasis and body weight.
We have used molecular dynamics simulations to explore the folding mechanism of leptin and its mutants.
We show that this mechanism is robust and general, and we demonstrate its relevance in vivo by showing that it occurs in a protein complex involved in DNA replication.
We expect this finding will have broad implications for understanding the structural diversity of knots in nature.
The use of coercion in mental health care is controversial.
The introduction of Community Treatment Orders ( CTOs) in England and Wales in 2008 has been controversial.
The main study was called the Oxford Community Treatment Order Evaluation Trial ( OCTET).
We recruited 336 people who had been detained in hospital because of their mental illness to take part in the study.
We found that, although CTOs were perceived to be very important for people with schizophrenia, their use was not always consistent and varied between teams.
The antibiotic septacidin is an example of a rare class of natural products called l-hexapyranose antibiotics.
However, the biosynthetic pathway for this compound has not been elucidated.
We have identified the biosynthetic gene cluster for septacidin, an antibiotic produced by Streptomyces platensis NR-18432, and demonstrated that it is responsible for its production.

We found that the hygromycin B biosynthetic gene cluster contains a gene encoding a protein ( HygP) that catalyzes the conversion of S-7-phosphorylheptitol ( S-7-P), a precursor for both LPS and hygromycin B, to ADP-d-glycero-b-d-altro-heptose, which is then converted by two other proteins encoded in the hygromycin B biosynthetic gene cluster to the b-hydroxyl group-containing sugar moiety of hygromycin B.
We have obtained high-precision sulfur isotope records of pyrite from the Upper Permian-Lower Triassic boundary ( P/LB) section in western Canada and Japan.

This study provides evidence for an early Mesozoic oceanic anoxic event ( EMAO) in the Early Triassic, which may have been caused by the injection of sulfur-rich fluids from the Earth's interior to the oceans.
Rabies is a neglected disease in India and other low- and middle-income countries.

The lack of evidence-based policies for the management of these diseases has been attributed to the lack of high-quality data and/or the failure of researchers to communicate their findings effectively to policymakers.
We reviewed the research output on rabies in India over the last decade ( 2008-2017) and examined how it aligned with the country's National Rabies Control Programme goals.
We found that biomedical research was the dominant type of research in this field, but there is still a need for more research on the social determinants of health, health systems and policy-making.
We conclude that there is a need to develop a research agenda that focuses on the development of effective interventions to prevent human deaths from dog-mediated rabies.
The measles virus ( MeV) causes severe disease in immunocompromised individuals, but also has a high mortality rate among children under five years of age.
We have developed two cell lines of measles virus ( MV), one in which the virus replicates efficiently in lymphocytes and another in epithelial cells.
We found that this mutation was consistently selected during lymphocytic infection, and it mapped to a region of the viral genome known as the phosphoprotein ( P) gene.
We found that this virus produces very little V protein because it edits its own genome to remove the gene encoding for V protein.
In contrast, in epithelial cells, the virus with the V protein is more fit than the one lacking it.

This study provides new insights into how HIV-1 and related viruses might be able to establish persistent infection of CD4+ T cells, which is important for understanding viral pathogenesis and developing novel antiviral therapies.
We identified a set of four MYB transcription factors that are required for suberin biosynthesis in Arabidopsis thaliana.
We show that this is due to the coordinated action of four transcription factors, which are required for the expression of all known suberin biosynthetic genes.
We reviewed all available studies on the performance of high-throughput NIPTs for Down's syndrome screening in pregnancy.

We used computer modelling to explore how different ways of using the test might affect the number of babies who are exposed to RhD antibodies, and therefore at risk of developing haemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN).

We found that the number of women who were at risk of becoming infected with schistosomiasis in these areas was very low, and that the number of women who had been exposed to schistosomes but did not have any symptoms was also low.
The review found that, on average, 1 in 1000 women who received anti-D during pregnancy would have had an adverse reaction to it.

We found that, if all women who are RhD negative were tested for RhD status during pregnancy, then there would be a substantial reduction in the number of women receiving an anti-D injection ( approximately 1/6th).
Cyanobacteria are photosynthetic bacteria that live in diverse habitats, from freshwater lakes to marine environments.
Phototactic bacteria use specialized sensory systems to sense light and change their swimming direction in response.
We show that cyanobacteria have two distinct systems for detecting light and regulating motility.
Astrocytes are known to provide metabolic support for neurons and have been implicated in regulating neuronal excitability through glutamate uptake and release of neurotransmitters.
Here, we show that astrocytes regulate the activity-dependent release of glutamate from neurons by controlling the availability of the neurotransmitter precursor serine.
Electroreception is an important sensory modality in aquatic animals, including sharks, rays, and electric fish.
Electrolocation is an important sensory modality for many aquatic animals, but it has not been studied in detail in any species of fish.
We show that S. macrurus has evolved to increase the number of voltage-gated sodium channels in its electric organ by rapidly transporting them from an intracellular pool to the cell membrane.
The speed at which electrical signals travel through neurons depends on their size and shape.
We use high-resolution 3D imaging to show that axons in the corpus callosum are not straight but instead follow tortuous paths through the tissue.
We show that the diameter of an axon is not constant along its length but varies by up to 20%, which has implications for how fast signals travel through the axon.
We show that this phenomenon is widespread in white matter tracts and affects the estimation of axonal diameters from diffusion MRI data.
We show that, under the current climate policy framework, the world is on track for a 5 degC warming by 2100, which would have devastating consequences for human societies and ecosystems.
Our results show that climate change will have a significant effect on dengue transmission in the future, but that socioeconomic factors are also important.
Herpesviruses are ubiquitous pathogens of humans and animals.
The current commercial vaccines have been unable to control the spread of MDV variants with increased virulence and pathogenicity.
The genetic changes in the virus that allow it to escape from the immune system are unknown.

We show that these viruses are able to overcome the host immune response by acquiring mutations in viral genes involved in the interaction with the host cell, thus allowing them to escape from the immune system.
The shoot apical meristem ( SAM) is the stem cell niche of plants, which maintains the balance between growth and differentiation to ensure plant development.
We previously reported that cyst nematodes deliver a set of secreted effector proteins into host cells to promote infection.
We found that the expression of WOX4 was reduced in a CLV1/CLV2/RPK2 triple mutant, indicating that WOX4 may be regulated by CLAVATA signaling.
This study provides new insights into how the nematodes establish their feeding sites within host plants and may lead to novel strategies for controlling plant-parasitic nematodes.
The aim of this review was to assess whether or not there is evidence for any difference in effectiveness and safety between these drugs.
We found 11 studies with 1, 115 participants.
The review found that citalopram is as effective as other antidepressants for treating depression in people with schizophrenia but has fewer side effects.
Ribosome biogenesis is a highly regulated and complex process in eukaryotic cells.
The assembly of ribosomes is a complex process involving hundreds of factors in yeast.
We have used an unbiased genetic screen to identify proteins required for ribosome assembly and function in human cells.
We used a novel approach based on machine learning and found that 153 human proteins are involved in ribosomal protein synthesis.
We found that the process is conserved in all eukaryotes and prokaryotes, but that there are some differences between them.

We found that the genetic variants associated with schizophrenia are enriched in genes involved in brain development, which may explain why these risk factors have been consistently linked to schizophrenia across multiple studies.
We have identified 11 single nucleotide polymorphisms ( SNPs) within the PARK2 gene regulatory region that are associated with leprosy risk in a North Indian population.
In this study we identified a quantitative trait locus ( QTL) for sporulation efficiency in yeast and found that it contains four genes.
We found that the mutation is located in the promoter region of the RAS2 gene and causes an increase in the expression level of this gene.

The concept of intraspecific competition is central in ecology, but its empirical assessment has been limited by the difficulty of distinguishing between competitive interactions and other factors that may influence population density.
However, it remains unclear how such competition occurs in nature and whether it affects bacterial populations in their natural habitats.
We show that V. fischeri strains from different locations have distinct genetic backgrounds and that these differences affect their ability to colonize the squid host.
We found that these two strains were able to coexist for long periods of time when they grew apart but eliminated each other when grown together.
Horizontal gene transfer ( HGT), or lateral gene transfer, is a process by which genetic information is transferred between organisms other than through vertical inheritance from parent to offspring.
However, the molecular mechanisms underlying this process are poorly understood.
We found that the bacterial genome of this strain is highly unstable, and that it undergoes a transient hypermutable phase during the initial stages of infection.
We found that the increased genetic diversity was associated with improved adaptation to the host environment and enhanced colonization success.
We found that plasmids with the imuABC cassette were more common among rhizobia than other bacteria and that they were enriched in strains that carried symbiotic plasmids.
The proteome is the set of proteins expressed by an organism and represents the sum of all protein functions in the cell.
We show that the growth rate of bacteria is limited by the availability of proteins required for cell division and DNA replication.
We find that cells can increase their growth rate by increasing the fraction of proteins allocated to translation, and that this is achieved at the expense of other cellular processes.
Cot nursing for preterm babies Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether or not cot nursing is better than incubator care for preterm babies.
The review found no evidence from trials to support or refute the use of open cots for preterm babies.

Cot nursing versus incubator care for preterm babies Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether or not cot nursing is better than incubator care for preterm babies (born before 37 weeks' gestation).

The review of trials found no evidence from randomised controlled trials that either approach is superior, although there was some weak evidence that babies in an incubator may have a slightly better outcome than those cared for in a standard cot.
Cot nursing did not result in any significant differences in mean body temperature or time spent crying.
The review authors found only one study, involving 38 babies, which showed a small reduction in weight gain in the first week when cot nursing was used instead of an incubator.
The use of a heated waterbed for cot nursing is as effective as an incubator in maintaining body temperature, but does not have the same effect on growth.
More research is needed to find out if these treatments are safe and effective for people with dementia.
In this study we have used a mathematical model to investigate how different types of interventions could affect the spread of HIV and TB in a population with high prevalence of both diseases.

We show that calcium binding to the adjacent to metal ion-dependent adhesion site ( ADMIDAS) domain of integrin b1 regulates its affinity for ligand by altering the conformation of the ligand-binding headpiece.
We show that the Ca^2+-binding site is located at the interface between the two subunits of the integrin and that it plays an important role in regulating the affinity of the receptor for its ligand.
We show that the two legs of an integrin are not rigidly connected, but instead have considerable flexibility.
Humans and other primates are able to track complex social dynamics involving multiple individuals.

We show that the brain represents social interactions as a function of their emotional content, and that this is reflected in the activity of a network of brain regions involved in emotion processing.
Silicon carbide ( SiC) is an attractive material for quantum computing due to its high carrier mobility and large bandgap, which allows for room temperature operation of quantum devices.

We demonstrate an approach to enhance the light-matter interaction in photonic crystal cavities by using a nanoscale defect cavity with a high-quality factor and a large mode volume, which is achieved through the integration of a single-crystal silicon nanowire on top of a photonic crystal slab.
The crystallization process is ubiquitous in nature, from snowflakes to biological macromolecules.
We show here that the liquid-liquid transition in triphenyl phosphite is coupled to crystal formation.
Histone deacetylase inhibitors ( HDACi) are promising anticancer agents, but their clinical efficacy is limited due to poor pharmacokinetic properties and lack of selectivity for cancer cells.
We show that HDAC6 is required for proper mitosis in human cells and that its inhibition causes defects in spindle formation and chromosome segregation.
The identification of small molecules that selectively inhibit HDAC6 may lead to the development of novel therapeutics for these diseases.
The crystal structure of one such inhibitor bound to HDAC6 reveals how this unique binding mode is achieved and provides insight into the design of future HDAC6-selective inhibitors.
The crystal structures of the HDAC6-trichostatin A complex reveal two distinct binding modes of trichostatin A to HDAC6, one with bidentate zinc chelation and another with monodentate zinc chelation.
Transposable elements ( TEs) are mobile genetic elements that insert themselves into genomes and can cause mutations by disrupting normal gene function.
Transposable elements ( TEs) are mobile genetic elements that have invaded all genomes, including those of plants.
We found that TEs are highly responsive to environmental stimuli, and their transcriptional activity is correlated with the level of stress experienced by the plant.
We find that expression of many of these genes is regulated by the same environmental signals, and we identify several transcription factors that regulate their expression.
We found that the transposable elements are located near genes involved in stress response, suggesting they might act as enhancers for stress responsive gene expression.
We found that transposable elements ( TEs) are a major contributor to natural variation in gene expression, and that they may be involved in adaptation to environmental stresses such as drought.
Argonaute proteins are central components of RNA silencing pathways, which regulate gene expression by small RNAs.
We found that loss of ZLL leads to an increase in AGO1 protein levels and that this is due to reduced degradation of AGO1 by the proteasome.

We show that the conserved Argonaute domains are sufficient for binding of small RNAs but not for their target recognition, whereas the non-conserved N-terminal domain is required for target recognition.
Microglia are the primary immune cells in the central nervous system ( CNS) and play an important role in neuroinflammation.
Microglial cells are the resident immune cells of the central nervous system ( CNS) and play critical roles in brain development and homeostasis.
We identified Tmem119 as a specific marker for microglial cells by analyzing gene expression profiles from single-cell RNA sequencing data.
We believe this resource will be useful to researchers studying microglial biology and function.
Chikungunya fever is an emerging disease caused by Chikungunya virus ( CHIKV), which has spread rapidly throughout the world since its introduction in the Caribbean in 2007.
Chikungunya virus ( CHIKV) is an emerging mosquito-borne viral disease that has spread rapidly throughout the world since its first detection in Tanzania in 1952.
We found that children under 5 years old had lower antibody levels than older children and adults, suggesting that they may be at higher risk for severe disease if exposed to CHIKV again.
We found that the proportion of people with evidence of past exposure to chikungunya virus was higher among older adults.
This study shows that Chikungunya virus has been circulating in Brazil since at least 2008, and it may be present in other countries in Central and South America.

The current standard treatment for exposure to bats in Australia is administration of human rabies immunoglobulin ( HRIG) and rabies vaccination, which aims to achieve high levels of antibodies against rabies virus.
We describe the use of post-exposure prophylaxis ( PEP) in four patients who had been exposed to rabies virus, but did not have any symptoms or signs of disease.
We report two cases of human rabies with different outcomes; one case died after receiving post-exposure prophylaxis ( PEP) treatment and another survived despite having received delayed vaccination.
He was given multiple doses of vaccine before developing antibodies to rabies virus.
The use of serology in this context may help identify those who have not responded to post-exposure prophylaxis ( PEP) and therefore require further treatment.
We show that zyxin is recruited to stress fibers by binding to actin filaments through its LIM domains.
We have previously shown that LIM domains are enriched at sites of mechanical stress in cells, but how they sense and respond to this mechanical signal is unknown.
We find that the LIM domains of both proteins bind to actin filaments under mechanical stress but not when they are relaxed.
The resulting droplets have a stable lipid membrane at their interface and can be used to encapsulate proteins or other molecules.
This work demonstrates that droplet interface bilayer systems can be used to sense airborne molecules and opens up new avenues for sensing applications.
The retina is a highly specialized neural tissue with a unique structure, function, and gene expression pattern.
In this study we used forward genetics to identify genes required for the formation of the Drosophila eye.
We used a mouse model system to identify mutations in 11 genes previously known to be important for normal vision.
We identified several genes associated with the disease phenotype in this study, including a novel gene, SFXN3, which has not been previously linked to human disease.
We show that SFXN3 is required for normal photoreceptor cell survival and function, and that it acts as an important regulator of mitochondrial biogenesis and dynamics.
Supraparticles are self-assembled structures composed of multiple nanoparticles held together by interparticle forces.
We show that the assembly of these proteins is self-limiting and that it occurs through a mechanism in which the protein-protein interaction is balanced by an electrostatic repulsion.
We show that these aggregates self-assemble into a core-shell structure, with the smaller particles forming the shell around a larger particle core.
Gene expression is regulated by complex networks of transcription factors and their target genes, which are often organized in modules.
We show that these responses are mediated by the transcription factor NRT2, which is activated in response to nitrate and controls expression of genes involved in nitrate uptake and assimilation.
We show that high-conflict countries have more extreme ambivalence toward outgroups than low-conflict countries.

We find that extremely peaceful and extremely conflictual countries are less ambivalent than countries in between, suggesting that ambivalence is not necessarily an indicator of low levels of conflict but rather a reflection of the complexity of intergroup relations.
Chagas disease is caused by Trypanosoma cruzi and affects millions of people worldwide.
It is characterized by an initial asymptomatic phase followed by a severe systemic disease with cardiac and gastrointestinal involvement.
The endothelium is activated in response to infection with Trypanosoma cruzi, the etiological agent of Chagas disease, leading to increased expression of adhesion molecules on its surface.
We found that both statins and benznidazole decreased the expression of adhesion molecules on infected endothelial cells through inhibition of the NF-kB signaling pathway.
Our results suggest that statins may be useful in preventing Chagas disease progression.
This work demonstrates the power of directed evolution to create highly enantioselective enzymes for the production of chiral molecules with pharmaceutical relevance.
This study demonstrates that it is possible to use computational methods to design an enzyme that has a native-like structure, function, and dynamics.

This work provides insight into how protein structure and function are related at the molecular level and suggests that future efforts to improve enzyme efficiency should focus on improving structural stability rather than solely on increasing catalytic activity.
We find that vaccination is most beneficial when the probability of infection is high and/or the vaccine is highly effective, but that it may be less valuable in settings where these factors are low.
We found that a vaccine would be cost-effective if it were priced at $10 or less per dose.
Herbicides are widely used for weed control and have led to the rapid evolution of resistant weeds.
We find that genes involved in herbicide detoxification are enriched within these regions and show evidence for positive selection.
We did not find any studies comparing propofol administered by an anaesthesiologist versus another type of doctor.
The review authors recommend that future research should be done using high-quality methods to ensure that the results are valid.
Walking is recommended as an easy way to get more exercise and improve health, but it is not clear how much walking is needed to be beneficial.
We wanted to find out if using a pedometer would help people who are not active to increase their levels of physical activity.
We found that the intervention was acceptable and feasible for participants, but it did not increase physical activity levels at 1 year.
We found that using a pedometer or having a nurse visit at home or receive a letter did not increase physical activity levels more than usual care.
We found that the intervention was acceptable to patients and staff, and it provided good value for money in terms of improving quality of life.

We found that using a pedometer to count steps and receive feedback on progress may be an effective way of increasing physical activity levels, but it is not clear whether or not this approach would work well enough to make a difference at a population level.
The brain is organized into large-scale networks of interconnected regions specialized for processing different types of sensory information.
We examined the functional connectivity between two groups of neurons with very similar response properties, but different spatial locations within the primary visual cortex ( V1).
Here, we show that the macaque intraparietal sulcus contains two distinct patches with similar depth selectivity but different response properties for object shape and size.

We found that these two subsectors were connected to different parts of the brain, with one being connected to areas involved in object recognition and the other to areas involved in spatial navigation.

We find that most PDZ domains are highly selective and do not bind to multiple proteins.
We have used this approach to identify the binding site for a model PDZ domain and show that it is highly specific.
We have developed a computational method for predicting PDZ domain binding partners based on their sequence similarity to known PDZ domains.
We show that these proteins are able to bind to specific PDZ domains of host proteins that play important roles in cell polarity and growth, thereby disrupting normal cellular function.
The retinoblastoma protein ( pRb) is a tumor suppressor that regulates cell proliferation and differentiation by binding to E2F transcription factors.
We show that the p53-dependent regulation of these genes is not conserved between mice and humans.
The faecal occult blood test ( FOBT) is a simple, non-invasive test that involves collecting small samples of stool from the bowel to check for hidden blood.
The faecal occult blood test is used to detect hidden blood in stool, which may be an indicator of bowel cancer.
We searched for all relevant studies up to July 2015.
The review found that FOBT screening is effective at reducing the number of deaths from bowel cancer, but it does not increase the number of people who survive the disease.
Mycoplasma pneumoniae is an important human pathogen and causes respiratory infections in humans, including pneumonia.
We found that the production of hydrogen peroxide by the pathogen is essential for virulence in vivo and that this process is regulated by the transcription factor RpoS.
The lung is an important organ for gas exchange in mammals, but it also serves as a barrier against pathogens and toxins entering the body.
We have identified two proteins in the bacterium Caulobacter crescentus that are required for the proper functioning of the flagellar motor.
We found that GlpQ is required for the production of reactive oxygen species ( ROS) from phospholipids, which are abundant in mammalian cells.

We found that the mutation in the gene encoding the protein Rho1, which is involved in cell wall synthesis and division, causes defects in the formation of the septum, leading to abnormal cell separation.
We found that GlpQ is involved in the regulation of gene expression, and we identified several genes whose expression was affected by the loss of GlpQ.
We found that this protein regulates the expression of genes involved in the metabolism of glucose and other sugars, but not those involved in the metabolism of amino acids or lipids.
Noncoding RNAs ( ncRNAs) are emerging as key regulators in gene expression.
However, distinguishing between coding and noncoding RNAs is difficult because both types of transcripts are produced by similar mechanisms and have similar properties.
The use of fire by early humans is one of the most important technological innovations in human history.
We analyzed 174 hours of conversation between two speakers, one of whom was an elderly hunter-gatherer from southern Africa.
We show that night talk is associated with higher levels of theory of mind, social capital, and trust, and that it predicts the emergence of complex societies.
We show that Ju/'hoan children's ability to learn and remember new words is enhanced by exposure to adult-child interaction in the evening, when adults often tell stories or sing songs.
The protozoan parasite Trichomonas vaginalis causes trichomoniasis, an infection of the urogenital tract in humans.
The disease is caused by infection with Leishmania parasites, which are transmitted through the bite of infected sand flies.
Here, we show that Trichomonas vaginalis, an important human pathogen, produces extracellular vesicles ( EVs) that are morphologically and biochemically similar to mammalian exosomes.
We found that exosomes derived from highly adhesive strains of T. trichiura are able to enhance the adhesion of less adhesive strains to host cells in vitro.
We found that exosomes from different strains of T. trichiura have distinct binding preferences for host cells, and that this is mediated by specific proteins on the surface of the exosome.
These findings provide new insights into how parasites interact with their hosts and each other.
The CALAA-01 nanomedicine platform has been used in multiple clinical trials for treatment of solid tumors with promising results.
We have developed a nanoparticle-based drug delivery system ( CALAA-01) for delivering siRNA to tumors in mice and now tested it in human cancer patients.
The increasing number of prescribed medications in modern medicine makes it essential for clinicians to be able to identify potential adverse drug-drug interactions ( DDIs).
The identification of drug-drug interactions ( DDIs) is an important task in drug development and clinical practice.

We show that drugs with adverse interactions are more likely to have synergistic effects on gene expression, and that these genes are enriched in biological pathways related to cell cycle regulation and DNA repair.
We leverage these features to train a machine learning model that predicts drug-drug interactions with high accuracy.
Our method provides both accurate predictions and interpretable results, which are important for drug development.
Membrane fusion is an essential process for life, but it also plays a role in disease.
We previously showed that VopQ is an inhibitor of the V-type ATPase, which is responsible for acidification of intracellular organelles and vesicles in eukaryotic cells.
Here, we show that VopQ is an inhibitor of vacuolar membrane fusion in yeast and that it acts by binding to the SNARE protein Vam7p.
The unfolded protein response ( UPR) is a cellular stress response that aims to restore homeostasis of the endoplasmic reticulum ( ER), which is responsible for protein folding and secretion.

The unfolded protein response ( UPR) is an essential cellular stress response that senses misfolded proteins in the endoplasmic reticulum ( ER), which are then degraded by proteases called proteasomes.
However, how the ER stress sensor Ire1 senses misfolded proteins has been unclear.

We find that the UPR is able to sense the magnitude of ER stress and modulate the duration of the response accordingly, thereby ensuring that cells respond appropriately to different levels of stress.
We show that BiP binding to Ire1 is required for efficient termination of UPR signaling, and that this interaction is regulated by the redox state of the cell.
Our results suggest that BiP acts as an inhibitor of IRE1a activation by binding to monomeric IRE1a and preventing its self-association.
We show that this mechanism of sensing ER stress is conserved in yeast, but not in plants.
We show that this mechanism is important for maintaining proper levels of active Ire1 in the cell and preventing inappropriate activation of the UPR.
We screened 42, 444 compounds from the GlaxoSmithKline compound library against T. brucei and identified several novel trypanocidal compounds with activity against bloodstream form parasites.
We screened for compounds that inhibit trypanosome growth in vitro and found several compounds with submicromolar activity against Trypanosoma brucei.
We used this information to identify three compounds that had good activity against Trypanosoma brucei rhodesiense parasites in vitro and in vivo.
These findings will be useful in the design of new drugs against this parasite.
Malaria is caused by Plasmodium parasites and results in over one million deaths per year.
The malaria parasite is an important human pathogen and has been studied extensively for decades; however, many aspects of its biology remain unknown.
We have used evolutionary analysis to identify genes that are likely to be important for adaptation, and we show that they are enriched in certain genomic features such as gene length and GC content.
We find that both coding and non-coding regions are under strong selective constraint in all seven species studied, with many conserved non-coding elements identified.
We found that the majority of the genome is under selection, but that most of this selection occurs within coding regions.

We show that the ability of a marine nitrogen-fixing cyanobacterium to adapt to high CO2 is linked to changes in gene expression and protein abundance, which are likely driven by changes in cellular pH.
We found that many of these responses are consistent with genetic assimilation, where the response to selection is mediated by existing variation in gene expression.
This study provides evidence for genetic assimilation in a marine cyanobacterium and demonstrates that this process is likely to be widespread among microbes.
Auditory sensation is mediated by specialized sensory hair cells in the inner ear, which respond to sound vibrations and transmit signals to the brain via electrical synapses with auditory neurons.
BK channels are activated by depolarization and intracellular calcium elevation.
We have investigated the role of two recently discovered accessory proteins, LRRC26 and LRRC52, which are expressed in inner hair cells but not outer hair cells.
Pyrethroids are widely used insecticides for controlling mosquitoes and other insects, but their use has been hampered by the emergence of pyrethroid-resistant mosquito populations.
The mechanism of resistance to this drug is unknown and has not been fully elucidated.
We found that the expression of several cytochrome P450 enzymes ( CYPs) is increased in resistant parasites and that these CYPs are likely involved in the metabolism of the drug.
We identified four cytochrome P450 genes ( CYP6BB2, CYP6Z8, CYP9M5 and CYP9M6) that are up-regulated in both susceptible and resistant strains of Aedes aegypti mosquitoes.
We found that the expression levels of several cytochrome P450 genes were altered in resistant strains, but only one gene, CYP6BB2, was associated with resistance.
We found that the expression of these genes was regulated by a single transcriptional regulator.
The increasing demand for seafood has led to an increase in seafood trade globally, but also raises concerns about food safety and sustainability.
Seafood fraud, including mislabeling of species, is widespread in global markets.
We found that mislabeling is widespread in the United States, with many people labeling themselves as having health conditions they do not have or denying having conditions they actually have.

Our results suggest that the EPYC1 protein is likely to be involved in the assembly and maintenance of the Rubisco holoenzyme, possibly by forming a scaffold for the binding of other proteins.
Our results provide insights into how pyrenoids are assembled and may help engineer more efficient photosynthetic organisms.
During meiosis, homologous recombination is required to ensure proper chromosome segregation by generating crossovers between homologs.

We show that this function of Mlh3 is not dependent on either the presence or absence of the meiotic recombination machinery, and that it does not require the presence of the synaptonemal complex ( SC).
Polycomb repressive complexes are epigenetic regulators that maintain gene silencing in many organisms and tissues.

We find that many of these regions are located at the boundaries between different chromatin domains, and we show that they are enriched for specific histone modifications.

Our results suggest that the loss of long-range interactions during mitosis is important for the release of Polycomb proteins from most genes, but not all, and that this process may be important for proper gene expression patterns in daughter cells.

Potato spindle tuber viroid ( PSTVd) is a plant pathogenic RNA virus that causes severe disease symptoms on potato plants.

We found that the expression of StTCP23 is downregulated by PSTVd infection, which may be due to the complementary sequences between the 3'UTR of StTCP23 and the viral RNA.
We found that StTCP23 is targeted by two miRNA-like small RNAs, which are derived from the viral genome and are complementary to the StTCP23 mRNA sequence.

We found that expression of these small RNAs in transgenic potato plants caused disease symptoms similar to those observed in virus infected plants, and we identified several genes whose expression was altered by the presence of these small RNAs.
We found that StTCP23 is required for normal tuber formation by regulating gibberellin ( GA) biosynthesis and signaling pathways.
Our study provides new insights into how virus infection affects host gene expression and leads to disease symptoms in plants.

Dengue is an important public health problem in tropical and subtropical regions of the world.
The Wolbachia bacteria are being tested for their ability to block transmission of dengue virus ( DENV) by mosquitoes.

However, current methods to evaluate the efficacy of Wolbachia are limited by their reliance on the detection of Wolbachia-infected mosquitoes and/or the presence of Wolbachia DNA in mosquito tissues.
In this study, we show that Wolbachia infection does not affect the ability of Aedes aegypti to transmit dengue virus ( DENV) when fed on an infected blood meal.
We found that wMel infection significantly reduced the frequency of dengue transmission from infected mosquitoes to humans by reducing the amount of virus present in the mosquito's saliva.
We found that the reduced saliva production is due to a decrease in the number of salivary glands, which are responsible for producing mosquito saliva.

These results provide new insights into how Wolbachia affects the mosquito, and may help to explain why Wolbachia has been shown to be effective at blocking dengue transmission in some field trials but not others.
The spike protein of SARS-CoV-2 contains an N-terminal domain ( NTD) and a C-terminal domain ( CT).
Neutralizing antibodies have been developed against SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, but their clinical efficacy is still unclear.
However, the use of antibodies is limited by their low potency in vivo.
We have identified a nanobody that binds with high affinity to the RBD of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein and show that it improves the binding and neutralizing activity of antibodies against SARS-CoV-2.
We show that SR31 is an inhibitor of SARS-CoV-2 infection in vitro, and that it acts by binding to the viral spike protein ( S) at a region that is conserved across all known coronaviruses.
We show that fusion of these antibodies with a small antibody fragment ( sybody) significantly enhances binding and neutralization of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein.
We present a method for improving the affinity of nanobodies by introducing a single mutation in the antigen-binding loop, which is a common feature among antibody fragments.

We found that the prevalence of Toxoplasma gondii infection among people living in rural areas is higher than in urban areas, and that the risk of acquiring this parasite is greater for those who live in rural areas.
In this study, we report on the prevalence of Toxoplasma gondii infection among pregnant women and children from the Kyrgyz Republic ( Central Asia).

We found that approximately 1 in 1000 pregnant women in Kyrgyzstan are infected with Toxoplasma gondii, and that about 173 children are born each year to mothers who have acquired infection during pregnancy.
The prevalence of Toxoplasma gondii infection in HIV-infected individuals is high, but little is known about the clinical significance of this co-infection.
We found that congenital and AIDS-related toxoplasmosis is an important cause of illness and death in Kyrgyzstan.
In this study we evaluated the susceptibility of three bat species from Brazil to dengue virus ( DENV).
We found that the majority of dengue virus ( DENV) infections were detected in wild-caught Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, but also in other mosquito species and in bats.
We also detected dengue virus in only three of the mosquitoes collected, but no bats were found to be infected with dengue virus.
We found that dengue virus was present in the saliva of Culex quinquefasciatus, but not in Aedes aegypti and Anopheles albopictus mosquitoes.
We found no evidence of dengue virus replication or transmission to humans from bats in these urban areas.

The legume-rhizobia symbiosis is one of the most important nitrogen-fixing systems in nature, but it has been difficult to engineer for use as a tool for bioremediation.
The symbiotic bacteria are able to recognize their host plant and infect it in a highly specific manner.
However, little is known about how this fungus infects its host plant.
We have investigated how the symbiont interacts with its host plant, Lotus japonicus, during the early stages of nodule formation.
Here we show that the symbiotic bacterium Mesorhizobium loti is able to sense the presence of its host plant and to move towards it in order to establish a symbiosis.

The ability of Toxoplasma gondii to infect and replicate within macrophages is essential for its survival and transmission.
The malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum expresses a large repertoire of variant surface antigens, called PfEMP1s, which are encoded by multiple var genes in the genome.
We found that parasite-infected erythrocytes preferentially bind to lung, bone marrow, and heart endothelium in vivo, and that these endothelial cells express specific subsets of PfEMP1 proteins.
We also show that these proteins are able to bind to human endothelial cells in vitro and in vivo, suggesting that they may be important for parasite entry into the brain.
These results suggest that parasites causing severe disease are able to bind to different types of human cells in the body than those causing uncomplicated infections.
This study shows that, contrary to the prevailing view, phosphocreatine kinase is not essential for the maintenance of ATP levels during exercise.
We show that CK has an important role in maintaining cellular energy homeostasis by regulating the concentration of ATP and ADP, which are essential for many cellular functions.
We developed a mathematical model to analyze how the heart muscle contracts and relaxes in time.
We used a combination of experimental measurements and mathematical modeling to study how the mitochondrial respiratory chain responds to changes in oxygen supply during ischemia-reperfusion injury.
We used computer models to predict how much energy is transferred from mitochondria to the rest of the cell in different conditions.
We show that CK is essential for maintaining cellular ATP homeostasis, and that it does so by buffering fluctuations in mitochondrial ATP production.

Here, we use scanning photocurrent microscopy to image the electronic structure of a Weyl semimetal TaIrTe4 in real space with submicrometer resolution.
We show how symmetry analysis of the photocurrent map allows us to distinguish between surface and bulk contributions, which is not possible with conventional methods.
We show that these currents are due to nonlinear transport properties, which are forbidden in the bulk but emerge at the surface.
We show that the surface of this material exhibits a unique electronic structure with a strong resonance feature not present in the bulk.

We searched for all studies that evaluated non-drug treatments for pain after spinal cord injury, including acupuncture, massage, transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS), and others.

We found eight studies investigating the use of electrical brain stimulation, which used either transcranial direct current stimulation ( tDCS), cranial electrotherapy stimulation ( CES) or repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation ( rTMS).
The quality of evidence for each outcome was low to moderate.
The studies were not well designed, and their results may be biased.

The results showed that there is moderate-quality evidence for the efficacy of tDCS in reducing pain, with a mean reduction of 1.9 points on a zero-to-10 scale at both short-term and mid-term follow-up.
The review found that exercise is effective at reducing pain, improving function and increasing the number of people who are able to use their arm normally after surgery.

We found no evidence to support the use of repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS), cranial electrostimulation (CES), acupuncture, self-hypnosis, transcutaneous electrical nerve level (TENS) or cognitive behavioural therapy for the treatment of neuropathic pain in adults.
However, there was not enough information about the effects of these treatments on anxiety, depression or quality of life to draw any conclusions.
There was no evidence of benefit from other non-drug treatments, such as acupuncture or massage therapy.
More research is needed to find out which non-drug treatments are effective for people with cancer pain.

We have developed an experimental system to study how the T cell receptor ( TCR) senses antigenic peptides presented by MHC molecules on the surface of infected cells.
We show that ANXA5 self-organizes into a 2D matrix and that this organization is required for efficient cross-linking of TCRs.
We show that fusion of annexin A5 ( ANXA5) to MHC class I molecules dramatically enhances their ability to activate T cells and break tolerance to self-antigens.
Study characteristics The evidence is current to: 11 February 2019.

We found five randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups), three non-randomised controlled trials and four non-randomised observational studies comparing Aquablation versus other treatments for men with moderate to severe symptoms of enlarged prostate.

The aim of this review was to find out whether or not there is evidence for the effectiveness of these treatments in reducing pain, improving function and quality of life at both short- and long-term follow-up (up to 24 months).

The study compared two different types of transurethral resection, which is a surgical procedure where the excess prostate tissue is removed from the urethra using an electrically activated cutting loop (electrosurgical resection) or water jet (Aquablation).

Quality of the evidence The quality of the evidence was low for all outcomes due to small numbers of participants and studies, and because there were no randomised controlled trials comparing aquablation with transurethral resection of prostate (TURP) or laser enucleation of prostate (LEP), which are other common treatments for LUTS/BPH.

We found that aquablation is probably more effective than laser or transurethral resection for treating BPH symptoms and reducing urine flow restriction, but it may not be as good at improving urinary bother.
The review found only one randomised controlled trial, which included 1000 men with an average age of 68 years and prostate volumes less than or equal to 80 mL.
More research is needed to determine how effective Aquablation is compared to other treatments, such as medication or surgery.

Synaptic scaling, which involves changes in the number and/or size of postsynaptic glutamate receptors ( AMPARs), is an important form of neuronal plasticity that has been implicated in learning and memory.

Our results suggest that this pathway is important for the proper functioning of the sensory neurons and may be involved in the regulation of other genes that are required for normal neuronal function.
We show that this negative feedback is mediated by the conserved GSK-3 kinase, which phosphorylates and thereby inhibits the activity of the transcription factor CMK-1.
Our results suggest that this negative feedback loop is important for maintaining proper levels of GLR-1 expression and function, and may be conserved in other neurons.
Anopheles mosquitoes are the primary vectors for malaria transmission, but their immune responses to Plasmodium parasites have been poorly characterized.
We found that Dasr1 knockout mosquitoes have an increased bacterial load in their gut and an altered gut microbiota.
We found that the changes in the gut microbiota of mosquitoes caused by the infection with Wolbachia altered the mosquito's ability to transmit dengue virus.

We found that the mosquito's microbiome is required for the development of Plasmodium falciparum sporozoites in the mosquito midgut, but not for the transmission of parasites from the mosquito to the mammalian host.
We also found that loss of lrim1 affects male fertility and reduces sperm transfer to females.
The grapevine leafhopper, Scaphoideus titanus, is the vector of Flavescence doree virus ( FDV), which causes severe damage to grapevines in Europe and Asia.
We found that the ability of S. titanus to infect grapevines is associated with specific vmp genes and their encoded proteins.

The circadian clock is an endogenous timekeeping system that regulates many physiological processes in mammals.

The study compared the effects of aspirin and warfarin on the risk of stroke in people with atrial fibrillation who had a recent stroke or transient ischaemic attack.
Warfarin is an anticoagulant drug that has been used for decades to prevent blood clots in people with atrial fibrillation.
Multidrug resistance is an increasing problem in hospitals, where patients are often exposed to multiple antibiotics during their stay.
The increasing prevalence of multidrug-resistant organisms ( MDR) in healthcare settings is a major public health concern, as they are associated with increased morbidity, mortality, and cost.
The increasing use of antibiotics has led to the emergence of multidrug-resistant organisms ( MDRs) and the need for new antimicrobial agents.

We found that patients who had been exposed to antibiotics were more likely to be colonized with resistant bacteria, and those who were colonized with one type of resistant bacterium were more likely to have others as well.

Genome-wide association studies have identified numerous loci associated with autism spectrum disorder ( ASD), but the majority of these variants are located in non-coding regions, making it difficult to identify the causal gene( s).
In this study, we used a large sample of individuals with no ASD diagnosis to test whether genetic variants associated with ASD also influence normal brain development and function.

We found that genetic variants associated with schizophrenia are also associated with cognitive performance in healthy individuals, and that these associations persist even when accounting for other psychiatric disorders, brain imaging phenotypes, and educational attainment.
We found evidence for genetic overlap between ASD risk and measures of social interaction, suggesting that these traits may be part of the same underlying biological pathway.
Tremors are abnormal movements caused by involuntary muscle contractions, which occur in many people with MS.
The review found that there was not enough evidence from randomised controlled trials to support any particular treatment for this condition.

We show that this occurs when the replication machinery encounters a strong rDNA replication fork barrier ( RFB), which causes the formation of a DNA loop that blocks the progression of the replisome.

However, it is unclear how this process works at the level of individual neurons.
We show that this network is able to learn and represent complex probability distributions over time, and we demonstrate how it could be used in a simple learning task.
This work demonstrates how the brain's intrinsic dynamics may be exploited to perform complex computations in a highly efficient way.
We recruited 655 people who had asthma and low quality of life despite their medication.
The study found that people who received the intervention had better quality of life than those in the control group.
We assessed the effects of the programme on participants' health-related quality of life ( QoL) and their use of healthcare services over time.
The results showed that the DVD improved knowledge and attitudes towards diabetes, but not behaviour ( physical activity) or health outcomes ( blood pressure).

We found that these breathing retraining techniques are not effective for improving quality of life or reducing hospital admissions in people who have had an exacerbation but they do improve quality of life and reduce healthcare costs in people whose asthma is poorly controlled despite medication.

Wildfires in the United States have become increasingly destructive over the past few decades, with more than half of all wildfires occurring at the wildland-urban interface ( WUI), where human development meets natural ecosystems.

We found that the number of houses built within the wildland-urban interface increased dramatically over the past two decades, with more than half of all new housing units being built in areas at high risk for wildfires.
We show that urban areas are growing faster than any other land use type in the United States, with 1.

Key results We found no differences in outcomes of ocular alignment, stereoacuity, or sound eye vision.

The review authors found that atropine is as effective as conventional occlusion in improving vision and reducing glare disability.
The INDETERMINATE DOMAINS ( IDD) /BIRD proteins are a large family of transcriptional regulators in plants, but their functions have not been well characterized.

We found that the phosphorylation of IDD4 is required for its function in plant immunity against bacterial infection.
We conclude that IDD4 is an important regulator of plant defense responses to pathogens by modulating hormone signaling pathways.

Cachexia is a condition of severe weight loss and muscle wasting, which affects many people with cancer.
EPA is an omega-6 fatty acid, which has been shown to have anti-inflammatory properties.

Study characteristics We searched for studies assessing the effect of oral EPA on the symptoms of cancer-related cachexia, which include loss of appetite, low mood, tiredness, weakness, restlessness, constipation, nausea and vomiting, and weight loss.
The review found no evidence of benefit for oral EPA in reducing pain, improving quality of life or reducing the need for analgesia.
However, there was some evidence that it may improve the symptoms and signs of cancer-related loss of weight and muscle mass when used together with megestrol acetate (a drug to stimulate appetite).

In this paper, we describe how to use these methods on an example model of HIV transmission in Uganda.
