Aripiprazole for treating mania Review question We reviewed the evidence for the effectiveness of aripiprazole (an antipsychotic medicine) in treating mania.
The review also looked at how well people tolerated the treatment and whether or not it had any side effects.
The review authors found seven trials comparing aripiprazole with placebo, and one trial comparing aripiprazole with lithium or haloperidol.
The other two trials compared aripiprazole with placebo, and one trial compared aripiprazole with haloperidol.
The studies were not well designed, and there was a high dropout rate ( > 20%) from most interventions.

Aripiprazole was also better than placebo for reducing depressive symptoms in children and adolescents after two weeks of treatment (Children's Depression Rating Scale; mean difference (MD) -6.29, 95% CI -8.19 to -4.39).

The review found no evidence for any significant difference between aripiprazole and placebo on the frequency of adverse events, but there were differences in the types of adverse events experienced by those taking aripiprazole compared to those taking placebo.
The review found no evidence to support the use of haloperidol over aripiprazole for schizophrenia.

The review authors found no completed randomised controlled trials to include in this review.
Study characteristics We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of adults who were given either a statin or placebo before having an operation on blood vessels (arteries).
The review authors found 11 studies, with a total of 1, 111 participants who received either antibiotics or placebo (a substance which contains no medication) before and/or after surgery.

The number of deaths from any cause was similar between groups at 12 months (14/105 (13.3%) with statins and 16/73 (21.9%) with placebo) but there were fewer deaths due to heart disease (4/105 (3.8%) with statins and 7/73 (9.6%) with placebo).

The brain is able to represent spatial information in a compact way, using a grid-like representation.

We show that, in addition to atmospheric deposition, mercury also enters the ocean through terrestrial runoff and sediment transport.

We show that the current estimate of the global terrestrial GEM sink is likely too low, and that it could be as large as 1-2 Tg yr^-1.

Our results suggest that terrestrial ecosystems are an important source of mercury to surface waters, and that the magnitude of this flux is comparable to other known sources such as atmospheric deposition.
Body-worn cameras ( BWCs) are increasingly being used by law enforcement agencies across the United States in an effort to increase transparency and accountability.

Our results suggest that targeting ERBB3 may be an effective strategy for treating colorectal cancer.

The results showed that the method was effective for identifying ticks collected from cattle and dogs but not as good when they were engorged or preserved in ethanol.
This work demonstrates the potential use of MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry to identify sand flies collected from the field and provides an alternative method for identifying sand fly species.

The use of immunosuppressive drugs to prevent rejection in patients with a transplanted heart Review question We reviewed the evidence about the effects of immunosuppressive drugs (drugs that suppress the immune system) on survival, adverse events and quality of life in people who have received a heart transplant.
The use of immunosuppressive drugs to prevent rejection of the donor heart has been a major challenge in cardiac transplantation.
The aim of this review was to find out which drugs are the best at preventing rejection in people who have had a heart transplant.

The aim of this review was to compare the effects of using immunosuppressive drugs (such as anti-T-cell monoclonal antibodies) versus other types of immunosuppressants in people who have received a heart transplant.
We wanted to find out if there was evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCTs) to support the use of anti-T-cell antibodies in heart transplant patients.

Antibiotics for treating gonorrhoea in non-pregnant people Gonorrhoea is a bacterial infection caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae.

Gentamicin is another antibiotic used for treating gonorrhoea, but it has not been tested in this way before.
The study compared two antibiotics, one given by injection and the other by mouth.

The study found no evidence that gentamicin was better than ceftriaxone in terms of cure rates, but there were too few participants to be sure about this.
The antibiotic treatment did not affect the rate of clearance of gonorrhoea from the throat or anus, but it reduced the number of people who had gonorrhoea in their urine.
The study found that people who received gentamicin had more pain and swelling at the injection site, but there were no differences in other adverse events between groups.

We show here that, in contrast to previous reports, hydrogen atoms do not remain trapped within the metal lattice but instead diffuse rapidly through the surface and migrate to other metals with weaker binding energies.
We show that this process is driven by the formation of weakly bound hydrogen atoms on the palladium surface, which are able to diffuse across the interface and bind to the silver surface.
We show that the reaction is driven by the interplay between the palladium and silver interfaces, which are in contact over a large area.

IRBIT is an important regulator of cellular functions such as calcium signaling, ion transport, RNA splicing, and cell proliferation.
The molecular mechanisms underlying the regulation of IRBIT activity are not well understood.
Here, we report that IRBIT is expressed in the brain and has two isoforms.

Our study provides insights into the molecular mechanism underlying the functional diversification of the IRBIT family, which may be useful for understanding the physiological functions of the IRBIT family in vivo.
Ribosomes are essential cellular organelles responsible for protein synthesis and are composed of hundreds of proteins encoded by ribosomal protein-encoding genes ( RPGs).

In this study, we show that many yeast introns are not required for efficient translation of their host genes and instead act as negative regulators of gene expression.
We found that the two genes are regulated by a single regulatory element in each genome, but this regulation is mediated by different RNA molecules.

Our results suggest that the mechanism of translational control of ribosomal protein synthesis is conserved across kingdoms and that it may be an important means of regulating gene expression in higher organisms.
Chagas disease is caused by Trypanosoma cruzi, a protozoan parasite transmitted to humans through insect vectors or blood transfusion.
Currently, there are no drugs available for treatment of this disease.
We identified three compounds with high activity against T. cruzi and low cytotoxicity on human cells.
The results presented here show that each compound has a distinct mode of action against T. cruzi, and therefore may be used in combination to improve treatment efficacy.
The heart is an organ with limited regenerative capacity, which makes it difficult to treat myocardial infarction ( MI) or other forms of cardiac injury.
We found that b-adrenergic receptor ( bAR) signaling in immune cells is required for efficient recruitment of macrophages and neutrophils to the injured heart, but not for their activation.
Our findings provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying cardiac inflammation and may lead to novel therapeutic strategies for treating myocardial infarction.
In this study we evaluated the impact of the national program on the prevalence of Trypanosoma cruzi infection in school children from Santa Cruz de la Sierra, Bolivia.
We found that the prevalence of T. cruzi infection among school-age children in these communities is high and that the use of benznidazol has no effect on the prevalence of T. cruzi infection.

The SARS-CoV-2 virus replicates within double-membrane vesicles ( DMVs) formed by the endoplasmic reticulum ( ER).

We found that GBF1 is required for efficient MHV replication and that its depletion leads to accumulation of misfolded proteins in the ER.

We demonstrate its application on real datasets of gene expression in mouse and yeast, and show that it is able to identify genes with similar functions even when they are not co-expressed.

We also modelled how many people would need to be tested for each of these interventions in order to identify one case of undiagnosed infection, and we compared the cost-effectiveness of different testing strategies.
We found that even small changes to current spending patterns, such as increasing funding for treatment or improving access to diagnostic tests, would lead to large improvements in health outcomes.
Cataracts are one of the leading causes of blindness worldwide, and there is no effective treatment available for this condition.
We found that mutations in RRAGA, which encodes for the RagA GTPase, cause human congenital cataract formation.
In this study, we used whole exome sequencing to identify a novel mutation in the RRAGA gene in a family with early onset cataract.
We also identified two novel mutations in the RRAGA gene in two unrelated patients with congenital lens opacity.
Our results suggest that the mutation disrupts normal regulation of protein synthesis and cell growth, leading to the development of cataract.

Height is a classic example of a polygenic trait, with hundreds of genes contributing to its variation in humans.
Genome-wide association studies ( GWAS) have identified hundreds of loci associated with height in European populations but have not yet fully explained the heritability of height variation.
We have analyzed data from 35, 000 individuals to identify genes that contribute to inbreeding depression for height.

We show that the average height of children born to first cousin parents is significantly lower than that of children born to unrelated parents, even when we control for other factors such as parental social class.

Influenza A virus ( IAV) is an important human pathogen and a major cause of seasonal epidemics and pandemics.
We have previously shown that IFITM3 restricts infection by several viruses including Ebola virus and influenza A virus but not Lassa fever virus ( LASV).
We show that IFITM3 is trafficked to the plasma membrane, where it blocks viral entry by inhibiting fusion between the virus and host membranes.
We found that IAV, but not LASV, is trapped in IFITM3-containing vesicles and undergoes hemifusion with the vesicle membrane.
We found that Lassa virus utilizes an alternative endocytic pathway to enter cells, which is not restricted by IFITM3.
Our data suggest that IFITM3 acts as an inhibitor of influenza A virus infection by trapping the virus at the site of cell entry in a hemifused state.
Infection with helminths is associated with an increased risk of developing active tuberculosis ( TB), but the mechanisms are not well understood.
In this study, we examined the prevalence of intestinal worm infections ( STH) among tuberculosis ( TB) patients and their household contacts in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania.
We found that S. mansoni infection is associated with reduced lung function and increased airway inflammation in children.
We found that patients with more severe disease were less likely to be treated with antibiotics, had more cavitations in their lungs, and had worse outcomes.
Chandipura virus ( CHPV) is an emerging zoonotic pathogen of bats and rodents in India.

Breastfeeding is an important method for birth control, especially in low-income countries where access to modern contraception may be limited.

Our results suggest that, in addition to its well-known effects on infant health, breastfeeding may also have an important role in controlling human fertility.

Juries are an important part of democratic societies, but their functioning has been studied mainly in terms of how they make decisions about guilt or innocence.
We use a novel method to analyze the language used by judges and lawyers during these trials, revealing a shift in their discourse over time.
Our results suggest that the legal system has been moving toward clearer distinctions between violent and nonviolent crimes over time.
Our results provide a quantitative measure of the relationship between the rise of the modern state and the decline of violence.

The treatment of choice is to immobilise the wrist in a plaster cast for 6-8 weeks.
External fixation is a technique used to fix broken bones in the lower limbs (legs) and upper limbs (arms).

The quality of evidence was low to moderate, with only one study rated as having high-quality evidence.
We found that people who used decision aids had more knowledge about their options than those who did not use them, and were better able to recall the information they had been given.

Beta-blockers are a class of medications used to treat high blood pressure, heart failure, angina pectoris (chest pain), arrhythmias (abnormal heart rhythm) and anxiety.

The aim of this review was to assess whether beta-blockers are effective at reducing death, hospital admissions for heart failure, and other serious complications of heart failure such as stroke, arrhythmias, and bleeding.
Beta-blockers are drugs that reduce blood pressure by decreasing heart rate and reducing the amount of blood pumped through the arteries.
Study characteristics We included 11 randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) published up to 6 July 2015.
Beta-blockers are commonly used as the first drug treatment for high blood pressure.

Beta-blockers were associated with more cardiovascular events than calcium channel blockers and ACE-inhibitors, but not diuretics.

The review found that azelaic acid is as good as low concentration of hydroquinone and better than high concentration of hydroquinone for treating melasma.
The trials were not long enough to assess any side effects from the treatments, but there was no evidence of any severe adverse events associated with tretinoin or hydroquinone.
However, there was no evidence of any serious side effects or allergic reactions to the treatment.
More research is needed to find out how long the effects last and whether it should be used for longer than six months.

The microbial community is an important part of our environment and plays a key role in many biological processes such as decomposition, nutrient cycling, and disease prevention.
The use of multiple strains in microbial cell factories has been shown to be an effective strategy to improve productivity and yield of products such as biofuels and pharmaceuticals.
We show how this strategy can be used to overcome thermodynamic constraints in metabolic pathways, which are often a major barrier to engineering new biochemical functions.
The method is implemented as a software tool and allows to analyze metabolic networks from different organisms under various conditions.
We found that there are many products in which a multi-strain community is more efficient than a single strain.

We compared three different types of hernia repair using either a synthetic mesh or a biological graft or a mesh kit to see how well they worked, whether there were any complications and what the impact was on patients' quality of life.

The review found that there is insufficient evidence to show whether or not surgery for uterine or vault prolapse reduces the risk of further prolapse, but it does reduce the symptoms and complications associated with prolapse.

The review found that anterior and posterior repairs are equally effective at preventing hernia recurrence (the reoccurrence of the bulge) but that mesh is not better than non-mesh techniques for this purpose.

The study found that there were no clear differences between the two treatments in terms of pain relief or quality of life.
In women who had a previous repair operation, we found some evidence to suggest that using mesh may be better at repairing prolapse than not using mesh but this result is uncertain.

Mosquitoes are the primary vectors for many important human pathogens including West Nile virus ( WNV), dengue virus ( DENV) and Zika virus ( ZIKV).

In this study, we show that the virus particles produced from insect cells are different to those produced by mammalian cells and that these differences affect the ability of the virus to infect human cells.
We found that the virus produced by insect cells is different from the virus produced by mammalian cells in terms of its glycoprotein composition and lipid content.
In this study we show that viruses grown in mosquito cells are more infectious than those grown in mammalian cells and that they have an increased ability to infect mosquitoes.
However, there are no reports on the production of infectious virus-like particles ( VLPs) from DENV replicons in insect cells.
We developed an efficient method for producing WNV replicons in mosquito cells, which allowed us to study the role of mosquito factors on WNV replication.

We show that Tax-induced senescence requires activation of the IKK/NF-kB pathway, which is known to be activated by Tax.
We also found that the HTLV-1 anti-sense protein, HBZ, delays or prevents Tax-induced senescence.
Our results suggest that the balance between Tax and HBZ is critical for determining the fate of HTLV-1-infected cells, and may be an important factor in the development of ATL.

Our method is able to identify and classify various types of plant stressors including bacterial and fungal infections as well as chemical injuries and nutrient deficiencies.
Biofilms are complex microbial communities that play an important role in many natural environments and human diseases.

The IQCODE is a screening tool that asks questions about an individual's daily functioning, such as their ability to manage finances or use household appliances.
The aim of this study was to find out how well the IQCODE tool works in identifying people with dementia.
We found no evidence to support or refute the use of antidepressants for depression in general practice settings.
The quality of the studies was low to moderate and the results were not consistent with each other.

The studies did not report on the number of people who dropped out early from treatment due to adverse events, or whether there were any differences between groups in terms of quality of life, hospital admissions, or death rates.
We found only one study, which was small and had some methodological problems.
The results showed that it was possible to use the IQCODE in general practice but that more work needs to be done to make sure that doctors and nurses are trained to do this well.

The relationship between parental separation and child outcomes is complex, with some studies showing that children of separated families do less well in school than those from intact families, while others show no difference or even an advantage for children from separated families.

Using data from the National Longitudinal Study of Adolescent Health, we show that parental divorce has a large and persistent impact on children's educational attainment, but only for those who are not at risk of divorce.
Children of parents who are not married but living together have poorer outcomes than those whose parents are married.
We show that this is not true for children whose parents are at risk of divorce.

We show that the brain regions involved in processing familiar faces contain information about the identity of specific individuals known to an individual observer.
We found that, when we interacted with two people in succession, our brain activity reflected not only the visual features of each person but also the commonalities between them.

The review authors found no evidence to support the use of SAMe as an antidepressant drug for people with major depression.

The review authors searched for all relevant randomised controlled trials (RCTs) in which participants were randomly allocated to receive either a combination of antibiotics and antifungal drugs or just antibiotics.
The studies did not show a clear difference between SAMe and escitalopram on the number of people who dropped out early from treatment for any reason.
The studies did not show a significant difference between SAMe and placebo when used with antidepressants for people with major depression.
The review authors found no evidence to support the use of SAMe as an antidepressant drug in people with depression.

Hepatitis B virus ( HBV) is a major human pathogen and a model system for studying viral replication in vivo.

We found that PD-1 expression is induced on virus-specific CD8+ T cells during chronic infection and that this contributes to their dysfunction.
This study provides insight into how exhausted T cells are maintained in chronic infection, and may help us develop strategies to restore their function.
Salmonella Typhi ( S. Typhi) causes typhoid fever, an infectious disease of humans caused by Salmonella enterica serovar Typhi.
We have identified two genes in Salmonella that are responsible for adding glucose to the lipopolysaccharide ( LPS) of Salmonella, which is an important virulence factor.
We found that Salmonella has multiple genes encoding GTR proteins, which are organized in clusters ( gtr operons) that encode different types of glycosyltransferases.
We found that the expression of genes encoding these proteins is regulated by two global regulators, Dam and OxyR, which are known to regulate many other genes in Salmonella.
This study demonstrates that the Gtr system plays an important role in generating phenotypic diversity among Salmonella strains.
These findings provide new insights into the pathogenesis of this disease and may help guide future vaccine development efforts.

We show that these motifs are not only found in protein-coding regions but also in noncoding RNAs, suggesting that they play an important role in gene regulation.
We demonstrate how this approach can be applied to design new RNA molecules with specific folding behaviors.
In this study we used an ecological approach to investigate the association between diarrheal disease incidence and climate and socio-demographic variables in northern Lao PDR.
Our results show that households in rural areas are more likely to use contaminated water sources and that these may pose a greater health risk than municipal supplies.
In this study, we used a combination of field measurements and modeling to investigate the relationship between groundwater levels and diarrhea incidence in rural Bangladesh.
The results are useful for the design of an early warning system to predict the occurrence of outbreaks, which will help to reduce the number of deaths due to dengue fever.
Visceral leishmaniasis ( VL) is a neglected tropical disease caused by Leishmania donovani parasites.
In this study, we have evaluated the antileishmanial activity of Cinnamomum cassia against Leishmania donovani, L. infantum and L. major.
We found that CBD is devoid of any toxic effects and has no significant side effects in mice.
This study provides evidence for the potential use of C. cassia bark as an alternative to conventional drugs in the treatment of leishmaniasis.

The shoot apical meristem is the plant stem cell niche and the site where all above-ground organs are formed.
In this study, we use a combination of mathematical modeling and experiments to investigate how auxin transport is regulated in Arabidopsis thaliana leaves.

Our results show that the model is able to reproduce many features of auxin transport in Arabidopsis thaliana, including the existence of two distinct transport systems and the presence of a feedback loop between the two systems.
Our model reproduces many features of the plant's growth pattern and allows us to identify the key factors involved in this process.
We also show how the initial network of veins is connected to the rest of the vascular system.
We found that older people who are most likely to have an ADR are those with certain health problems, such as diabetes or dementia, and those who use multiple medications.

We also found that the electronic version of the Frailty Index was not as good at identifying people who are frail, but it is still better than using no information about frailty at all.
The immune system has evolved to maintain T cells at a constant number, and this process is known as T cell homeostasis.
Here we show that the JAK1/2 inhibitor tofacitinib blocks HIV-1 infection of resting CD4+ T cells by inhibiting viral entry and blocking the establishment of latent infection.
We found that JAK inhibition blocks HIV replication and prevents virus spread to uninfected CD4+ T cells.
We found that Jak inhibition impacts multiple pathways involved in HIV infection and replication, including those activated by g-cytokines such as IL-2, IL-7 and IL-15.
We have identified a mutation in the histone H3 gene that causes loss of heterochromatin and increased expression of genes normally silenced by heterochromatin.
We have identified several new mutants with defects in silencing, including mutations in histone H3 and H4, as well as in the BAH domain of Sir3p.
We have identified a surface on Sir3p that is required for silencing and found that this surface interacts with the histone H1 protein, which is known to be important in chromatin compaction.
We also show that the binding of Sir3p to the LRS is regulated by a large and extensive surface on the Sir3p BAH domain.

We show that, despite the diversity of biological systems and their computational tasks, there is a common underlying principle governing how these systems perform numerical computations on noisy inputs.
We show that our method is able to identify the underlying structure of the data, including the number of clusters and their relative sizes, with high accuracy.
During meiosis, each pair of homologous chromosomes must exchange genetic material to ensure proper chromosome segregation.
The synaptonemal complex ( SC) is a proteinaceous structure that forms around each pair of homologous chromosomes during meiosis, and it is required for proper chromosome segregation.
The Zip1 protein is essential for the formation of most crossovers and has been proposed to act as a molecular ruler that ensures proper spacing between them.

We show that Zip1 has two distinct functions during meiosis: it promotes crossover formation by promoting the recruitment of MutSg to recombination intermediates, and it prevents crossover interference by preventing excessive SUMOylation of Ecm11.
We show that Zip1 is modified by SUMOylation at multiple lysine residues in vivo and that this modification is important for its function in meiosis.
We show that Zip1 is required for proper localization of Zip3 to the synaptonemal complex ( SC) and that this function requires a specific region of Zip1.
We show that Zip1 has distinct domains required for its role in crossover formation versus synaptonemal complex assembly, suggesting that it may act as a bridge or liaison between the two processes.
We have identified and characterized the threonyl-tRNA synthetase from L. donovani, an intracellular protozoan parasite causing visceral leishmaniasis in humans.
We have developed an efficient method to express recombinant LdThrRS in Escherichia coli, which is used for structural studies of this protein.
We have identified a mutant of Leishmania donovani with reduced virulence, which is defective in threonine tRNA synthetase ( ThrRS) activity.
We found that the compound is able to inhibit the aminoacylation activity of Leishmania donovani tRNA synthetase, which is essential for protein synthesis.
Chromatin structure plays an important role in regulating gene expression, but how this occurs at the molecular level remains unclear.
We show that this is indeed possible and demonstrate that the number of genes that can be brought together in the nucleus is limited by the size of the chromosome.
We use computer simulations to study how these interactions affect the folding of a large piece of DNA, a region of chromosome 19 containing over 1, 000 genes.
We find that genes with similar expression patterns tend to be located close to each other on the chromosome, suggesting that the spatial organization of the genome is related to its function.
The identification of regulatory elements such as enhancers has been a major challenge for decades.
We find that CHD8 is enriched at promoters of genes involved in cell cycle regulation and DNA repair.
We found that CHD8 is required for the recruitment of RNA polymerase II to enhancers and for the production of enhancer-derived non-coding RNAs.
Here, we describe a method to generate a large number of lipids with different chemical properties by combining different lipid building blocks.

We have developed an mRNA delivery system that uses amphiphilic charge-altering releasable transporters ( CARTs), which are designed to deliver mRNA to cells by endocytosis followed by intracellular release of mRNA via a chemical reaction.

Here we report that by combining two different lipids with complementary properties, we have developed a new class of lipid-based nanoparticles ( lipid-varied cell-penetrating peptides) that significantly enhance gene delivery and expression in primary human T cells.
We demonstrate that these lipids enhance gene delivery to primary human T cells and mouse splenocytes, both in vitro and in vivo.
Staphylococcus aureus is an opportunistic pathogen that causes a wide range of infections, from skin and soft tissue to bloodstream and endocardial infections.
Staphylococcus aureus is an opportunistic pathogen that causes disease in humans by infecting various body sites including skin, lung, heart, and blood.
We used transposon sequencing to identify genes required for S. aureus survival within the host, and found that many of these genes are involved in virulence factor production or regulation.
We found that S. aureus infection causes significant decreases in oxygen levels within infected bones.
We found that SsrB is required for full virulence in vivo and that it confers resistance to oxidative stress.
We found that S. aureus uses its SrrAB system to sense changes in oxygen levels and adjust its virulence gene expression accordingly.
Our study demonstrates that S. aureus uses multiple mechanisms to sense and respond to low oxygen levels in order to survive within the host.
The mitochondrial respiratory chain is composed of two large protein complexes ( I and III) that generate most of the energy for the cell by transferring electrons from NADH to oxygen.
Here, we report on the structure of an intact mitochondrial F1Fo-ATP synthase dimer from the green alga Polytomella sp., which is one of the most efficient ATP-producing enzymes known to date.
We show that the dimeric F1-ATPases of two different bacterial species, Thermus thermophilus and Bacillus subtilis, form rows in the membrane and bend it locally.
The F1F0-ATP synthase is an essential enzyme in mitochondria and chloroplasts, which generates most of the energy for eukaryotic cells by converting the proton gradient across the membrane into ATP.
The disease was first identified in China in 2009 and has spread rapidly across East Asia.
However, due to the lack of knowledge about this disease, it is difficult to develop effective countermeasures against SFTS.
We developed two live-attenuated SFTS vaccines ( rSFTS-1 and rSFTS-2) that induced robust antibody responses against SFTS virus infection in ferrets.

We found that immunization with recombinant NSs protein from SFTS virus strain HB29 induced a strong humoral and cellular immune responses in mice, which protected them from challenge with different genotypes of SFTS viruses.
The assembly of viral replication complexes is essential for efficient virus replication and propagation, but how these structures are formed remains poorly understood.

We found that the ER-associated SNARE proteins Ufe1 and Use1 are required for efficient virus replication.

We conclude that Ufe1 and Use1 play an essential role in the formation of the viral replication compartment by acting as assembly hubs for the recruitment of other components of the replication complex.

Our results provide new insights into how the viral RNA genome is targeted to the endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) and how this membrane organelle is modified by the virus to become an efficient platform for viral replication.
The pathogenesis of Huntington disease ( HD) is characterized by progressive degeneration of medium spiny neurons in the striatal region of the brain.
We show here that Hap1 is required for survival of medium spiny neurons in the striatum and that its loss causes progressive degeneration of these cells in an HD mouse model.
We show that Hap1 is required for the survival of neurons in vivo and that its loss leads to neurodegeneration in HD mice.
These results highlight the importance of understanding how different factors interact with each other to cause disease.
Malnutrition is common among older adults, with up to 50% of those in hospital being malnourished.
We found no evidence that dietary advice alone or combined with supplements improved mortality, weight gain or body measurements (anthropometry) compared with usual care.
We found 14 studies (with 1, 511 participants) that compared giving dietary advice and/or oral nutritional supplements to those who were not receiving them.
The review of trials found that dietary advice resulted in an average loss of 1.5 kg more weight over one year than 'no advice' or 'usual care'.
The review found evidence to suggest that dietary advice combined with oral nutritional supplements may help people lose weight and reduce their risk of falls.
More research is needed to find out if there is an effect of nutrition on health in people with dementia.
We found that bladder cancer cells with high metastatic ability were more sensitive to chloroquine ( CQ), an inhibitor of autophagy, than those with low metastatic ability.
We found that both drugs are more toxic to cancer cells with high metastatic potential than those with low metastatic potential, suggesting that they may be useful for treating metastatic disease.
We show that this is due to increased sensitivity to lysosomal inhibitors, which are known to be effective against cancer cells with high levels of autophagy.

This study provides evidence for a novel retinotopic organization in the medial part of the inferior pulvinar, which may be important for the development of the visual cortex.

The basic reproduction number ( R0) is an important parameter in epidemiology, but its estimation remains challenging.
We introduce a novel method for inferring population size histories from molecular data based on a combination of summary statistics derived from the phylogeny and the lineage-through-time plot.
We show that our method is more accurate than previous approaches in estimating key epidemiological quantities such as transmission rate and basic reproduction number.
We also show that our approach is able to provide more accurate parameter estimates than traditional methods when applied to real-world data.

The DNA methylation landscape in mammalian genomes has been extensively studied, but the role of DNA methylation on gene expression remains poorly understood.

We find that these changes occur at specific genomic locations, and we identify several genes whose expression is affected by this change.

The review found no clear evidence that any type of manual therapy improved lung function or reduced symptoms in people with asthma.

Our results suggest that tropical forests will be sensitive to climate change and that their carbon storage capacity is likely to decline with increasing temperature.

Nematophagous fungi are known for their ability to trap nematodes using specialized infection structures ( traps) that have evolved from fungal hyphae.
We found that the evolution of these structures has occurred independently in different groups of spiders, including orb-web spinners, sheet-web spinners and funnel-web spinners.
We sequenced the genomes of two nematode-trapping fungi, one that forms traps ( M. haptotylum) and another that does not ( A. oligospora).
We found that the nematode-trapping fungi have undergone extensive genome rearrangements, including gene duplications and losses, which are likely to be associated with their unique lifestyle.

The use of medication to treat acute aggression is common in psychiatric settings.
This review compared the effectiveness and safety of zuclopenthixol with other medications commonly used to treat similar conditions.
Study characteristics We searched for all relevant studies up to September 2015 and found 12 studies, which included 1, 511 participants.
Zuclopenthixol was no better than other drugs in terms of reducing symptoms and improving quality of life.
The studies did not show any differences between the two types of medication for the symptoms of dry mouth and blurred vision.
The studies were small, and most had serious problems in their design or reporting.
The review found no evidence that zuclopenthixol was better at treating aggression than haloperidol, but it may be associated with fewer side-effects.
The review found that zuclopenthixol is an effective antipsychotic for schizophrenia, but it has more side effects than some other drugs.

We find that the number of modifications in the protein sequence has a strong effect on yield and that the most important factors are the number of modifications per protein and the total number of proteins.

The use of hypothermia in adults who have had a cardiac arrest Review question We reviewed the evidence for using low body temperature (hypothermia) in people with heart failure and who have had a cardiac arrest, in order to improve survival and brain function.
The initial management of patients with cardiac arrest is critical to their survival, as it determines whether they will survive or die.
The aim of this study was to find out whether cooling the body temperature of people who have had a cardiac arrest is beneficial or not.

The review authors found 11 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with 1, 000 participants in which people were randomly assigned to receive hypothermia or no cooling after resuscitation for cardiac arrest.
Study characteristics We identified six studies with 1412 newborn infants enrolled in total.
Quality of evidence The quality of the included studies was good to very good.
The quality of the evidence for pneumonia and hypokalaemia was moderate, but low for all other outcomes.
The review authors found 11 randomised controlled trials, with a total of 1, 111 participants, that compared cooling to no cooling at birth.
Leishmaniasis is a neglected tropical disease caused by Leishmania parasites and transmitted by the bite of infected female Phlebotomus papatasi sand fly.
The disease is usually asymptomatic and does not cause any symptoms until it has progressed to an advanced stage.

We conducted a cross-sectional survey of 872 households in four neighborhoods of Kabul, Afghanistan to assess the association between active and inactive leprosy cases and various household characteristics.

Peer support programmes are increasingly being used in prisons across the UK as well as other countries around the world.
We conducted a systematic review of studies evaluating peer-based interventions for prisoners, which we then discussed at a meeting with experts from relevant fields.
We found that peer-based programmes may be effective in improving prisoners' physical and mental health, reducing substance use and offending behaviour.
We found that there is some evidence to support the use of peer-based interventions for reducing substance misuse, but more research is needed.
The research also found that peer education programmes were effective in changing behaviour, but that they need to be well designed and delivered by people who are good role models.

Antibiotics for treating respiratory tract infections in children Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether or not antibiotics are effective at reducing the symptoms of upper respiratory tract infection (URTIs), such as sore throat, runny nose, coughing and fever, in children.
Antibiotics are commonly prescribed for acute respiratory tract infections ( RTIs) such as bronchitis, pneumonia and sinusitis.
We reviewed all studies evaluating the effects of interventions aimed at improving antibiotic prescribing practices for respiratory tract infections (RTIs) in children under 18 years of age.
The review found that there is not enough evidence to show whether or not these interventions are effective in changing prescribing practices.
The review found that using printed information, or simply giving doctors feedback on their current practice, did not improve how they prescribe medicines for depression.
The review found that interactive education was more effective than didactic lectures, but there were no clear differences between other types of education.

We found that educational outreach visits, which involve a doctor or nurse visiting doctors to give them information about how to improve their practice, were not effective in improving prescribing for antibiotics.
We found that giving people with chesty coughs a delayed prescription for an antibiotic did not increase the risk of complications from their illness or death.

We found that interventions are more likely to be effective if they target specific groups, such as doctors who prescribe most antibiotics, or if they use multiple methods ( e. g., both educational and financial incentives).
We found no evidence to support the use of any particular approach or intervention for improving hand hygiene practices in healthcare settings.

We found that multi-level interventions, which address barriers at different levels of influence ( e. g., individual, community and policy), are more likely to be successful than single-level interventions.

Our results suggest that the increasing rate of river fragmentation has had a major impact on the distribution of freshwater fishes.

The ability to identify cancer cells in a mixed population is critical for understanding the biology of tumors, predicting clinical outcome, and guiding treatment decisions.
We present a method to infer the clonal structure of tumors based on sequencing data from different regions of a single tumor sample.
We show that our method is able to identify clonal relationships between tumors with high accuracy.

We used molecular dynamics ( MD) simulations to study the interaction between the G-protein and the k-opioid receptor, which is an important target for pain relief.
The binding of G proteins is facilitated by the interaction with specific amino acids within the binding pocket and the subsequent conformational changes in the receptor.

This work demonstrates how the I-TevI homing endonuclease can be used to improve CRISPR-Cas9-mediated genome editing by reducing off-target effects and increasing the efficiency of precise gene deletion.

The alternative pathway of the complement system is activated by direct binding of C3b to self-antigen and is important for protection against bacterial infections.

Here we report that ecotin, a protein from the venom of the snake Naja mossambica, specifically inhibits the activation of the lectin pathway by binding to and blocking the activity of MASP-3, the only known activator of this pathway in human blood.
We show that ecotin is a potent inhibitor of several human immune defense proteins and that it may be used by bacteria to evade host defenses.
The cell division machinery is modified during meiosis to ensure that each daughter cell receives only one copy of each chromosome.
However, the molecular mechanism by which this occurs is unknown.
Here we report that the anaphase-promoting complex ( APC) is required for proper progression of meiosis in Arabidopsis thaliana and that it acts through its E2 ubiquitin ligases Apc2 and Cdh1.
We show that TDM1 is required for proper completion of meiosis II in Arabidopsis thaliana and that its function is regulated by CDKA; 1-mediated phosphorylation.
We propose that this mechanism may be conserved in other organisms where the number of cell divisions is regulated by similar mechanisms.
The Himalayan region is experiencing rapid warming, which poses significant risks to local communities and ecosystems.
We show that the number of avalanches in the Western Indian Himalayas has increased over the last few decades and that this trend is consistent with climate change.
Our results show that the number of snow avalanches has increased with increasing temperatures, but not precipitation, over the past century.

We show that the presence of hot water in the vicinity of fossil-bearing sites is common in Africa, and we suggest that this may have played an important role in human evolution.

We present an assessment of the impact of climate change on the production of nonstaple vegetables and legumes, which are important components of healthy diets in many developing countries.

We show that climate change will have large impacts on global food production, with important implications for human health.

The review found no clear evidence that suctioning with or without disconnecting the tube from the ventilator improved survival, length of time on the ventilator and other important clinical outcomes.

We also show that the mosquito's ability to deposit iron is dependent on the presence of blood meal-derived heme and that this process is mediated by the mosquito's midgut epithelium.

We have previously shown that albimanus mosquitoes are more susceptible to P. berghei infection than other Anopheles species, and we have identified several genes that may be involved in this increased susceptibility.

We found that iron chelation also resulted in a decrease in copper levels and an increase in oxidative stress.

The results presented here demonstrate the potential for BoHV-4 to be used as an effective vaccine vector against HSV-1 infection in mice, providing a new tool for future studies on HSV-1 pathogenesis and immunology.

We have analyzed the evolution of influenza A viruses circulating in humans and found that reassortment is associated with an increased rate of amino acid substitutions in the viral genome.

We found that the genetic diversity of influenza A viruses is not only determined by their evolutionary history but also by the genomic segments they carry, and that the evolution of individual segments is influenced by the other segments carried by the virus.

The use of different types of needles for reducing post-dural puncture headache Post-dural puncture headache (PDPH) is a complication of lumbar puncture, which occurs in about 1% to 2% of people undergoing this procedure.
The use of different types of needles, or different sizes of needles, may help reduce the risk of post-dural puncture headache.
The review authors searched the literature up to September 2015 and identified 11 studies, which included 1, 111 participants.
Study characteristics The evidence is current to September 2016.
The use of traumatic needles was associated with an increased risk of post-dural puncture headache when compared to atraumatic needles.

The review authors found that there is insufficient evidence from randomised controlled trials to show that atraumatic needles are better than traumatic needles for preventing post-dural puncture headache.
The review found that there was moderate-quality evidence that atraumatic needles are less likely to cause PDPH than traumatic needles, but no difference in other complications.
We found no clear evidence that one type of needle was better than another, but we are not very sure about this as the quality of the evidence was low.
High-throughput data is often analyzed using dimensionality reduction techniques, which reduce the number of variables in the data while retaining as much information as possible.
The analysis of gene expression data is often complicated by confounding factors such as batch effects and cell type heterogeneity.
We present an approach called AC-PCA that combines PCA with regression to simultaneously reduce dimensionality and adjust for confounders.
We demonstrate that our method is able to identify genes with significant differences between individuals, tissues, or species using both simulated data and real datasets.
Archaeological sites are threatened by the increasing global population and urbanization, which results in the loss of irreplaceable historical data.
We use a combination of historical aerial photographs from 1917-2016 and airborne laser scanning to create a high-resolution digital elevation model of the ancient city of Jerash in northern Jordan.
The city's history is well-documented in written records, but many aspects of its layout and appearance have been lost due to centuries of urbanization and destruction.

Trichomonas vaginalis is a protozoan parasite that infects millions of people worldwide and causes inflammation in the vagina.
However, little is known about how the host responds to infection with this parasite.
The host immune response to infection is mediated by various immune cells, including neutrophils, which are recruited to the site of infection in large numbers.
We found that human PMNs are able to kill T. vaginalis through a mechanism that is dependent on direct cell-cell contact between the parasite and the phagocyte.
We found that PMNs kill T. vaginalis by ingesting small portions of the parasite ( called trogocytosis) and then killing them.
We found that both mechanisms require the presence of human serum, but not complement, and that they are mediated by neutrophil serine proteases.
This study provides evidence that neutrophils use trogocytosis as an alternative strategy to clear pathogens from the host.
The aim of this study was to find out if or how well the new treatment works for young people with schizophrenia.

We conducted a pilot study to test whether or not it would be possible to conduct a larger study of this treatment in the future.
We also interviewed patients, staff and managers to find out if it was acceptable and feasible to do the trial.
The study compared the effects of adding an antipsychotic to the treatment plan of people who were already receiving antidepressants for depression and/or anxiety.
The review found that there is insufficient evidence to support the use of psychological interventions alone or in combination with medication for people who have schizophrenia.
The study was well conducted, with no major problems or concerns identified.
We found that it was possible to run a study like this but we would need to make some changes to how the study is designed.

We found that the tongue is able to lap up water at speeds as high as 0.

Physiotherapy for complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) Review question We reviewed the evidence about the effects of physiotherapy on people with complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS).
Study characteristics We found 11 studies, with a total of 1, 111 participants, that met our inclusion criteria.
Study characteristics We found 18 studies with a total of 739 participants, all aged between 6 and 16 years.
The review authors found no studies that looked at the effects of physiotherapy on pain or disability in CRPS type 1.
The quality of evidence was low to moderate, as most studies had methodological flaws.
We found no evidence that any of these treatments were effective at improving pain in the long term.
There was no evidence to support the use of electrotherapy (including ultrasound, laser, light therapy) or manual lymphatic drainage in CRPS type I or II.

The use of dexamethasone to prevent intraventricular haemorrhage in preterm babies Review question We reviewed the evidence for using dexamethasone to prevent bleeding in the brain (intraventricular haemorrhage) in preterm babies.
Study characteristics We found 11 studies with 1, 111 participants.

The review authors found that there was no evidence from randomised controlled trials that any one type of surgery is better than another for preventing bleeding, but the trials were small and had some design problems.

This review found no evidence that giving phenobarbital to mothers before birth prevents bleeding in the brain in their babies, and there is also no evidence that it reduces long-term problems with movement or development in children who have had intraventricular haemorrhage (IVH).
We found no evidence to support or refute the use of any particular type of analgesic medication for the treatment of postoperative pain after surgery on the lower limb.

We found no studies that looked at the effects of using computerised video games for improving driving skills, or the effect of using a combination of different types of therapy.
Aluminum salts, such as aluminum hydroxide or aluminum phosphate, are commonly used as vaccine adjuvants to enhance immune responses to antigens.

We found that IL-33 secreted from dying cells induces type 2 immune responses in mice immunized with alum vaccine.
The schistosome egg is an important target for drug development, as it represents a critical stage in the parasite's lifecycle that must be overcome by the immune system to prevent infection.
The parasite secretes proteins that stimulate the immune system, causing it to form a barrier around the egg in order to prevent its escape.

We have studied the process of egg hatching in S. japonicum using a combination of microscopy, electron microscopy, and mathematical modeling to describe the dynamics of this process.

We found that the major changes that occur to the egg during activation include the breakdown of the outermost layer ( the eggshell) and the inner membrane ( the endotrophe), as well as the hydration of the gel-like substance that fills the space between the parasite and its membranes.

Integrated information theory ( IIT) is a theoretical framework for understanding consciousness, which has been successful in explaining many aspects of conscious experience and has been used to predict new phenomena.
We formalize these principles into a set of axioms and show that they are sufficient for generating a wide range of experiences.

The theory predicts that consciousness arises from the integrated information generated by the causal interactions within a system, and that this integrated information is encoded in the form of a maximal amount of information that cannot be decomposed into independent parts.

We show how this theory explains many phenomena in consciousness research, such as why we have multiple levels of consciousness, why some parts of our brain are more important for consciousness than others, and why some people with damaged brains remain conscious despite having no subjective experience.
Nitric oxide ( NO) is an important antimicrobial agent produced by macrophages and neutrophils in response to infection.

However, it is unclear how much nitric oxide ( NO) stress Salmonella encounters during infection and whether this affects bacterial virulence.
We found that bacterial DNA is damaged by reactive nitrogen species ( RNS) produced by host cells, but that this damage does not affect bacterial growth in vitro or in vivo.
We show that this damage is caused by reactive nitrogen species produced from nitric oxide ( NO) generated by the host immune response.

The color of an object is determined by its reflectance spectrum, but also depends on the way it interacts with light and the environment.

We show that trichromacy is advantageous for detecting ripe fruit, but not for detecting leaves or other food items.

The circadian clock is regulated at multiple levels including transcriptional, translational, and posttranslational processes.
In this study, we show that the circadian clock regulates chromatin remodeling at the level of histone modification and transcription factor binding to DNA.
We show that the Brm complex, which is known to regulate gene expression in other organisms, regulates the circadian clock in Drosophila melanogaster.
We show that BRM is required for maintaining high levels of nucleosomes at the promoter regions of genes that are repressed in the morning but not those that are expressed in the evening.
We show that BRM has both catalytic and non-catalytic roles in regulating gene expression.
We have generated a mouse model to study the function of ATF2 in melanoma and found that loss of ATF2 expression in melanocytes leads to increased tumor growth and metastasis.
We found that expression of mutant ATF2 in melanocytes is sufficient to induce melanoma formation in mice.
We found that MITF is downregulated by ATF2 in mouse skin and human melanoma cells.
We found that loss of ATF2 in melanoma cells significantly reduced their ability to form colonies on soft agar, an assay for anchorage-independent growth.
We show that ATF2 is required for melanoma cell proliferation and survival, and that it acts by regulating the expression of the master regulator of melanocyte differentiation, MITF.
The ability of multiple species to coexist in nature is a fundamental question in ecology and evolutionary biology.
The question of how species diversity is generated and maintained remains one of the most fundamental questions in ecology and evolution.
We present a new method for detecting and quantifying clusters of species in ecological communities using abundance data.
Our results suggest that cluster formation is not limited to specific circumstances and may be a common feature of many biological systems.
The endocannabinoid system ( ECS) plays an important role in pain perception and has been shown to be involved in the analgesic action of morphine.
We show that the antinociception induced by THC is mediated through cannabinoid CB1 receptors, whereas its amnesia is mediated by serotonin 5-HT2A receptors.
We found that CB1R and 5-HT2A receptors form heteromers in vivo and that these heteromers are functional in specific brain areas where they may be important for cognitive functions.
This study provides evidence for the existence of CB1R-5-HT2A receptor heteromers in the brain and their role in modulating cognition.
We show that languages with fewer speakers are changing faster than those with more speakers.
The study of language evolution is an important part of understanding how humans have evolved as a species, yet it remains unclear whether small populations or large ones drive linguistic change.
Insecticide treated targets are widely used to control tsetse fly populations in Africa, but their effectiveness is reduced when they become obscured by vegetation growth.
This is particularly important in the case of new, smaller targets where the number of available antibodies is limited.

We show that the presence of gaps in vegetation, which are common in nature, can lead to the emergence of complex spatiotemporal patterns of disease spread.

We conclude that the current recommended treatment strategy for this important vector species may not be optimal and that a more efficient strategy could be implemented to reduce costs associated with control programmes.
Aging is associated with progressive loss of muscle mass and strength, which contributes to frailty and disability.
We have used a forward genetics approach to identify genes that modify the rate of aging in Drosophila melanogaster.
We used the nematode C. elegans to identify genes that are required for normal aging of the nervous system.
We found that the Ras-related GTPase RAGA-1 is required to maintain normal locomotor activity in old age, and that its loss causes an increase in locomotor activity.
We found that RAGA-1 is required for longevity in C. elegans, as loss of raga-1 extended lifespan, whereas overexpression of raga-1 shortened it.

Genome-wide association studies have been widely used for identifying genetic variants associated with complex diseases.
We propose a new method, Multiple Loci Linear Mixed Model ( MLMM), which combines the advantages of both approaches by incorporating multiple markers simultaneously in a linear mixed model framework.
We have developed an efficient algorithm to perform GWAS on large-scale datasets using a combination of parallel computing and memory-efficient data structures.

We show that this approach is effective in training a generic brain-computer interface and demonstrate its ability to learn from human subjects with paralysis.

The Mediterranean region is home to many species of plants and animals that are endemic to this area.
Our results highlight the importance of conserving relict tree populations in the face of climate change, especially in areas with high biodiversity.
TLX is a transcription factor expressed in adult-born neurons, but its function remains unknown.
We have developed a novel transgenic mouse model to investigate the role of TLX in the developing brain.

We found that TLX promotes neurogenesis by increasing proliferation of neural stem/progenitor cells ( NSPCs) in the subgranular zone ( SGZ), a region of the hippocampus where new neurons are generated throughout life, and that this effect is mediated through activation of the Wnt/b-catenin signaling pathway.
We found that the absence of TLX in the developing brain causes severe defects in the generation of neurons and glial cells from neural stem cells.
The TLX transgenic mice showed improved cognitive function and reduced anxiety-like behavior.
In this study we show that mice lacking the enzyme MKP-2 are unable to control L. major infection and develop severe disease.

In this study we show that mice lacking the enzyme myeloid cell-specific protein tyrosine phosphatase 2 ( MKP-2) are not able to control Leishmania major infection in their skin but do clear the parasites from their blood.
We found that although Leishmania-infected MKP-2-deficient macrophages are less able to kill parasites when unstimulated, they respond normally to stimulation with IFN-g and LPS.
Iron-sulfur ( Fe-S) clusters are essential cofactors found in many enzymes involved in diverse biological processes.
In this study, we show that the mitochondrial glutaredoxin GRXS15 is required for the assembly of iron-sulfur cluster-containing aconitase ( ISC-ACO).
We found that GRXS15 coordinates with the ISC by binding to it.
In this study, we identified an Arabidopsis thaliana gene ( AtGRXS15) that is required for proper iron homeostasis in plants and demonstrated that it functions as a cysteine desulfurase.
We also identified key amino acids in GRXS15 that are important for its function.
We show that GRXS15 is required for the activation of several Fe-S cluster-containing enzymes and that it is essential for plant growth under normal conditions.

The new oral anticoagulants are safer and easier to use than warfarin but may be more expensive.

Salmonella uses two type III secretion systems ( T3SSs) to inject virulence factors directly into host cells and to establish infection.
The bacterial type III secretion system ( T3SS) is an important virulence factor for many pathogenic bacteria.
Inflammasome activation by Salmonella in macrophages has been reported, but its role in vivo is unknown.
We show that both inflammasomes are activated by Salmonella infection of human macrophages and that their combined action is required to control bacterial growth.
We found that Salmonella induces activation of two inflammasome complexes, NLRC4/NAIP and NLRP3, which are important for host defense against this pathogen.
Arthropod-borne viruses ( arboviruses) have evolved to infect two hosts, vertebrates and insects, but the origin of this dual host range is unknown.
We have used a combination of experimental evolution and mathematical modeling to study how viruses evolve when they are transmitted by insects.
We found that both viruses are closely related to each other and to other viruses in the genus Orthobunyavirus.

Wolbachia are bacteria that live inside cells and are found in many insects, including mosquitoes, ticks, and fruit flies.
We have sequenced part of the genome of a Wolbachia strain ( wOvo) from the filarial worm Onchocerca volvulus, which causes river blindness in humans.

We found that the Wolbachia strain from the human filarial nematode Brugia malayi ( wBm) has a higher similarity to the Wolbachia strain from fruit flies ( wMel) than it does to the Wolbachia strain from mosquitoes ( wOvo).
We found evidence for horizontal gene transfer from Wolbachia to Onchocerca and demonstrated that this occurred in the recent evolutionary past.
We found that this gene was lost in the ancestor of the human parasite Onchocerca volvulus and its cattle-parasitic sister species, Onchocerca ochengi, but not in other filarial nematodes.

The ability to perceive the distance between two stimuli is essential for our everyday life, and it has been studied extensively in humans and animals.

We show that the brain represents tactile distance in a way that is consistent with a simple metric representation of space, and that this representation is not distorted by the presence of obstacles or other objects.

Our results suggest that SPF45 may have evolved to play a role in DNA repair by promoting the recombination-dependent pathway of homologous recombination.

We show that Tax binds to and inhibits the function of the A20 protein, which is an inhibitor of NF-kB activation.

We found that Tax interacts with cell adhesion molecule 1 ( CADM1), which is known as an HIV-1 receptor, and induces its ubiquitination by recruiting E2 conjugating enzyme Ubc13.
We found that the C-terminal domain of CADM1, which contains a PDZ-binding motif, is required for Tax to activate NF-kB.
We found that this interaction is required for efficient activation of the IKK complex and subsequent NF-kB activation by Tax.

Q fever is caused by Coxiella burnetii, a bacterium that infects cells and resides within them for extended periods of time.

We present a method for testing these hypotheses in an efficient way by first testing simpler hypotheses and then combining them to test more complex ones.
Our method is shown to be more powerful than existing methods for detecting differential expression in RNA-Seq data.
This approach provides a systems-level view of disease mechanisms and is useful in identifying potential drug targets.

The TRP family of ion channels are important in sensing temperature, mechanical stress, and chemical stimuli.
The transient receptor potential vanilloid type 1 ( TRPV1) ion channel is activated by capsaicin and other noxious stimuli, leading to pain sensation.

Our findings provide evidence for a novel mechanism by which TRP channels contribute to the development of tachyphylaxis, and may have implications for the design of new analgesic drugs.

The development of effective vaccines is one of the most important strategies for controlling the spread of rabies in humans and animals.
We developed two vaccine candidates based on the use of a recombinant plasmid DNA or a modified vaccinia virus Ankara vector expressing the RVFV envelope glycoprotein Gc.
We found that the rMVA-Gn/Gc vaccine was able to induce both humoral and cellular immune responses in mice lacking type I interferon receptor ( IFNAR) but not in wild-type mice.
We found that vaccination with the rMVA-N vaccine alone did not confer complete protection from RVFV infection and disease but was able to reduce the severity of the disease.

In this study, we evaluated the impact of ten years of vector control on dengue transmission in Comapa, a rural community located in the state of Pernambuco, Brazil.
We found that the number of mosquitoes collected from houses was significantly lower in 2011 than in 2001 and 2007.
Our results highlight the need for continued vector control, as well as improved screening and treatment of congenital cases in endemic areas.

Our results suggest that adipose tissue may be an important reservoir of Mtb in humans, and that it could potentially serve as a site for immune surveillance by CD8+ T cells and natural killer ( NK) cells.

We show that the bactericidal activity of PCI is mediated by its interaction with bacterial membranes, which results in the disruption of cell envelope integrity.

We found no evidence to support the use of antileukotriene drugs as an add-on treatment for adults or children with acute asthma, but more research is needed.

We found eight studies that compared different types of antibiotics in 1470 adults and 470 children.
The studies included in this review were of variable quality, and had small numbers of participants.
There were no differences in the number of children admitted to hospital with asthma during the study period for those who received long-term beta-agonists compared to placebo or usual care.

Key results The review found that there was an improvement in lung function for people who received long-acting beta-agonists compared to placebo, but this result is based on only two studies and so should be treated with caution.
We found no evidence of benefit for any other outcomes, including pain relief or reduction in the number of days with fever.

Oral cholera vaccines ( OCVs) are recommended by the WHO for use in endemic areas, but their effectiveness is unknown.
In this study we evaluated the efficacy of Dukoral, a killed whole cell vaccine against cholera, administered as a two dose regimen in a mass vaccination campaign in Zanzibar, Tanzania.
We used mathematical models to estimate the number of cases, deaths, and costs associated with cholera in Bangladesh during the period of the campaign.

We found that the most effective interventions are those that target the entire population, such as vaccination programmes, but these are also the least cost-effective in terms of cost per death or disability averted.
Vibrio cholerae O1 strains are responsible for most of the cases of cholera worldwide and have been classified as serogroup I or III.
The World Health Organization recommends the use of rapid diagnostic tests ( RDTs) to detect cholera cases in outbreak settings.
We found that the prevalence of V. cholerae in stool samples was higher in children than adults, with no significant difference between males and females.
We developed a stool DNA-based test for rapid detection of Vibrio cholerae in stool samples from patients with diarrhea.
The sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value ( PPV) and negative predictive value ( NPV) were calculated for each test.
We found that the sensitivity of the RDT was higher in children with severe diarrhea ( 92%) than in those with mild-moderate diarrhea ( 83%).
The results showed that the new RDTs were as good as the old ones in detecting schistosomiasis infection.

We also looked at how well the studies were carried out and whether or not they were likely to be biased.
The results showed that there was no difference between the two treatments in terms of overall survival, local recurrence-free survival, disease-free survival, and adverse events.

The results of this study suggest that, if the current evidence holds true, the cost-effectiveness of the treatment would be very low and it could be considered as an option for patients with mild or moderate dementia who have not responded to other treatments.

The use of alpha-blockers for treating ureteral stones Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether or not alpha-blockers are effective in relieving pain from kidney stones (ureteral stones) and preventing complications.

The main treatments for stones in the ureters are extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL), which uses shock waves from outside the body to break up the stone, and ureteroscopy, which involves using a small tube to remove the stone through the bladder and into the kidney.
The aim of this review was to compare the effectiveness and safety of these two treatments for stones in the ureters.

The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the need for rapid development of new assays to detect antibodies against SARS-CoV-2 in serum samples.

We compared ten different serology tests on a panel of 160 samples from COVID-19 patients and healthy individuals.
We found that the sensitivity of these tests was very high ( >95%) if they were performed at least 20 days after the onset of symptoms.

The differences between the drug and the dummy treatment were small, with an increase in the number of bowel movements per week.
The results show that tegaserod improves the symptoms of IBS, but does not have an effect on the quality of life or the severity of the disease.
More research is needed to find out if it works for women and men.
The management of urinary incontinence after stroke Review question We reviewed the evidence for the effectiveness of different ways to manage urine leakage after a stroke.
We developed a systematic voiding programme ( SVP) to help people who have had a stroke to manage their bladder problems more effectively.

The research was divided into two phases: Phase I involved an overview of all current ways of managing urinary incontinence after stroke, followed by a detailed case study of how the SVP was introduced at one NHS trust.
We found that there was no difference in the number of people who had returned home from hospital at 30 days between those who received the intervention and those who did not.
The results showed that both interventions were better at treating urinary incontinence than usual care, but there was no difference between them.
We found that, although we did not have enough data to be sure whether or not the intervention was effective in improving quality of life, it was acceptable to patients and staff.
The results of this study suggest that the intervention may help people who have both types of urinary leakage but more research is needed before it can be recommended for use.
Lyme disease is caused by Borrelia burgdorferi, which is transmitted by Ixodes ticks.

We found that B. burgdorferi has a very small number of genes that are known to protect bacteria from oxidative damage and that these genes do not appear to be sufficient to confer resistance to oxidative stress.

We found that B. burgdorferi mutants lacking predicted outer membrane proteins are more sensitive to oxidative stress than wild-type bacteria, while mutants lacking predicted inner membrane proteins are less sensitive.
Our results suggest that B. burgdorferi has evolved mechanisms to sense oxidative stress and respond by altering gene expression to promote survival within its host.
The evolution of bipedal locomotion is one of the most important events in primate history, but its impact on the skull has been less well studied.
The FOXP2 gene has been identified as a major contributor to this disorder, but its function remains poorly understood.

This study provides evidence for a role of FOXP2 in the evolution of human language, and it shows how a single mutation could have had such a profound effect on our species' ability to communicate with each other.

We found that Foxp2 is required for proper development of the musculoskeletal system in mice, including the formation of joints and cartilage, both of which are critical components of the human pelvis and legs.
We propose that FOXP2 was involved in the evolution of human bipedalism and speech.

Opioid medications for treating pain in people with cancer Background Opioids (such as morphine) are commonly used to treat severe pain caused by cancer.
The majority of studies have focused on people who inject heroin and are not dependent on other drugs.
Maintenance pharmacotherapies are drugs used to help people who have become dependent on opioids (such as heroin) to stop using them.
Study characteristics We searched for all relevant studies up to May 2015.
We found 12 studies that compared buprenorphine to other opioids, including methadone, morphine, codeine, and LAAM, and we found five studies that compared buprenorphine to placebo.
We searched for all relevant studies up to July 2015, using standard methods of systematic review.
The review authors rated the quality of the evidence as low for retention in care, high for depression and anxiety, and moderate for self-reported opioid use and urinalysis.

Key results We found some low quality evidence showing that people who are maintained on buprenorphine for six months have less opioid use than those who receive detoxification or psychological support only.

The review found that there is low-quality evidence from one study (N = 50) that people who are dependent on opioids and receive an opioid agonist medication as part of their treatment have less chance of dropping out of treatment than those who do not receive this type of medication.
The studies were generally well conducted but had some limitations in terms of study design and reporting.

The recent emergence of Zika virus ( ZIKV), which is transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, has caused severe neurological complications and birth defects.

We also developed a mathematical model to simulate the spread of Zika virus within a population of mosquitoes and humans.

Amineptine is an antidepressant medication that has been used in Europe and Japan since the 1970s but is not currently approved for use in the United States.

The aim of this review was to find out whether or not exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation programmes are effective at reducing the risk of further heart problems and improving quality of life for people who have had an MI.

Bicycle helmets have been shown to be effective in reducing the number and severity of head injuries from cycling accidents.
Some countries have introduced legislation requiring helmets for all cyclists, but there is controversy over whether this should be done.

The authors found six relevant studies from three countries (Australia, Canada and the United States) in which laws were introduced to make it compulsory for children to wear helmets when riding bicycles.
We found no evidence to support or refute the effectiveness of any particular type of law, although some types of laws may be more effective than others.
The results of this review show that, on average, bicycle helmet laws increase helmet use by around 10% and reduce head injuries by about 20%.
There is no evidence to support or refute the effectiveness of any particular type of scheme.
