The use of endotracheal tubes for ventilation in critically ill patients is common practice, but it also carries risks such as damage to the trachea and complications related to the insertion of the tube.

The review found no evidence to support or refute the use of routine suctioning versus suctioning only when there is an indication, or more frequent versus less frequent suctioning for reducing lung injury in newborn babies receiving assisted ventilation.
The review authors searched for all relevant randomised controlled trials (RCTs) published up to July 2016, as well as ongoing studies.
The review authors found no evidence to support or refute any particular strategy for how often to clean the tube in newborn babies on breathing machines.
Study characteristics We found only one small trial (97 participants) of preterm babies with very low birth weight, which compared giving vitamin A to those who did not receive it.

The review found no clear evidence of a difference between the two treatments in terms of rates of complications such as bronchopulmonary dysplasia, air leak into the space around the lung (pneumothorax), bleeding within the brain (intraventricular hemorrhage), death during the newborn period, or duration of ventilation support.
The review authors found one randomised controlled trial (RCT) of 100 participants, which compared the two treatments and showed no significant difference in the number of deaths between the groups.
The review found no clear evidence on how often to perform suctioning for babies receiving assisted ventilation, and more research is needed.
We show that the chemical properties of the most abundant amino acids in proteins are consistent with their selection by early Earth's primordial soup.

We show that the last two amino acids to be incorporated into proteins were likely fixed by the action of molecular oxygen on simple organic compounds.

Mirror therapy for improving recovery of movement control in people with hemiparesis after stroke Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether mirror therapy improves recovery of movement control in people who have had a stroke and have weakness on one side of their body (hemiparesis).
Mirror therapy for stroke patients Review question We reviewed the evidence for the use of mirror therapy to improve recovery after stroke.
Mirror therapy is a form of rehabilitation in which patients are asked to perform active movements while looking at a mirror image of their own body.
The aim of this review was to find out if mirror therapy is effective in improving recovery from stroke.
The review authors found no studies that compared the effectiveness of different types of exercise programs for people with schizophrenia.
Study characteristics We found 62 studies, involving 1982 people who had had a stroke, which compared mirror therapy with another treatment or no treatment.
Mirror therapy also improved activities of daily living in people with stroke.
We found some evidence that mirror therapy is effective in improving arm movement, functional use of the affected limb, and reducing pain when used alongside standard physiotherapy treatment.
Quality of the evidence The quality of the evidence was low due to small numbers of participants in the studies and poor reporting of study methods.
The review found that there was not enough evidence to show whether or not combining treatments are better than either treatment alone.
We found 14 studies (with 12, 000 participants) that evaluated the effect of adding behavioural support to medication for smoking cessation.
Study characteristics We searched for all randomised controlled trials (RCTs) of interventions to reduce alcohol consumption in adults aged 18 years or older.
The trial also showed that people who were given more support to quit smoking did not have better outcomes than those who received less support.
The review found 52 studies involving 19,488 people who were overweight or obese.
We found that people who were recruited from the community had less improvement in their symptoms and quality of life than those recruited in hospitals.
The review found that combining medication with counselling increases the chances of quitting, but it is not clear if one method is better than another.

PfEMP1 proteins are expressed on the surface of malaria-infected red blood cells and mediate their adhesion to endothelial cells lining blood vessels, which is essential for parasite survival in the host.
PfEMP1 proteins are encoded by large multigene families and their sequences vary greatly between parasites.

We found that OsIAA26 is degraded by the ubiquitin-26S proteasome system in response to auxin treatment and that this process requires the E3 ligase SOR1.
This work identifies a novel regulatory mechanism for auxin-mediated root growth regulation and provides insights into how plants integrate multiple hormone signals to regulate plant development.

Gemcitabine for advanced or recurrent bladder cancer Review question We reviewed the evidence about the effect of gemcitabine on survival in people with advanced or recurrent bladder cancer (cancer that has spread to other parts of the body).
We searched for all studies that compared gemcitabine with other treatments in patients with NMIIBC and found one study (a randomised controlled trial) that met our inclusion criteria.

The review authors identified one randomised controlled trial comparing a single post-operative instillation of gemcitabine with a saline placebo in 341 patients who had undergone TURBT for non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer.
The results from this study showed that there was no difference in the rate of tumour recurrence or progression between patients who received gemcitabine and those who received BCG.

Intravesical chemotherapy for non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether or not chemotherapy given directly into the bladder (intravesically) after surgery reduces the risk of recurrence and progression of non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC).

We found that the number of essential genes is higher in regions with lower recombination rates, and that this relationship holds true for all species examined.
p53 is a well-known tumor suppressor protein, which has been implicated in many human cancers.
MicroRNAs ( miRs) are small non-coding RNAs that regulate gene expression at the post-transcriptional level and have been implicated in many diseases including cancer.
However, the mechanisms underlying the regulation of p53 by miRNAs are not well understood.

Liquid crystals are ubiquitous in modern technology, from smartphones to televisions and computer monitors.
We show that LCD screens emit many volatile organic compounds ( VOCs), including some known indoor air pollutants and others previously unknown in this context.

We found that LCD screens emit volatile organic compounds ( VOCs) at levels comparable to those from other common sources, and that they contribute significantly to indoor air pollution in homes with high screen usage.

We found that avian viruses tend to be transmitted by only a few mosquito species that inhabit particular habitats, whereas mammalian viruses are transmitted by many different mosquito species across a broad range of habitats.
This study provides new insights into the ecology of dengue virus transmission in Thailand, which will help to inform future vector control strategies.
Breath-holding spells are common in young children and may be caused by a variety of factors including iron deficiency.
The review authors found no studies to include in this review.
The review found that iron supplementation may reduce the number of breath-holding attacks, but there is no evidence that it reduces their severity.
Breast milk is a rich source of nutrients and antibodies for infants; however, it also contains viruses such as HIV, hepatitis B virus ( HBV), and cytomegalovirus ( CMV).
Our results suggest that breastfeeding should be avoided in cases of ZIKV or USUV infection, as recommended by the World Health Organization.

We find that neighborhoods with higher shares of same-sex couples have less access to credit, lower loan amounts, and higher interest rates than other neighborhoods.
The inositol phosphates are a family of important cellular signaling molecules.

We have developed a method for generating antibodies against inositol phosphates ( InsPs) and pyrophosphoinositides ( PP-InsPs), which are important signaling molecules involved in many cellular processes.
We found that the majority of these interactions were with proteins involved in nucleotide metabolism, ribosome assembly, and protein modification by phosphorylation.

This work demonstrates that inositol pyrophosphates are important regulators of cellular metabolism and that they may be used as potential therapeutic targets for diseases associated with aberrant lipid metabolism.
We identify the major drivers of mangrove loss globally, including deforestation for rice production in Myanmar, oil palm expansion in Southeast Asia, and urbanization in China.

We show that mangrove forests are being cleared at an alarming rate in the border regions of Myanmar, Indonesia, and Malaysia, with implications for biodiversity conservation and climate change mitigation.
Neutrophil granulocytes are the first line of defense against invading pathogens and play an important role in the regulation of adaptive immunity.
We found that neutrophils from preeclamptic women are unable to activate natural regulatory T ( niT) cells due to an intrinsic defect in transferring the transcription factor FOXO1 to T cells.

Our findings provide new insights into how immune cells regulate vascular remodeling and may have implications for understanding human pregnancy complications such as preeclampsia, where aberrant angiogenesis is implicated.
Dendritic cells ( DCs) are specialized antigen-presenting cells that play an essential role in initiating immune responses against pathogens.

The innate immune system is activated by pathogen recognition receptors, such as Toll-like receptors ( TLRs), which trigger downstream signaling pathways that lead to the production of cytokines and chemokines.
We show that this is not the case; instead, we find that the two pathways have distinct roles in promoting adaptive immune responses.
The photosynthetic electron transfer chain is vulnerable to oxidative damage, which can be caused by both internal and external factors.

We report here that the photosynthetic protein Photosystem I ( PS1) uses vibrational modes to enhance its light-harvesting efficiency and protect itself from photooxidation by exploiting the principles of quantum mechanics.

We show that this mechanism is particularly important in the presence of oxidative stress, where the resonance between the exciton and the vibrational mode is broken, leading to increased exciton quenching.
We have developed a live-attenuated chikungunya virus ( CHIKV) vaccine candidate that is safe, immunogenic and protective in mice and non-human primates.
This study provides evidence that the CHIKV/IRES vaccine is safe and efficacious in mice, making it an ideal candidate for future clinical trials.
The COVID-19 pandemic has caused unprecedented changes in human behavior and activity patterns worldwide.
We show how the UK's first national lockdown in March 2020 affected people's lives by analyzing data from the UK Household Longitudinal Study ( UHLS).
We found that, although people's thoughts about COVID-19 were reduced by the end of the lockdown, they did not return to pre-lockdown levels.
The effect of surface roughness on wetting properties has been studied extensively, but its influence on flow through porous materials remains poorly understood.

This work demonstrates how a mixed-wet surface can be achieved by combining two different wetting liquids on a single surface, which has implications for understanding natural processes such as oil-water-gas interactions in porous media and designing materials for enhanced fluid transport.
Segmental duplications ( SDs), which are large-scale DNA segments with high sequence identity, have been implicated in many human genetic disorders and cancers.

We found that this organism has a unique metabolism, including a novel nitrogen assimilation pathway, and is capable of using multiple hydrogenases in a single cell.
We found that the rgy gene is widely distributed among bacteria living at high temperatures, including those from deep-sea hydrothermal vents, hot springs, and geothermal fields.

We show that N. profundicola has all of the genetic tools required to survive in an anaerobic environment rich in hydrogen gas and sulfide ions, which are thought to have been present on Earth during its early history.
Biodiversity is essential to human well-being, yet its value is difficult to quantify.

The aim of this review was to find out if lowering or raising blood pressure in people with acute stroke improves their outcome.
The review found that lowering blood pressure is not harmful to people with acute stroke but does not improve their outcome.

Study characteristics We searched for all randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatments groups) published up to July 2016, which included adults who had had a stroke within seven days and were treated with antihypertensive drugs (drugs used to lower blood pressure).

We found no evidence of any benefit from using these drugs in this way, but there was some evidence that they may cause side effects such as dizziness, nausea, vomiting, headache, dry mouth, constipation, and urinary retention.
Study characteristics We found 26 studies with 17,011 participants.
The review found no evidence for or against withholding antihypertensive medication in people with subarachnoid haemorrhage.

The ability to generate variability in gene expression is essential for normal development and function of multicellular organisms.
We have studied how the green alga Volvox folds its embryo into a sphere by analysing the dynamics of individual cells within the embryo.

Our results provide evidence for a transition from simple to complex multicellular development in the green algal lineage, which is likely to be an important step in the evolution of multicellularity.
Celiac disease ( CelD) is an autoimmune disorder of the small intestine caused by gluten consumption.
Celiac disease ( CD) is an autoimmune disorder of the small intestine caused by gluten consumption in genetically predisposed individuals.
We found that these two diseases are likely to be caused by similar genetic factors, but that they have different environmental triggers.
We found that the genetic variants associated with both diseases are located within or near genes involved in immune function.
We performed a meta-analysis of genome-wide association studies ( GWASs) of Crohn's disease ( CD) and celiac disease ( CelD).
We found that these variants are associated with both diseases but have different effects on risk of each disease.
We identified several new genetic variants associated with CD and/or Crohn's disease ( CD), including one variant at the IL23R locus, which was previously known to be associated with CD but not UC.
We found that there are at least four genetic variants in common between Crohn's disease and celiac disease.

We have identified four genes that are required for EEEV virulence, and we show that these genes are not essential for virus replication in vitro but are necessary for efficient infection of mice.

This study provides an insight to the spatial distribution of dengue fever in the city of Davao and may be used as a reference for other cities with similar climate conditions.

However, there are no computational tools available to systematically integrate these data and identify the underlying mechanisms.
We have developed an algorithm, IMPACT ( Integrated Multi-phenotype Analysis for Complex Traits), which identifies the most consistent set of genes across multiple phenotypes and conditions.
We use the information about gene interactions to filter out phenotypes that are unlikely to be associated with the disease.
We show that this method improves the performance of genome-wide RNA interference screens in Drosophila cells by increasing the number of detectable phenotypes and decreasing false positives.
We show how IMPACT can be used to select high-confidence gene sets from large-scale genetic interaction data and to identify novel disease genes in human patients with rare diseases.

The diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder ( ASD) is based on clinical observation, which is highly variable in its reliability and validity.

We found that autistic individuals were less able to use this information than typical adults, especially in situations where they had to consider others' incentives.
This study provides a framework to better understand the complex nature of autism, which may help guide diagnosis and treatment.
Proteins are the workhorses of all living cells, and they interact with each other to perform most cellular functions.

Radiotherapy versus chemotherapy for treating primary central nervous system lymphoma Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether radiotherapy or chemotherapy is better at improving survival and reducing symptoms in people with primary central nervous system lymphoma (PCNSL).
The role of chemotherapy is still unclear, although it may be beneficial for patients with high-risk disease or those who have relapsed after initial treatment.
Study characteristics The evidence is current to February 2014.
One study compared methotrexate and whole brain radiotherapy to methotrexate alone.
The study was conducted in 11 centres across the UK between 2010 and 2012.
We found no evidence that adding radiotherapy to chemotherapy improved overall survival, but this result is uncertain.

The results of this study showed that adding whole brain radiotherapy to surgery and chemotherapy did improve survival compared to surgery and chemotherapy alone for people with high risk of recurrence.
Neurotoxicity was reported in 49% of patients who received both chemotherapy and radiotherapy compared with 26% of those who had chemotherapy alone.
Valosin-containing protein ( VCP) is an essential protein involved in many cellular functions including protein degradation, organelle biogenesis, and cell division.

The chromosome organization of bacteria is highly conserved, even in distantly related species.
Here we show that the E. coli genome is organized into large-scale domains ( macrodomains) that are replicated simultaneously during cell division.
We show that changes in the composition of these regions and their replication timing do not affect cell physiology to a great extent.
We show that the balance between these two replication arms is critical to ensure proper segregation of chromosomes in mitosis.
We found that the Ter region is required for proper chromosome segregation and that its disruption causes chromosome missegregation in yeast cells.
We discuss how this mechanism could be used to regulate the cell cycle in bacteria, and how it might have contributed to the evolution of bacterial chromosomes with multiple replicons.
The origin of microtubules is unknown because they are absent from all known bacterial genomes except for those of cyanobacteria.
Here, using cryo-EM, we show that two bacterial proteins, BtubA and BtubB, form microtubule-like structures.
The bMTs have only five protofilaments and lack tubulin posttranslational modifications, which are important for microtubule stability and function.
We show that these proteins are not related to modern tubulins but instead likely evolved from ancient tubulin-like proteins that were involved in forming short microtubules.

We show here that the brain represents objects as a combination of their shape and texture, and that these two features are represented separately in different parts of the visual cortex.

We found that S. strix is able to obtain essential nutrients from its bacterial symbiont, but also has the ability to acquire additional nutrients by consuming other bacteria.
We found that the bacteria lack the enzyme enolase, which is required for glycolysis, and instead use an alternative metabolic pathway to produce pyruvate from glucose.

The ability to remember past events is essential for our daily lives, but we do not know how the brain distinguishes between memories of past versus future events.
We show that optogenetic stimulation of dopamine neurons in the hippocampus impairs spatial memory by disrupting the normal pattern of electrical activity between neurons in the hippocampus.
Our findings provide insight into how the brain may be able to store memories in a way that is resistant to interference from new information.
The anthrax toxin is composed of three components: PA, LF, and EF.
The anthrax toxin is composed of three proteins, lethal factor ( LF) and two components of the protective antigen ( PA), which are secreted by the bacteria.
LRP6 is an important component of Wnt signaling pathway, which plays a key role in cell proliferation, differentiation, and survival.
Cannabis use in pregnancy and breastfeeding Cannabis is the most widely used illegal drug worldwide, with an estimated 200 million users.
We found that there is some evidence to support the use of psychological treatments for people with alcohol misuse problems in general practice.
We reviewed all studies that evaluated the effects of psychological treatments to help people stop using cannabis.
We found no evidence to support or refute the use of CBT for cannabis dependence in people with psychosis.
We also found that there was no evidence to support the use of longer courses of CBT or MI, but more research is needed to find out if these are effective.
Salmonella infection of humans and mice results in a strong Th1 response with CD4+ T cells being central to host defense.

We found that the protective immunity conferred by live-attenuated S. Typhimurium vaccine was mediated by a population of memory CD4+ T cells that were activated in response to infection and produced IFN-g.
We found that liver-resident memory CD4+ T cells are generated from naive CD4+ T cells that migrate to the liver, where they undergo clonal expansion and acquire a memory phenotype.

We found that Salmonella infection induces a rapid and transient expansion of Salmonella-specific CD4+ T cells in the spleen, but these cells are rapidly lost from the spleen and migrate to peripheral tissues where they persist for months.

The genetic mechanisms underlying the development of sensory neurons and their associated behaviors have been studied extensively in model organisms such as Drosophila melanogaster ( fruit fly) and Caenorhabditis elegans ( roundworm).
We show that the transcription factor Lef1 is essential for the development of anxiolytic neurons in both zebrafish and mice.
We also found that the Lef1-dependent genes are conserved between zebrafish and Drosophila, but not mice, suggesting that this pathway is evolutionarily conserved but has been lost in mammals.
We also found that orthologs of these genes are expressed in the hypothalamus of marmoset monkeys and humans, suggesting that they may be involved in anxiety regulation in primates.
Our results suggest that Lef1 may be an important target for developing novel treatments for anxiety disorders.

The Sonic hedgehog ( Shh) pathway is essential for the formation and maintenance of the anterior-posterior ( AP) pattern in developing limbs, but how it regulates gene expression in this process remains unclear.
We found that Srg3 is required for proper patterning of the developing kidney and that its absence results in abnormal development of the ureteric bud, which gives rise to the collecting ducts.
Louse-borne relapsing fever ( LBRF) is caused by Borrelia recurrentis and transmitted by body lice.
In Europe, the disease has been reported in several countries, but only few cases have been reported in the last years.
We have developed a method to grow B. recurrentis in vitro, which has allowed us to study the genetic diversity of this bacterium.
We used Illumina sequencing to generate whole genome sequences of two strains of this species, which we then compared with other related bacteria.
We found that the isolates obtained in 2015 were genetically different from those isolated previously, despite being phenotypically similar.
Herpesviruses are large DNA viruses that replicate in the host cell nucleus and exit the nucleus via budding from the inner side of the nuclear envelope.
We show that pUL31 binds to the viral capsid protein, which is essential for virus assembly, and escorts it to the inner nuclear envelope where it mediates the formation of infectious virions.
The COVID-19 pandemic has caused significant economic losses, especially for small businesses and low-income households.
We found that the probability of exporting at least one infected person per day was greater than 95% in early January 2020.
The results showed that the travel restriction had an immediate effect on reducing the number of cases exported from the city to other areas in China.

The review found that there was not enough information to be sure whether or not blood pressure lowering drugs are beneficial for people aged under 60 years, although there were suggestions that they may help reduce heart disease risk in this group.
We found no evidence of an effect of blood pressure lowering drugs on death from any cause in people aged between 18 and 59 years with high blood pressure.
The treatments were bendroflumethiazide ( Bendrofluazide) and propranolol, which are commonly prescribed drugs for high blood pressure.

Key results The review of trials found that blood pressure lowering drugs had little or no effect on death due to any cause (low quality evidence), heart attack, stroke, and heart failure, but did reduce the risk of having another heart attack (moderate quality evidence) and the need for a heart bypass operation (high quality evidence).
The review found low quality evidence that drug therapy reduces the risk of death or stroke by about one-fifth over five years but does not have any effect on heart failure or other adverse events.
Quality of the evidence The quality of the evidence was very low for all outcomes.
The review found no studies that looked at the effects of stopping treatment because of side effects, but there was some information from other sources.
We found that T. cruzi RPA is able to bind to single-stranded DNA and RNA, but it has an increased capacity for binding to double-stranded DNA when compared to its human counterpart.
We found that RPA-2 is required for efficient DNA replication and cell division in C. elegans embryos.
We found that TcTat1 is important for parasite survival and infectivity of Trypanosoma cruzi.
These results provide new insights on the role of RPA during parasite development and may help to identify potential targets for drug intervention.
The human immunodeficiency virus ( HIV) is a highly adaptable pathogen, capable of evading the host immune system through rapid mutation in its envelope protein gp120.
The virus has evolved to evade recognition by the immune system through mutations in its proteins that bind to HLA molecules on the surface of infected cells.

We have used a novel technique called next generation sequencing ( NGS) to characterise the genetic diversity of HIV within each pair and compare it with the genetic diversity of the virus found in the mother's blood sample.
The ability of HIV-1 to adapt to its host has been well documented, but the extent of this adaptation in vivo remains unclear.
We show that this form of plasticity is not only essential for normal brain function but also plays an important role in recovery from stroke and other forms of neurological injury.
However, the molecular and cellular mechanisms that regulate this process remain unclear.
We show that the recovery from deprivation is achieved by the formation of new connections between neurons located both inside and outside the lesioned area.

We find that this mechanism is sufficient to explain the observed changes in firing rate and spike timing, and we show that it also contributes to maintaining the activity level of individual neurons.
We show that this model is able to restore normal activity in the network after deafferentation and that it is robust to changes in the number of neurons and synapses.
The p53 protein is one of the most important tumour suppressors in human cancer.

We also show that a frameshift mutation in Gata3 leads to aberrant expression of genes involved in cell proliferation and DNA repair, suggesting that this mutation may contribute to tumorigenesis by altering gene expression patterns.
These results have implications for understanding how mutations in GATA3 contribute to breast cancer development and progression.
Wildfires are an important component of many terrestrial ecosystems, but their role in the global carbon cycle is not well understood.
Wildfires are an important component of many ecosystems but also cause significant damage to human life, property, and the environment.
We show that the temperature of fine-scale fuels is controlled by convection, not radiative heating, during wildfires.
The oceans are home to an enormous number of microorganisms, which play key roles in global nutrient cycling and carbon sequestration.
We use a combination of theory and modeling to show how the interplay between physical and biological timescales affects the response of phytoplankton populations to changes in their environment.

We found that populations with more short-term transgenerational effects were better able to respond to changing environments in the short term but had slower long-term adaptation rates, and vice versa.

The ability to predict how long it will take for a population to evolve under different environmental conditions is essential for understanding the response of microbes to changing environments, such as those found in the human body or in the environment.
The ability of plants to sense and respond to environmental cues is essential for their survival in nature.
The resurrection plant Boea hygrometrica is able to survive extreme desiccation by acquiring rapid desiccation tolerance ( RDT).
We found that drought acclimation-induced resistance to drought tolerance ( RDT) is maintained for at least four weeks in Arabidopsis thaliana.

We found that drought stress induces hypomethylation in all three sequence contexts ( CG, CHG, and CHH) in the B. hygrometrica genome, whereas acclimation to drought leads to increased methylation in the CHH context.
Our results suggest that epigenetic regulation plays an important role in plant adaptation to environmental stresses such as drought.

The germinant receptor for bile acids is well characterized; however, the co-germinant receptor remains unknown.
We have identified a new germinant receptor in Bacillus subtilis and show that it is required for growth on certain amino acids.
We found that some mutants of Bacillus subtilis are able to germinate in response to a single amino acid, tryptophan ( TA), but not to other amino acids or sugars.
We found that two of these proteins, CspA and CspC, are required for spore germination in vitro and in vivo.
Triatomine bugs are vectors of Trypanosoma cruzi, the etiological agent of American trypanosomiasis ( Chagas disease), in Latin America.
The olfactory system is one of the most important sensory systems in insects and plays an essential role in their survival.
Triatomine bugs are vectors of Chagas disease and their ability to detect host odors is essential for their survival.

We used scanning electron microscopy to examine the structure of the antennae, and then recorded the electrical activity in response to different stimuli from two closely related species of triatomine bugs.

We found that these two species have different olfactory systems, with distinct numbers of sensilla on each antennal segment and different expression patterns of odorant receptors ( ORs) across segments.

Rapid sequence induction of anaesthesia for tracheal intubation Review question We reviewed the evidence about the best way to perform a rapid sequence induction of anaesthesia for tracheal intubation in adults and children.
Succinylcholine is the most commonly used drug to induce paralysis in children but it can cause cardiac arrhythmias (abnormal heart rhythm) which may be life-threatening.
The aim of this review was to find out if there are any benefits or harms from using these drugs for people with schizophrenia.
Study characteristics We searched the literature up to 14 February 2015 and found 11 studies with 1000 participants.

The review of trials found that succinylcholine is better than rocuronium at providing good intubation conditions, but has more side effects.

We used a combination of 2D and 3D imaging to examine individuals with trachoma in Ethiopia.

We compiled and analyzed six coral reef food webs from different bioregions to test whether reefs are structured similarly or differently compared with other ecosystems.
Coral reefs are among the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth and provide important ecosystem services for millions of people worldwide.

This study shows how conciliatory gestures can be used to repair relationships after conflict, but also highlights the importance of considering the victim's perspective when designing interventions aimed at promoting forgiveness.

We show that this mechanism is robust to mutations and allows for the coexistence of multiple species with different genome sizes.

The process of stem cell differentiation is thought to be governed by a network of interacting genes, and it has been proposed that this network forms a landscape with multiple stable states ( or attractors) corresponding to different cell types.
We show how to construct the epistatic network from the gene expression data and use it to predict the effects of mutations on gene expression levels.

We show how our method can explain the heterogeneity observed in the expression levels of the transcription factor Nanog in mouse embryonic stem cells, as well as the role of Nanog in maintaining the balance between self-renewal and differentiation.
Our results show that the switching rate between two stable states depends on the strength and speed of the interaction with the DNA, as well as the number of molecules bound to the DNA.

These results suggest that PAI-1 is an important modulator of vascular function and may be a potential therapeutic target for diseases associated with decreased nitric oxide bioavailability.

In this study we show that mice lacking PMEL have normal pigmentation but are defective in melanoma formation.
We have generated mice with a mutation in the Pmel gene and found that these mice are unable to produce melanin.
We show that Pmel is required for the formation of melanin pigment in the skin and hair but not in the eyes or brain.
We show that this is due to a defect in melanosome maturation and transport within keratinocytes.

We identified a long non-coding RNA named PAHAL that regulates dopamine biosynthesis and locomotion in locusts.

The non-primate hepacivirus ( NPHV) genus includes several viruses that infect rodents and bats but not humans.
We found that NPHV infection is persistent, with virus detected by PCR for up to 10 years post-infection.
We found that NPHV is able to cause persistent infection in mice, which resembles the natural history of HCV infection in humans.

The World Health Organization recommends annual deworming programs to reduce morbidity and mortality from intestinal worms in children, but there is little evidence on how these programs affect the prevalence of other parasites.
We found that, in addition to the standard measures of drug efficacy and coverage, we need to consider the impact of mass treatment on the prevalence of infection with other parasites.
In this study, we surveyed parents in rural Tanzania to find out how many children had received deworming medication in the previous year and where they got it from.
We found that over half of the children had been given deworming drugs at some point during the year, but only one-third had received deworming within the past six months.

We found that both individual-level treatment and unprogrammed deworming are likely to be effective at reducing worm burdens, but that they may also lead to an overestimation of programmatic coverage.
We developed and tested a new tool for assessing unprogrammed deworming in schools.
Single nucleotide polymorphisms ( SNPs), which occur at every 100-200 base pairs in the human genome, have been widely used to map disease genes and study gene expression regulation.
We show that these changes are mediated by altered tRNA abundance, providing direct evidence for the central role of tRNAs as key regulators of gene expression.
Prostate cancer ( PCa) is one of the most common cancers among men, and it is the second leading cause of cancer-related deaths in men worldwide.
The development of new therapeutic strategies for prostate cancer ( PCa) is hampered by its clinical and molecular heterogeneity, as well as the lack of appropriate preclinical models.
Our study provides new insights into the role of genetic alterations in prostate cancer progression and highlights the potential of using organoid models to identify novel therapeutic targets.
Phosphorylation is one of the most common posttranslational modifications in eukaryotes and plays a key role in regulating protein function.
We identified two phosphorylation sites in PtrAldOMT2, which are important for its enzymatic function.

Innate immunity is the first line of defense against invading pathogens.
The immune system is capable of mounting an effective response against invading pathogens and foreign substances through activation of innate immunity.

The innate immune system is capable of sensing microbial infection through pattern recognition receptors ( PRRs) that recognize conserved molecular patterns on microbes, such as lipopolysaccharide ( LPS).
The mechanism of this process is unknown and has been the subject of intense research for decades.

Our results provide new insights into the mechanisms underlying endotoxin tolerance and may help to develop novel therapeutic strategies against sepsis.

Omega-3 fatty acids for preventing vascular access failure in people with end-stage renal disease Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether or not taking oral supplements containing omega-3 fatty acids could help to keep dialysis access open in people with kidney failure who are on haemodialysis.
Study characteristics We searched the literature up to January 2016, and found one randomised controlled trial (RCT) with 30 participants comparing omega-3 fatty acids with placebo.

Study characteristics We found two randomised controlled trials, with a total of 1000 participants comparing omega-3 fatty acid supplementation to no treatment or another type of supplement for preventing blockage of dialysis access points.
We found no studies that compared the effectiveness of different types of exercise programmes for people with schizophrenia.
We found no evidence to support the use of antiplatelet drugs in people with peripheral artery disease who are undergoing surgery on their leg or foot.
The review found that there is insufficient evidence to support the use of these drugs in people with cirrhosis.
The review authors found 11 studies, which included 1, 111 participants.
The four studies in this review included patients who had an arteriovenous graft, which is a tube made of synthetic material placed between an artery and vein.

Quality of evidence The quality of evidence was low or very low for all outcomes, as there were only small numbers of participants in the included studies, and the results were not consistent across studies.
Key results Omega-3 fatty acids did not make any difference in terms of the number of blockages, deaths or hospital admissions over one year.

We found that for people who have a fistula, we are moderately confident that omega-3 fatty acids do not prevent blockage of the fistula but we are very uncertain about whether they prevent blockage of grafts.
Our results suggest that the stability of proteins is not only determined by their sequence but also by the way they are folded, i.e., the conformational space accessible to them.
We find that the stability of proteins is determined by the balance between hydrophobic burial and hydrogen bonding, with additional contributions from entropy in the folded state.
Our results provide insight into how proteins fold and how they are stabilized by their environment.
We present a method to rapidly generate kinetic models from experimental data by combining a curated metabolic network with information about the distribution of carbon atoms in metabolites.
We have used this method to measure fluxes in seven single gene deletion mutants of E. coli.

We present an approach, called K-FIT ( kinetic flux balance-based identification of target interventions), which combines metabolic modeling with machine learning to identify optimal combinations of gene knockouts or overexpressions that maximize the desired phenotype.
We show that the ability to predict product yields is highly dependent on the strain used for training, and that the inclusion of additional metabolic reactions improves predictive performance.
We demonstrate how to use this method for evaluating the quality of fluxomic data and the robustness of metabolic models.

Riboswitches are RNA elements that sense cellular metabolites and control gene expression by binding to them.
We find that these biases are consistent with the hypothesis that riboswitches evolved from ancient regulatory elements of the RNA world.
We have discovered a new class of riboswitches that bind ppGpp, a small-molecule second messenger involved in stress response in bacteria.
Our results suggest that ancient RNA-based regulatory systems are still used today to control important cellular functions, and that these systems may be more common than previously thought.
The ability to fly is an extraordinary feat of engineering in which insects use their wings to generate lift and thrust while simultaneously sensing the environment and controlling their movements.
In Diptera, the haltere sensory organs provide rapid feedback to the wing motor system and are thought to be essential for flight control.
We show that the thorax is capable of generating passive coupling between wings and halteres through a mechanical linkage that connects the two wings to each other.
Here, we show that the fly wing hinge is capable of independent movement and that this ability is essential for flight.
The mechanism is based on a novel type of gear system that allows for independent control of each wing.
The wingbeat frequency of Drosophila melanogaster is determined by the interaction between the wing and the thorax, which is controlled by the nervous system.

Pain perception is essential for survival as it allows animals to avoid harmful situations.
We have used a genetic approach in the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster to identify genes involved in heat nociception.
We have previously shown that genetic ablation of PIP5Ka or PI3Kg in nociceptors results in reduced sensitivity to heat pain.
This study provides a comprehensive map of the molecular pathways involved in pain sensation, which will be useful to identify new targets for pain relief.

Mosquito-borne diseases such as dengue, chikungunya, yellow fever, malaria, and Zika virus infections have become major public health problems in tropical and subtropical regions of the world.
The use of Wolbachia bacteria to control dengue virus transmission is currently being tested in several countries around the world.
We found that Wolbachia infection had no effect on the probing behavior of infected mosquitoes.
We found that Wolbachia-infected mosquitoes were less likely to probe than uninfected mosquitoes, but this effect diminished with age.
This study provides evidence of an effect of Wolbachia infection on mosquito blood feeding behavior, which could potentially impact transmission of dengue virus by Aedes aegypti.
Mother-to-child transmission of HIV is a major cause of pediatric AIDS cases worldwide.

We found that HIV-1 infects cells in the rectum within four hours of exposure.
We also observed that the virus was present in the lower parts of the GI tract, but not in the upper parts.

We found that clear information about the risks and benefits of vaccines is more effective at increasing vaccination intentions than vague or reassuring messages.

We found that the loss of dendrites was associated with reduced functional connectivity between cortical regions and impaired sensory processing.
Circulating tumor cells ( CTCs), which are shed from primary tumors, have been implicated in cancer metastasis.
We developed a method for quantifying CTC secretion in a high-throughput manner, which we applied to a panel of human cancer cell lines.
Here we describe a microfluidic device that rapidly and efficiently isolates circulating tumor cells ( CTCs) from blood samples with high purity and concentration.

We found that circulating tumor cells ( CTCs) from patients with metastatic breast cancer have higher levels of matrix metalloproteinase-9 ( MMP-9) than nonmetastatic patients, suggesting that MMP-9 could serve as a prognostic marker for metastasis.
The prion protein ( PrP) is the only known cellular receptor for scrapie and BSE prions.

Type III secretion systems are protein secretion machineries found in many pathogenic bacteria, which allow them to inject virulence factors directly into host cells.
We have used a combination of biochemical and biophysical approaches to study the molecular details of this process.
We show that this is due to the inability of the protein to unfold in the narrow channel of the secretion apparatus.
We show that these channels are able to transport proteins across membranes in both directions, but that they do so by transporting them through the membrane with their N-termini leading the way.
We show that the channel is able to shield the fusion protein from proteolytic degradation, thereby increasing its half-life in cells.
This study provides a solid foundation for further structural studies on the T3SS, which will be essential to understand its function.
We propose that this mechanism is likely to be conserved in other bacterial secretion systems, and may have implications for the design of novel antibiotics.
The spatiotemporal scan statistic is used to identify the high-risk areas and time points for dengue fever in Guangzhou, China.
The spatial distribution of dengue fever cases in Guangzhou was mainly concentrated in the southwestern area, which is consistent with previous studies.

Our results suggest that environmental and socio-economic factors are likely to be associated with dengue fever incidence, which should be considered when developing effective control strategies for this disease.
The presence of non-retroviral RNA viruses ( NRVs) in vertebrate genomes has been reported for decades; however, their origin is unknown.

We found that the closest relatives of RnPV2 are not viruses but rather partitiviruses from plants.
We found that many plant genomes contain large numbers of non-coding RNAs with viral origin, which may be used as markers for resolving unclear phylogeny of plants.
We show that PER is required for the rhythmic binding of CLOCK-BMAL1 to E-boxes in vivo, and that this interaction is necessary for the repression of target genes.
The mechanism of how this repression is achieved has been unclear.
We found that CWO binds to E-boxes upstream of core clock genes in a rhythmic manner, which promotes the recruitment of PER to E-boxes and maintains repression until PER is degraded.

We propose that CWO acts as a repressor of the core clock gene expression by competing with the core clock activator complex ( CLK-CYC) for DNA binding sites, thereby preventing premature activation of target genes.
We propose that this mechanism is conserved in mammals, where it could be important for regulating circadian gene expression.
The innate immune system is the first line of defense against invading pathogens, including viruses.
The type I interferon ( IFN) response is one of the first lines of defense against viral infections.

The inflammasome is an intracellular multiprotein complex that senses microbial infection and activates inflammatory responses by cleaving pro-caspase-1, which in turn cleaves pro-interleukin-1b and pro-IL-18 to generate mature forms of these cytokines.

Our results suggest that the innate immune system has evolved to utilize multiple mechanisms to sense viral infections, and that these mechanisms are not redundant but rather complementary in their ability to induce protective responses against viral pathogens.
The insulin-like growth factor receptor DAF-2 activates the transcription factor DAF-16, which promotes longevity and stress resistance in response to nutrient deprivation.

We found that the insulin/IGF-1 receptor-like kinase DAF-2, which is required for longevity, interacts with the TGF-b/bone morphogenetic protein ( BMP) signaling pathway to regulate germ cell proliferation and migration.
We found that DAF-16 regulates the expression of genes involved in the mTORC1 signaling pathway by interacting with two SMAD proteins ( SMA-2 and SMA-3) in the nucleus.
Our results suggest that DAF-16 has two distinct functions in C. elegans development, one promoting proliferation in somatic cells and another repressing proliferation in germ cells.
We have used a genetic approach to show that Pold is required for efficient replication of the fission yeast genome and that it acts on both leading and lagging strands.
We show that rNMPs accumulate in the absence of RNase H2 activity, but not when RNase H1 is absent.

We show that this is due to the fact that the RNA polymerases of both strands are active, and that the leading strand polymerase is more efficient at copying the template than the lagging strand polymerase.
Our findings suggest that the functions of Pold and Pole at the eukaryotic replication fork are conserved from yeast to humans.
The evolution of endosymbiosis has been pivotal in shaping the diversity of life on Earth, but the mechanisms underlying this process are not well understood.
The symbiotic association between dinoflagellates and green algae is one of the most widespread examples of marine photosymbiosis, but its evolutionary history remains poorly understood.
We combine high-resolution microscopy, metabolic imaging, and transcriptomics to reveal how host cells control symbiotic bacteria by blocking their division and altering their metabolism.
We show that the increase in plastid size is accompanied by an extensive proliferation of mitochondria and that these two organelles are closely associated.

This study provides evidence for an ancient and highly specialized photosynthetic symbiosis between a fungus and its cyanobacterial endosymbiont.
The process of X chromosome inactivation is essential for dosage compensation and occurs early during embryogenesis.
We show that this is due to an increase in the number of active genes on the single X chromosome during early stages of differentiation.
We find that X chromosome inactivation is not uniform across all genes; instead, it occurs at different times for different genes.
We found that many genes were expressed only in differentiated cells and not in stem cells, but some genes were expressed in both cell types.
The aim of this study was to evaluate the efficacy and safety of mesalazine (mesalamine) in the treatment of ulcerative colitis.
We found five randomised controlled trials with a total of 1057 participants comparing probiotics to placebo or mesalazine (a drug used for ulcerative colitis treatment).
Quality of evidence The quality of the evidence was low or very low for most outcomes.
There was no clear evidence showing whether or not probiotics reduced the rate of recurrence of symptoms after treatment (very-low certainty).

There was no significant difference between probiotics and mesalazine for preventing relapse (low-certainty evidence), maintaining remission (low-certainty evidence), improving quality of life (moderate-certainty evidence) or reducing side effects (low-certainty evidence).
The addition of probiotics to 5-aminosalicylic acid did not result in any significant improvement in relapse rates.
The review found that most of the studies did not report any side effects from taking probiotics and only one study reported an increase in side effects for the probiotic group.
Probiotics are live micro-organisms that have been shown to be effective at treating some gastrointestinal diseases, but their use for maintaining remission in ulcerative colitis is not clear.
The quality of the evidence was low or very low for all outcomes, as the included studies were poorly designed and had few participants.
We found no evidence to support or refute the use of any particular type of medication for preventing falls in people with dementia, and more research is needed.

Single-cell sequencing has become an important tool for studying cell heterogeneity, but accurate variant calling is still a major challenge due to low coverage and high error rates.
We have developed a method to isolate individual chromosomes from a single cell, amplify them using rolling circle replication, and construct barcoded libraries for high-throughput sequencing.
Single-cell genomics has been used in many studies, but it is still challenging to obtain high-quality genomes from individual cells due to the low amount of DNA available.

Autophagy is an evolutionarily conserved cellular process that degrades intracellular pathogens to prevent their replication and dissemination in host cells.
In this study, we found that autophagy is induced in skeletal muscle cells during Toxoplasma gondii and Trichinella spiralis infections.
We found that T. spiralis infection induces autophagy in infected muscle cells, and this process is required for parasite survival within the host.
Ultrasound is widely used in medicine for imaging and therapy of soft tissues but has not been applied to manipulate solid objects such as bones or implants.

The HIV-1 Gag protein is synthesized as a large precursor polypeptide that undergoes multiple proteolytic cleavage events to form the mature viral particles.
The mechanism of how these proteins assemble into virus particles is not well understood.
We used electron microscopy to study the structure of HIV-1 particles with mutations at cleavage sites within the viral capsid protein ( Gag).
We found that cleavage at either end of the major coat protein ( CA) is sufficient to form immature particles but not mature virions.
Our results suggest that the six-helix bundle is the major structural element in the assembly and maturation process of the HIV-1 envelope glycoprotein trimer.

We found that the genome of this bacterium contains a large number of RIPs and that they are differentially expressed during infection.

In this study we present the first large-scale screening of wild-caught rodents and bats in Africa.
We found that the majority of these infections were in the South African four-striped mouse, Rhabdomys pumilio.

Acetaminophen versus non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for acute postoperative pain in adults Acetaminophen is one of the most widely used analgesics worldwide, but its effectiveness compared to other commonly prescribed medications has not been well studied.
We found no evidence that one or the other was safer, but there were too few participants in our study to be sure about this.
We found that there was no evidence to support or refute the use of any particular type of analgesic medication for pain relief in people with cancer.
We found that the intervention resulted in a small improvement in QoL, but this result is imprecise and may not be meaningful for individuals.
The yellow fever ( YF) vaccine is one of the oldest vaccines used to prevent infectious diseases, and it has been produced for more than 80 years using embryonated chicken eggs as cell factories.
However, there are no studies on the pathogenesis of YFV in mice.
We found that the virus infects many different cell types including skeletal muscle cells, heart muscle cells, nerve cells, kidney cells, lung cells, and skin cells.
Our results provide a better understanding of the pathogenesis of yellow fever virus and will help to develop a more effective vaccine against this disease.
Cyclotides are a family of ribosomally synthesized and posttranslationally modified peptides with diverse biological activities.
We have identified and characterized a family of cysteine proteases, called CysCys, which are responsible for the initial processing of cyclotides.

This study provides insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying cyclotide biosynthesis and lays the foundation for engineering cyclotide biosynthetic pathways in plants to produce novel cyclotides with improved properties.

We found that IFN-l treatment increased expression of PDCD4, a protein that promotes inflammation and is downregulated by miR-21.

We found that IFN-I signaling in macrophages is required for optimal inflammatory responses to bacterial infection and that this response is mediated through inhibition of microRNA-21 ( miR-21) -mediated down-regulation of programmed cell death protein 4 ( PDCD4).
Branching morphogenesis is a fundamental biological process in which epithelial cells branch out to form tubular structures such as blood vessels and airways.
The ECM is composed of proteins, glycoproteins, proteoglycans, lipids, and other macromolecules secreted by cells to form an extracellular network.
We found that loss of heparan sulfate synthesis results in an increase in branching tip formation but a decrease in total branch number.
Our results suggest that the loss of Ext1 function may be responsible for the abnormal lung development observed in patients with this disease.

We found that the loss of one copy of the gene encoding the transcription factor PAX2 results in increased levels of the growth factor FGF10 and decreased levels of the Hedgehog ( Hh) signaling pathway.
Our findings highlight the importance of heparan sulfate in regulating Shh signaling and lung development.
The mechanisms underlying memory formation are not well understood.

Here we show how such a mechanism could be implemented in a biologically plausible way by combining a novel theory of network remodeling with numerical simulations of a model brain area ( the primary visual cortex).
The brain is thought to store memories in neuronal networks formed by the strengthening of synaptic connections between neurons.

The World Health Organization recommends artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs) as first-line treatment for uncomplicated malaria in Africa, but there is little evidence on their relative effectiveness and safety.
In this review we compared the efficacy of two different treatments against P. vivax malaria in Africa: dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHA-P) and artemether lumefantrine (AL).
The results showed that DHA-P had a longer protective effect against new infections and reduced the risk of malaria by 29%.
The review found that DHA supplementation may cause an increase in heart rate and prolongation of the time between heart beats, but this did not lead to any serious adverse events.
The use of artificial electrical stimulation of peripheral nerves has been proposed as a means to restore sensation in amputees.
We developed a computational model of a peripheral nerve trunk to study how electrical activity propagates through the nerve tissue.

We show how these effects are related to the underlying biophysical properties of neurons, such as their membrane resistance and capacitance, and we use this understanding to predict the effect of different types of electrical stimulation on the spread of activity in the brain.
Prion diseases are caused by misfolded protein aggregates, which propagate and spread through cells and tissues in an autocatalytic manner.
The yeast prion Sup35 is an example of such a protein, which forms amyloid fibrils in vivo and causes disease when overexpressed.
We show that the protein quality control system in yeast is able to prevent this aggregation by continuously breaking down the aggregates into smaller pieces.
Here we show that the overexpression of a protein called Pin4, which is known to cause the formation of large aggregates, leads to the curing of [PSI+].
We found that the presence of heterologous Q/N-rich proteins in yeast cells impairs the propagation of the [PSI+] prion.

Our method is robust to noise in the input data and has been validated on simulated data as well as real data from two species of yeast.

We show that our method is able to identify genes with high probability of being involved in cancer, and we demonstrate its ability to predict the presence of copy number variations ( CNVs) in cancer cells.
We show that the choice of method depends on the type of data available and the question being asked, and we provide guidelines to help researchers choose the appropriate method for their study.

Extracellular vesicles ( EVs) have been found to be involved in intercellular communication, but the mechanisms by which they are produced and released remain unclear.
Currently, the most commonly used method to isolate EVs from parasites is differential centrifugation.
We have developed an efficient method for isolating EVs from F. hepatica using size exclusion chromatography ( SEC) and density gradient centrifugation ( DC).

We found that the use of size exclusion chromatography resulted in a higher EV purity, with less contamination from free proteins such as cathepsins, which are known to be present in the parasite's secretions.
We found that different EV isolation methods yield distinct populations of EVs from dendritic cells ( DC) and serum exosomes ( SEC), which may have implications for their use as vaccine vectors.

Mechanical bowel preparation for colon cancer surgery Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether or not mechanical bowel preparation (MBP) before surgery reduces the risk of complications after surgery in people with colon cancer.
The review authors searched the literature and found five randomised controlled trials, with a total of 1000 patients who had undergone bowel surgery.
We found 11 studies, with a total of 1, 111 participants.
The review authors found 18 studies with 5805 participants who had either received mechanical bowel preparation or not before their planned bowel operation.
There was no clear evidence that any method of bowel preparation improved the success rate for colonoscopy or reduced the risk of complications.

The intracellular redox state, which is determined by the balance between the production and consumption of reactive oxygen species ( ROS), plays an important role in cellular signaling pathways.
The ability to monitor cellular redox states is essential for understanding how cells respond to changes in their environment and for identifying potential disease mechanisms.

The kinetochore is an essential structure for chromosome segregation, and its assembly depends on the organization of centromeric chromatin.
The budding yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae has only one centromere per chromosome arm, and its centromere consists of a short DNA sequence ( 125 bp) with no apparent secondary structure.
The centromere is a specialized chromatin domain that contains the binding sites for the kinetochore proteins and the microtubules that mediate chromosome segregation during cell division.
Here, we describe a method for mapping the composition of chromatin at centromeres using a combination of cross-linking and mass spectrometry.
We show that H3 is localized to centromeres throughout the cell cycle, and that this localization is essential for proper chromosome segregation in mitosis.

Schistosomiasis is one of the most prevalent parasitic diseases in China, and it has been reported to be caused by three species of schistosomes ( S. japonicum, S. mansoni and S. haematobium).

In this study, we evaluated the performance of two different methods used to detect intestinal schistosomiasis in stool samples from children living in an area where Schistosoma mansoni infection is endemic.
In this study, we compared the sensitivity of two methods for detecting Schistosoma japonicum eggs in stool samples collected from patients with suspected schistosomiasis in China.
We conclude that this method does not have much value for the detection of low intensity S. japonicum infections and should be replaced by other diagnostic methods such as PCR or Kato-Katz.
We have generated a mouse model of DCM with cardiac and skeletal muscle involvement by introducing a mutation in Tnni2, encoding troponin I type 2 ( skeletal).
We generated Tnni2 knockout mice with a deletion of exon 5 in the ttn gene encoding titin, which resulted in a severe phenotype characterized by dwarfism and limb abnormalities.
We found that the mutation in Tnni2 is responsible for the small body size of DA2B mice and that this effect is due to an increase in energy expenditure.

We found that the deletion of one copy of the Tnni2 gene resulted in an increase in the expression of hypoxia-inducible factor-1 alpha ( Hif1a) and Hif3a, which are transcription factors that regulate genes involved in angiogenesis.
Our results provide evidence for the role of Tnni2 in skeletal development and reveal a novel molecular pathway involved in this process.
Focused assessment of sonography for trauma ( FAST) is an ultrasound examination used to identify injuries to internal organs such as the spleen, liver, heart and lungs.
The use of ultrasound (US) has been shown to be effective for detecting injuries to the spleen and liver but not as good at detecting other injuries.

Exercise for weight loss in adults with obesity Review question We reviewed the evidence on the effects of exercise on weight loss in adults who are obese (have a body mass index (BMI) of 30 kg/m2 or higher).

We studied the immune response to HIV in a group of patients who were infected with HIV for less than six months, before they started ART.
We found that the ability of B cells to produce antibodies was reduced in the presence of T follicular helper ( Tfh) cells, which are specialized CD4+ T cells that help B cells make antibodies.
We also show that elevated levels of HIV-1 viremia are associated with impaired antibody responses, suggesting that high levels of virus may be detrimental to the development of protective immunity.
We found that the number of activated B cells is reduced during late stages of infection, which may be due to an increase in the number of activated T follicular helper ( Tfh) cells.
Our results suggest that early treatment of HIV infection may be beneficial to preserve the ability of B cells to produce antibodies against pathogens, including influenza virus.

In this study we evaluated the performance of a new molecular tool ( Nested PCR) to detect Schistosoma mansoni infection in human stool samples from endemic areas in Brazil.
The results showed that our assay is highly sensitive, specific and cost-effective for the diagnosis of schistosomiasis in humans and snail hosts.
This study demonstrates the utility of this method for identifying areas with high prevalence of S. mansoni infection, and thus, where control efforts should focus on reducing transmission.

The ability to predict how climate change will affect the distribution and abundance of marine megafauna is critical for effective conservation planning, but this has been difficult due to the lack of data on their migration patterns.
The leatherback sea turtle is one of the most endangered species on Earth, with populations declining at an alarming rate.
We used satellite tracking data to investigate the migration patterns of leatherback sea turtles in the western North Atlantic Ocean.
We tracked female leatherback turtles from the largest known nesting colony in the eastern Pacific Ocean to identify habitat use and migration patterns of this endangered species.
We found that sea turtle migration is not random and that turtles use ocean currents to their advantage by migrating along the most efficient routes.

We found that leatherback turtles migrate long distances across the entire Pacific Ocean, traveling through a consistent route from their nesting grounds in Central America to foraging grounds in the South Pacific Gyre.
We show how ocean circulation patterns shape the migration route of leatherbacks, which migrate from their breeding grounds in the western Atlantic to foraging grounds in the eastern Pacific Ocean.
Our results provide a framework to prioritize areas of high conservation value and highlight the importance of considering the spatial scale at which conservation actions are implemented.

We found that mice lacking acetylcholinesterase ( AChE) activity exhibit reduced aggression in response to stress, whereas mice lacking butyrylcholinesterase ( BChE) activity are hyperaggressive.
These results suggest that BChE may be an important target in the treatment of aggressive behavior.

We found that tetrac has two distinct mechanisms of action, one affecting the initiation and another affecting the progression of the cell cycle.

Many collective phenomena exhibited by animal groups have been shown to emerge from simple rules applied by each individual.
We investigated how well a simple mathematical model could explain the observed behavior of a group of wild mice living in a large enclosure.
We show that these rules are sufficient to reproduce the observed social structure, and we use them to predict how the social structure will change when the environment changes.

Our results show that, despite the complexity of the observed behaviour, it is possible to describe the population dynamics using a simple model based on individual interactions and local information about the environment.
This work provides an example of how simple rules can lead to complex and robust collective phenomena, which are ubiquitous in nature.

We found that treatment with this compound was able to reduce parasite burden in both acute and chronic models of infection.

Our results indicate that the current target product profile for anti-chagasic drugs needs to be revised, and that the focus on achieving a single treatment with a single dose should be replaced by a strategy that allows multiple treatments over the lifetime of the patient.

The cellulosome is an enzyme complex found in anaerobic bacteria that degrades plant biomass by hydrolyzing cellulose and hemicellulose.
The engineered cellulosome is capable of degrading crystalline cellulose efficiently in vitro and in vivo.
We found that the number of cohesins in the cellulosome is important for efficient hydrolysis of crystalline cellulose.
The engineered strain produced 61 g/L of ethanol from lignocellulose hydrolysate, which is the highest reported value to date for a yeast cellulosome system.
Exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals is known to affect human health.
We found that exposure to BPA during pregnancy alters the expression of genes in the placenta that are important for normal development.
We found that BPA and BPS have similar effects on gene expression in human cells, but that they do not affect all genes equally.
We found that BPS has similar effects on the placenta and fetal development as BPA.
The HIV-1 accessory protein Vpu is known to antagonize the host cell's ability to restrict virus spread.
We found that HIV-1 Vpu sequences from infected individuals are highly diverse, but that this diversity is not random; instead, it is driven by immune selection.
We show that Vpu is required for efficient viral replication in macrophages and that it maintains its function to target CD4 and counteract tetherin during chronic infection.
We found that only a small fraction of HIV-1 viruses have mutations in either activity and that these mutations are strongly selected against during chronic infection.
This study provides evidence that Vpu is important for viral persistence in vivo and may be an attractive target for novel anti-HIV therapies.
Male sterility is an important trait for hybrid seed production, but its genetic control has been difficult to achieve in many crop species.
We identified a novel male-sterility gene ZmMs7 from maize and developed a dominant male-sterility system using this gene.
We found that ZmMs7 is required for normal pollen wall formation by regulating the expression of genes involved in pollen wall biosynthesis.
Copy number variations ( CNVs) are one of the most common types of genetic variation in humans and other species.
We find that CNVs are not randomly distributed in the human genome; instead, they tend to cluster together at specific genomic regions.

We found that these genes have lost their olfactory function but retain many of their original features, including high levels of gene expression in the brain and significant conservation of protein sequence.
We conclude that the current view of CNVs as a major source of genetic variation is not supported by our results and that the role of CNVs in human disease needs to be reevaluated.
However, little is known about how biodiversity affects ecosystem functioning in natural terrestrial ecosystems with large spatial heterogeneity and strong disturbance regimes.
We analyzed data from 6, 098 sites in China to examine how climate, soil, and human activities affect ecosystem carbon storage.
These results suggest that maintaining biodiversity is essential to maximize the potential of terrestrial ecosystems to mitigate climate change through carbon sequestration.
Dendritic cells ( DCs) are specialized immune cells that present antigens to T cells, thereby initiating adaptive immunity.

In this study we show that in mice infected with Plasmodium berghei ANKA ( PbANKA) parasites, dendritic cells ( DCs) are unable to induce protective immunity against secondary challenge with unrelated pathogens.
We show here that malaria parasites inhibit dendritic cells ( DCs) from activating CD8+ T cells by blocking their ability to produce IL-12.
We found that this was due to an alteration in the ability of dendritic cells ( DCs) to activate T cells.
The fatty acid synthase of Escherichia coli is composed of three subunits, FabB, FabF, and FabH, which are encoded by genes located on the chromosome.
FabA is an essential enzyme in fatty acid biosynthesis, but its function has been poorly understood due to the lack of structural information.
We use molecular dynamics simulations to show how these two enzymes are biased toward different reactions by their distinct active site geometries.

Trachoma is an infectious eye disease caused by the bacterium Chlamydia trachomatis, which causes blindness in children and adults living in low-income countries.
However, the exact mechanisms by which C. trachomatis causes scarring are unknown.

We found that the majority of children who received the vaccine had no adverse events or only mild side effects.

We found no evidence to support the use of deltamethrin impregnated dog collars for controlling canine rabies in dogs and humans in rural Tanzania.
The results showed that the use of these collars is not effective in controlling the disease and may be considered as an alternative to other methods such as vaccination or insecticide spraying.
Early childhood vocabulary is an important predictor of later literacy skills, but the genetic factors contributing to individual differences in early vocabulary remain unknown.
We investigated the genetic basis of early childhood language development using genome-wide association studies ( GWASs).
We found that there are many genes involved in early language development, and that these genes have different effects on different aspects of language.
We found that one of these genetic factors, which is associated with early language development, continues to influence language ability throughout childhood.

The actin cytoskeleton plays a central role in cell migration, division and shape maintenance of cells.
We have identified a novel mechanism of actin binding by Toxoplasma profilin, which is mediated by an additional structural element not found in other profilins.

Microbes are important for both primary production and particle formation, but their role in the ocean's carbon cycle is poorly understood.
The major histocompatibility complex ( MHC) class I-like molecule, MR1, is expressed on the surface of antigen-presenting cells and presents microbial metabolites to MAIT cells.
In this study we investigated whether MAIT cells are also affected by HIV-1 infection.

We found that MAIT cells are not only important for protection against bacterial infections but also contribute to the control of viral infection by producing cytokines that recruit other immune cells to the site of infection.
We found that MAIT cells from HIV-infected individuals were dysfunctional, failing to produce cytokines or cytotoxic granules when stimulated with bacteria.

We found that this defect was associated with an altered gene expression pattern of transcription factors, including T-bet and Eomesodermin ( Eomes), which are important for the differentiation and effector functions of CD8+ T cells.
We found that IL-7 enhances the ability of MAIT cells to respond to bacterial infection and increases their sensitivity to low levels of bacteria.
We found that MAIT cells are functionally impaired in HIV-1-infected individuals, which may contribute to their reduced ability to control viral replication.

These results suggest that IL-7 is an important regulator of MAIT cell function and may have potential as a therapeutic agent for restoring MAIT cell numbers and function in patients with reduced levels of these cells.

We found that people living in rural areas, and those born outside of Santander department had higher risk of having dengue fever than others.
This study provides evidence for the importance of considering local transmission patterns in designing effective dengue fever control strategies.
Protein dynamics are essential for many biological processes, but their regulation by external stimuli remains poorly understood.
Here we use molecular dynamics ( MD) simulations to study the conformational changes in three different members of the Hsp90 family.
The structure of the Hsp70 protein family is highly conserved across all kingdoms of life.
We have used a novel method to compare the internal dynamics of these two proteins and found that they exhibit similar dynamic properties, despite having different structures.
We also identified a new binding site for Hsp70, which may be a potential target for drugs.
